wildflowers.desc: California state flower. Brilliant orange cups that close at night and in overcast weather. Self-sows vigorously on disturbed ground.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native sweat bees, mining bees, and hover flies. Pollen source only (no nectar).

wildflowers.desc: Coastal scrub sunflower with dark brown center disk. Dominant shrubby wildflower on coastal slopes. Drought-deciduous in summer.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage. Visited by many butterfly species.

wildflowers.desc: Silver-leaved desert shrub with bright yellow flowers. Resinous stems once used as incense by Native Americans. Desert indicator species.

fm.Color: Yellow rays, yellow disk (vs dark disk in E. californica)

wildflowers.hp: Important desert pollinator plant — silver-felted leaves reflect heat. Visited by native bees and Painted Lady butterflies. Foundation species in coastal sage scrub.

wildflowers.desc: Coiled scorpioid cyme of small orange-yellow flowers unfurls like a fiddlehead. Bristly-hairy stems. Abundant on disturbed grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Fiddleneck — coiled flower clusters uncurl as flowers open (scorpioid cyme). Important early-season bee forage. Seeds contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender flowers with distinctive narrow-clawed petals. Woodland edges and openings. Important native pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Elegant clarkia — showy pink-purple petals with distinctive narrow 'claw' bases. Important native bee forage. Self-incompatible — requires pollinators for seed set.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-lavender Clarkia blooming as other spring wildflowers fade. Cup-shaped flowers with darker center blotch.
wildflowers.hp: Farewell-to-spring — pink-lavender flowers marking end of spring wildflower season. Often the last showy wildflower still blooming in June.

wildflowers.desc: Chunky annual lupine with deep blue-purple flower spikes. Succulent hollow stems. Dominant in superbloom years on slopes.

fm.Leaves: Thick, FLESHY, large — leaflets to 3 in (much larger than L. bicolor)
fm.Habitat: Clay soil grasslands, moist areas — the big fleshy lupine
fm.vs: Miniature Lupine (L. bicolor) is tiny (4-12 in), delicate, with small leaflets under 1 in

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee specialist — large flowers require strong bees to trip pollination mechanism. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual lupine — flowers barely half an inch. Blue and white bicolored. Often carpets grasslands in spring.

fm.Habitat: Open grasslands, disturbed areas — the tiny lupine
fm.vs: Arroyo Lupine (L. succulentus) is much larger (1-2 ft) with thick fleshy leaves to 3 in

wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Painted Lady butterfly larvae. Bumble bee pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: Shrubby perennial lupine of coastal scrub. Blue-purple flower spikes. Important early-succession species after fire.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds stimulated by heat/smoke; pioneer after fire

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by native bees. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisy that carpets grasslands in wet springs. Among the most abundant superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: Spring carpet wildflower forming brilliant yellow displays. Important early-season bee forage. Vernal pool indicator in some habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with distinctive white-tipped rays — looks hand-painted. Classic superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: Showy white-tipped yellow ray flowers. Major superbloom species. Important native bee forage during spring peak.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple paintbrush-like annual. Hemiparasitic — draws nutrients from neighboring grass roots. Superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: Purple owl clover — hemiparasitic on neighboring grass roots. Dramatic magenta bracts. Important Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly host.

wildflowers.desc: Perennial paintbrush with scarlet-tipped bracts. Hemiparasitic on nearby plant roots. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral edges.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — perennial root crown survives fire

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds. Host for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Large white trumpet flowers open at dusk, pollinated by hawkmoths. All parts extremely toxic — hallucinogenic alkaloids. Native ceremonial use.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by White-lined Sphinx Moth (hawkmoth). Host for Tobacco Hornworm.

wildflowers.desc: Stunning blue-purple flowers with long curving stamens wrapped in woolly purple fuzz. California endemic. Famous fire follower — mass blooms in chaparral 2–4 years after burns, producing spectacular displays on blackened slopes, then declining as the canopy closes back in. Obligate seeder killed by fire.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds germinate in post-fire soil. Famous fire follower — mass blooms 2–4 years after burns, then declines as chaparral closes

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees — especially bumble bees and large carpenter bees.

wildflowers.desc: Giant white poppy — the largest native flower in California. Crepe-paper petals surround golden stamen boss. Fire follower.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — rhizome resprouter + smoke-germinated seed
fm.Flowers: 5–8 in across — largest native CA flower

wildflowers.hp: Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by seed-harvesting ants. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Orange to salmon tubular flowers on sticky stems. Blooms nearly year-round in coastal areas. Hummingbird-pollinated.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes, road cuts, coastal bluffs

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of tubular scarlet flowers. Premier hummingbird magnet. Post-fire specialist in chaparral.
wildflowers.hp: Specialist hummingbird flower — tube length matches bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-violet tubular flowers in whorled clusters. Narrow leaves. Important garden native. Hummingbird and bee pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee and hummingbird pollinated. Important foothill nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant three-petaled lily of grasslands. White to lilac with dark basal spot on each petal. Catalina Island namesake.

fm.Color: White to lilac, dark basal spots

wildflowers.hp: Catalina mariposa lily — white petals with purple chevron marks. Island origin but now on mainland. Grassland and chaparral openings.

wildflowers.desc: Large yellow three-petaled lily with hairy inner surfaces. Club-shaped hairs on petals. Chaparral and foothill specialist.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — bulb survives fire underground; often more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Yellow mariposa lily — CNPS 4.3. Large bright yellow flowers with dark basal spot. Chaparral openings after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Round heads of tiny blue flowers atop wiry stems. Delicate but abundant. Classic spring wildflower of disturbed grasslands.

fm.Color: Pale blue to lavender spherical heads

wildflowers.hp: Globe gilia — spherical blue flower clusters. Important native bee forage. Widely used in wildflower seed mixes.

wildflowers.desc: Whorled blue flower heads on wiry stems. Seeds were a staple food for indigenous Californians — highly nutritious. Desert and scrub species.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — annual; seeds germinate abundantly in post-fire ash beds

wildflowers.hp: Seeds historically a staple food of Tongva and Chumash peoples — highly nutritious. Pollinated by native bees. Fire follower — seeds germinate in post-fire ash beds.

wildflowers.desc: Iconic silver-white aromatic shrub. Sacred plant in indigenous ceremonies. White flower spikes attract many native bees. Coastal sage scrub indicator.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds germinate post-fire
fm.Cultural: Sacred in indigenous ceremony

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated primarily by native carpenter bees (Xylocopa). Critical nectar source for native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Dark green aromatic sage with pale blue-lavender whorled flowers. Major honey plant — "mellifera" means honey-bearing. Coastal sage scrub dominant.

fm.Color: Pale BLUE-WHITE flower whorls along stem
fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, CSS, chaparral — below 4,000 ft
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; soil seed bank germinates post-fire
fm.Ecology: Major honey source — mellifera = honey-bearing
fm.Leaves: Dark green, wrinkled, strongly aromatic — SMALLER than S. leucophylla
fm.vs: Purple Sage (S. leucophylla) has PINK-PURPLE flowers and GRAY-WHITE woolly leaves

wildflowers.hp: Major honey bee forage. Visited by native bumble bees, carpenter bees, and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Intensely blue bell-shaped flowers. Desert annual that appears in wet years. One of the most vivid blues in the California flora.
wildflowers.hp: Among the best native bee forage plants in desert habitats. Attracts swarms of native bees including mining bees (Andrena), sweat bees (Halictidae), and bee flies (Bombyliidae).

wildflowers.desc: Coiled scorpioid cymes of white to lavender flowers resembling caterpillars. Bristly stems. Common post-fire colonizer.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — abundant colonizer 1–2 years post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Important post-fire bee forage — one of the first annuals to colonize burns, providing nectar when it's most needed. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: White daisy-like flowers on coastal and rocky slopes. Blooms through summer when most other wildflowers are done.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal cliff specialist with milky latex. Important late-season nectar source for native bees on coastal bluffs.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-white phlox flowers on prickly needle-leaved stems. Chaparral openings. Can bloom year-round with sufficient moisture.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — woody base resprouts after fire

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and butterflies. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

wildflowers.desc: Large soft-leaved sunflower of shady canyon bottoms. Distinctively large heart-shaped leaves. California endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Large magenta flower whorls in shady woodland understory. Powerfully fragrant foliage. Premier hummingbird plant.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — powerfully fragrant, attracting native bees as well.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple flower clusters hugging desert sand. Key superbloom species in Antelope Valley. Fragrant in evening.
wildflowers.hp: Desert sand verbena — magenta-pink flower clusters. Mojave Desert. Fragrant at dusk — attracts White-lined Sphinx Moths. Sand stabilizer.

wildflowers.desc: Magenta trumpets opening in afternoon. Sprawling perennial of dry slopes. Hawkmoth-pollinated at dusk.
wildflowers.hp: California four o'clock — flowers open late afternoon. Fragrant — pollinated by hawkmoths at dusk. Tuberous roots.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate purple-blue flowers on weak scrambling stems with tiny hooked prickles. Shady slopes and stream banks in spring.
wildflowers.hp: Fiesta flower — delicate purple bell flowers with sticky auricle-clasping leaves. Canyon understory. Blooms prolifically in shade.

wildflowers.desc: Rounded clusters of blue-purple flowers atop bare stems from underground corm. One of the most common spring wildflowers in CA grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Geophyte — corm produces explosive post-fire flower displays. One of the most common spring wildflowers.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant swept-back petals like a dart in flight. Cyclamen-relative. Moist grassy slopes in spring. Goes dormant in summer.
wildflowers.hp: Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees — requires sonication to release pollen. Cyclamen relative. Summer dormant.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular orange spotted lily to 6 ft. California endemic. Shady canyons. Declining from habitat loss and deer browsing.

fm.Color: Orange with maroon spots, recurved petals

wildflowers.hp: Humboldt lily — the largest native lily. Turk's-cap orange flowers with maroon spots. Shaded woodland streamsides. Bulb eaten by gophers.

wildflowers.desc: Dense clusters of small golden flower heads. Woolly-leaved. One of the most common and persistent chaparral understory wildflowers.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; persistent chaparral understory species

wildflowers.hp: Important bee forage — visited by many small native bees. Persistent chaparral understory nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow pea flowers on sprawling green stems. Major post-fire colonizer and nitrogen fixer. Important habitat for El Segundo Blue butterfly.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — critical post-fire pioneer

wildflowers.hp: Larval host for Acmon Blue and El Segundo Blue (ESA-listed) butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Pink five-petaled native rose of stream banks and moist slopes. Rose hips persist through winter. Important wildlife food.
wildflowers.hp: Rose hips are critical winter food for birds. Host for Western Tussock Moth.

wildflowers.desc: Spiny seed capsules and white trumpet flowers. Introduced weedy relative of Sacred Datura. All parts highly toxic.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native jimsonweed — HIGHLY TOXIC tropane alkaloids. White trumpet flowers. Seeds and leaves cause dangerous hallucinations and are potentially fatal.

wildflowers.desc: Purple daisy-like flowers into late fall when most wildflowers are done. Woolly leaves. Important late-season pollinator plant.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; important post-fire colonizer

wildflowers.hp: Critical late-season pollinator plant — blooms Jun–Nov when most other wildflowers are done. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and flower flies. Fire-adapted resprouter.

wildflowers.desc: Pink hibiscus-like flowers on wand-like stems emerging from chaparral. Fire follower — seeds can remain dormant in soil for decades until fire smoke and heat trigger germination. Vigorous post-fire colonizer.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower — seeds triggered by heat/smoke

wildflowers.hp: Host for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterfly larvae. Fire follower — seeds dormant in soil for decades until heat/smoke triggers germination. CA endemic.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny magenta flowers that carpet wet areas in spring. Opens in sunshine, closes on cloudy days. Succulent stems.
wildflowers.hp: Red maids — bright magenta flowers opening only in sun. Common in grasslands. Leaves and seeds edible — indigenous food plant.

wildflowers.desc: Vivid orange four-petaled poppy with dark basal spots. Burns and disturbed chaparral. Less common than California Poppy.

fm.Color: Vivid orange with dark basal spots
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds stimulated by smoke/heat

wildflowers.hp: Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by seed-harvesting ants. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery.

wildflowers.desc: Dramatic cobwebby-white thistle heads with crimson-magenta flowers. Native — unlike invasive thistles. Important nectar source.

fm.Native: Yes — do not confuse with invasive thistles
fm.vs: Bull Thistle (C. vulgare, invasive) has CONTINUOUS spiny wings on stems; Cobwebby Thistle (native) has COBWEBBY hairs and no stem wings

wildflowers.hp: Native thistle with cobwebby-white stems. Critical bumble bee forage — large flower heads provide abundant nectar. Important for Painted Lady larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender-pink three-petaled lily. Among the showiest Calochortus. Grasslands and open woodland.
wildflowers.hp: Splendid mariposa lily — lavender-pink petals with dark chevron marks. Chaparral openings. One of the showiest native bulbs.

wildflowers.desc: Dark chocolate-brown nodding bells. Unusual color for a flower. Grasslands and coastal scrub. Bulb was indigenous food source.

fm.Cultural: Bulb was Tongva food source
fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep bulb survives fire underground

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Deep bulb survives fire underground. Indigenous food source — bulbs roasted.

wildflowers.desc: Wavy-leaved rosette sending up tall stalk with small white flowers that open only in late afternoon. Bulb lathers when crushed — indigenous soap.

fm.Cultural: Bulb used as soap by indigenous peoples
fm.vs: Indian Soap Plant (Chenopodium californicum) is a goosefoot with fleshy triangular leaves — completely unrelated despite shared "soap plant" name

wildflowers.hp: Night-blooming — pollinated by hawkmoths (Manduca, Hyles). Indigenous multipurpose plant: bulb used as soap, fish poison, and food. Geophyte.

wildflowers.desc: Striking crimson cone-shaped flower heads. European cover crop that escapes to roadsides. Nitrogen fixer.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native crimson clover — vivid red cylindrical flower heads. European cover crop. Important bee forage. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to purple owl clover of grasslands. Denser flower heads than C. exserta. Hemiparasitic on grass roots.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic on grasses

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring grass roots for water and nutrients while photosynthesizing independently. Visited by hummingbirds and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny blue-violet flowers on grass-like stems. Actually an iris, not a grass. Moist grasslands and openings.
wildflowers.hp: Blue-eyed grass — tiny blue iris relative forming grassland carpets. Not a grass — member of Iridaceae. Important early native bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow snapdragon-like flowers with red spots in throat. Stream banks and seeps. One of the most common streamside wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Creek monkeyflower — yellow flowers with red spots. Stream banks. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly. Edible leaves — peppery taste.

wildflowers.desc: Golden plumes in fall — important late-season nectar source. Often blamed for hay fever but wind-pollinated ragweed is the real culprit.

fm.Ecology: Does NOT cause hay fever — ragweed does

wildflowers.hp: Important fall nectar source for native bees and migrating Monarchs. Does NOT cause hay fever — insect-pollinated (wind-pollinated ragweed is the real culprit).

wildflowers.desc: Coastal buckwheat with ashy-gray woolly leaves and pink-white flowers. Coastal bluffs. Important for native butterflies.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Mormon Metalmark butterfly (Apodemia mormo). Important nectar for native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-magenta flower clusters on prostrate stems on coastal dunes. Declining with beach development. Fragrant.
wildflowers.hp: Sticky sand verbena — pink flower clusters on coastal sand dunes. Sand stabilizer. Evening-fragrant for hawkmoth pollination.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender pea flowers on climbing tendrils in chaparral and woodland. Pods snap open, flinging seeds.
wildflowers.hp: Canyon pea — climbing vine with lavender pea flowers. Canyon and chaparral margins. Nitrogen fixer. Seeds eaten by quail.

wildflowers.desc: Large purple to white iris flowers. Coastal and foothill species. Leathery strap-like evergreen leaves.
wildflowers.hp: Douglas iris — large purple-blue flowers. Coastal grasslands. Hybridizes freely with other native irises. Leaves contain toxins.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-yellow bells persist papery — whisper in the breeze. Fire follower.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — mass germination after fire; seeds viable decades in soil

wildflowers.hp: Whispering bells — persistent dry calyces rattle in wind. Fire follower appearing abundantly after burns. Important bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny orange poppy ONLY appearing after fire. Seeds wait decades in soil.

fm.Ecology: Obligate fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — obligate; seeds germinate ONLY after fire

wildflowers.hp: Fire poppy — brilliant orange-red flowers appearing only after fire. Among the showiest fire-follower wildflowers. No opiate alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow bleeding-heart flowers on tall stems in chaparral burns. Post-fire specialist appearing 1–3 years after fire. Related to Dutchman's Breeches. Tuberous root. One of the showiest fire followers.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower

wildflowers.hp: Golden eardrops — yellow bleeding-heart flowers. Fire follower. Deep tuberous roots survive intense burns. Re-emerges from underground.

wildflowers.desc: Golden desert sunflower carpeting Antelope Valley floors in wet springs. The defining superbloom species — millions per acre in good rain years. Annual that seeds can wait years for the right conditions.
wildflowers.hp: Major Mojave Desert carpet wildflower. Thousands of acres of yellow blooms in good years. Important bee forage during desert spring.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow cruciform flowers blanketing hillsides. Mediterranean invasive — extremely common across LA.

fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — tall dense stands carry fire through native scrub

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Wild mustard — dominant invasive of coastal hillsides. Bright yellow flowers blanket entire slopes. Fire-follower.

wildflowers.desc: White to purple cruciform flowers. Escaped garden radish now invasive.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native wild radish — white-to-purple flowers. Garden escape. Edible young pods. Hybridizes with R. raphanistrum. Very common roadside plant.

wildflowers.desc: Tall anise-scented plant with yellow umbels. Mediterranean invasive. Host for Anise Swallowtail.
wildflowers.hp: Host for Anise Swallowtail butterfly (invasive but ecologically important relationship).

wildflowers.desc: Succulent groundcover with magenta flowers. South African invasive smothering coastal bluffs.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Red and yellow nodding flowers with spurs. Hummingbird favorite. Mountain streams.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — red spurred flowers adapted to hummingbird bill length. Also visited by long-tongued bumble bees.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red star-shaped flowers with fringed petals. Hummingbird-pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Hummingbird-pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: One of few native SoCal orchids. Green and purple-veined. Stream banks. Declining.
wildflowers.hp: Specialized pollination — often by specific bee or moth species.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flowers with yellow stamen cone. Buzz-pollinated. Mildly toxic.
wildflowers.hp: Purple nightshade — native purple flowers with yellow stamens. Most common native Solanum. Berry fruit eaten by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers on coiled stems. Popcorn-scented. Among most common spring wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Common cryptantha — tiny white forget-me-not-like flowers. Among the most abundant spring wildflowers on dry slopes. Nutlet seeds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny pink flowers. Corkscrew seeds drill into soil. Mediterranean weed but important early bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Corkscrew seed dispersal mechanism. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Large white flowers opening at dusk on desert sandy flats. Petals turn pink as they wilt. Key superbloom species in Antelope Valley.
wildflowers.hp: Desert evening primrose — large white flowers opening at dusk, fading pink by morning. Pollinated by White-lined Sphinx Moths. Fragrant.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow daisy with woolly silver foliage. Blooms after any significant rain. Desert roadsides and washes.
wildflowers.hp: Long-blooming desert perennial with papery yellow ray flowers. Toxic to livestock (sesquiterpene lactones). Visited by desert-specialist bees.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet-red paintbrush of desert margins and sagebrush flats. Hemiparasitic. More arid-adapted than C. affinis.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring shrub and grass roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds. More arid-adapted than coastal paintbrushes.

wildflowers.desc: Translucent cream snapdragon-like flowers with maroon spots. Desert endemic. Mimics Mentzelia flowers to lure pollinators.

fm.Ecology: Floral mimic — lures pollinators via deception

wildflowers.hp: Ghost flower — translucent cream flowers mimicking desert bees to attract pollinators via deception. Fascinating pollination biology.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular large pale yellow star flowers that open in late afternoon. Barbed hairs on leaves and stems stick to clothing.
wildflowers.hp: Giant blazing star — large star-shaped flowers with hundreds of stamens. Opens in afternoon heat. Sticky barbed hairs on stems and leaves.

wildflowers.desc: Gray-woolly paintbrush with orange-red tips. Perennial. More woolly than other paintbrushes. Chaparral and coastal scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Perennial root crown survives fire. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Nodding mottled brown-green bells. Related to Chocolate Lily but taller and more northern. Rare in LA County mountains.
wildflowers.hp: Chocolate drops — nodding bell flowers in mottled brown-purple checkered pattern. Woodland bulb. Edible but uncommon.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant scarlet-red larkspur — unusual color for the genus. Hummingbird-pollinated. Rocky chaparral slopes. Summer-dormant.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — tuberous root survives fire; more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant scarlet color rare in the genus. Tuberous root survives fire underground; more abundant post-fire.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of deep blue flowers. Spring chaparral and grasslands. Toxic to livestock.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees. Toxic to livestock — contains delphinine alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet tubular flowers on scrambling shrubby stems. Heart-shaped leaves. Premier hummingbird plant of shady canyons.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — scarlet tubular flowers on scrambling stems. Premier canyon-bottom hummingbird plant.

wildflowers.desc: Orange-gold star flowers in spherical clusters atop bare stems. From underground corm. Grasslands and open scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Important early-season pollen source in grasslands. Geophyte — corm stores energy for explosive post-fire flowering. CA endemic.

wildflowers.desc: Flat white flower clusters with ferny aromatic foliage. Medicinal — "Achilles plant." Both native and introduced forms exist in LA.

fm.Cultural: Medicinal since antiquity — named for Achilles

wildflowers.hp: Important for beneficial insects — predatory wasps, ladybugs, hoverflies. Attracts pest-controlling insects to gardens.

wildflowers.desc: Round leaf wrapping stem like a saucer. Edible — Gold Rush miners ate it to prevent scurvy. Tiny white flowers in center of disc.

fm.Color: White, tiny, in center of disc-leaf
fm.Edible: Yes — mild flavor, high in vitamin C
fm.Cultural: Gold Rush scurvy prevention

wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Edible — yes — mild flavor, high in vitamin c.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet-orange tubes in late summer. Critical late-season hummingbird food when almost nothing else blooms.
wildflowers.hp: Critical late-season nectar for Anna's Hummingbird and migrating Rufous Hummingbird.

wildflowers.desc: Dark maroon-red nodding flowers. Only native peony in SoCal. Burns, shady slopes. Dormant bulb in summer.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep tuberous root; fire follower — more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: White to pink flower clusters on tall bare stems. Important late-season pollinator plant. Many local variants.
wildflowers.hp: Important late-season bee forage. Nectar source for blue and hairstreak butterflies. Dried seed heads provide winter bird food.

wildflowers.desc: Low yellow four-petaled flowers dotting coastal grasslands in spring. Red-spotted center. Ground-hugging.

fm.Color: Yellow with red spots at base

wildflowers.hp: Sun cup — bright yellow bowl-shaped flowers. Common in grasslands. Seeds eaten by sparrows. Important early-season bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Sky-blue bowl-shaped flowers with white center. Classic California spring wildflower. Moist grasslands and woodland edges.
wildflowers.hp: Baby blue eyes — iconic California wildflower with sky-blue petals. Important native bee forage. Grassland and woodland.

wildflowers.desc: White flower with dark purple spot at tip of each petal. Related to Baby Blue Eyes. Moist woodland openings.
wildflowers.hp: Five-spot — white petals each with distinctive purple spot at tip. Woodland specialist. Less common than N. menziesii.

wildflowers.desc: Tiered whorls of purple and white flowers like a pagoda. Shady slopes. One of the showiest spring annuals.
wildflowers.hp: Chinese houses — distinctive whorled purple-and-white flowers in pagoda-like tiers. Shade-tolerant woodland wildflower.

wildflowers.desc: Pink four-petaled flowers with darker center. Blooms as spring fades into summer. Grasslands and open scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees including sweat bees (Halictidae) and small carpenter bees. Late-spring nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Slender annual lupine of desert edges and sandy washes. Blue-purple flowers. Less robust than Arroyo Lupine (L. succulentus).
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Desert superbloom species.

wildflowers.desc: Native desert Datura with violet-throated white trumpets. Smaller than Sacred Datura. Desert washes.
wildflowers.hp: Desert jimsonweed — TOXIC. Purple-tinged white trumpets. Distinguished from D. wrightii by purple throat markings and shorter stature.

wildflowers.desc: Primary Monarch butterfly host plant in LA. Narrow leaves, white-pink flower clusters. Critical for Monarch conservation. All milkweeds contain toxic cardiac glycosides.

fm.Ecology: Primary Monarch butterfly host plant in LA

wildflowers.hp: Primary host plant for Monarch butterfly (Danaus plexippus). Also visited by native bees, beetles, and wasps.

wildflowers.desc: Woolly milkweed with drooping maroon-cream flower clusters. Monarch host plant. More drought-tolerant than Narrowleaf Milkweed.

fm.Ecology: Monarch host plant

wildflowers.hp: Host for Monarch butterfly. Visited by native bees — buzz-pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: Tall aromatic herb of stream banks. Silver-backed leaves. Major indigenous medicinal plant — used for pain, dreams, and ceremony. Spreads by rhizomes.

fm.Cultural: Major Tongva/Chumash medicinal plant — dreams, pain relief

wildflowers.hp: Mugwort — aromatic silver-green leaves. Important indigenous ceremonial plant. Used for smudging. Provides hummingbird nesting material.

wildflowers.desc: Painful stinging hairs inject formic acid on contact. But: young leaves are edible when cooked (like spinach) and highly nutritious. Important larval host for butterflies.

fm.Edible: Young leaves edible when cooked — very nutritious
fm.Ecology: Host plant for Red Admiral butterfly
fm.Toxicity: Painful — formic acid injection on contact

wildflowers.hp: Larval host for Red Admiral and West Coast Lady butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: White snapdragon-like flowers on slender stems. Native annual of disturbed slopes. Important pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Coulter's snapdragon — white flowers with purple palate. Visited by large native bees that can open the snapdragon 'mouth.'

wildflowers.desc: Dark purple-blue spikes on a leguminous shrub. Nitrogen fixer. Chaparral and woodland understory.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer (legume)

wildflowers.hp: Host plant for California Dogface butterfly (Zerene eurydice — CA state insect), Gray Hairstreak, and Northern Cloudy-wing. Nitrogen fixer. Chaparral understory.

wildflowers.desc: Cream pea flowers on sprawling stems. Toxic to livestock ("locoweed" — causes neurological damage). Coastal sage scrub and grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Southern California locoweed — inflated seed pods. Accumulates toxic selenium from soil. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple pea flowers on thorny stems. Only species in its genus. Fire-resprouting. Post-fire specialist.

fm.Ecology: Fire-resprouting — post-fire specialist
fm.Fire: Resprouter + fire follower — resprouts from root crown; also seed-activated

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by native bees. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow flowers blanketing small shrubs in early spring when everything else is still brown. Among the first chaparral plants to bloom each year. Important early-season bee forage.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; early post-fire bloomer

wildflowers.hp: Among the first chaparral shrubs to bloom each year (Feb–Apr). Critical early-season bee forage when little else is flowering.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender mariposa lily of dry rocky slopes. Named for Sara Plummer Lemmon, pioneering CA botanist. Griffith Park specialty.

fm.Cultural: Named for Sara Plummer Lemmon — pioneering CA botanist

wildflowers.hp: Plummer's mariposa lily — CNPS 4.2. Pink-lavender petals. Named for Sara Plummer (Lemmon), pioneering CA botanist.

wildflowers.desc: Parasitic evergreen growing on oak branches. White berries in winter. Spread by Phainopepla bird. Traditional holiday decoration.

fm.Ecology: Parasitic on oaks — spread by Phainopepla
fm.Cultural: Traditional holiday plant

wildflowers.hp: Spread by Phainopepla (silky flycatcher) which eats berries. Important winter food for many birds.

wildflowers.desc: Native dandelion-like flower of mountain meadows. Larger than introduced dandelion. Orange sap when broken.
wildflowers.hp: Mountain dandelion with large orange-yellow heads. Important early-season bee forage in mountain meadows. Latex-producing.

wildflowers.desc: Wiry-stemmed plant with small pink flowers. Rush-skeleton appearance. Very common on dry slopes in late season.
wildflowers.hp: Tall wirelettuce with slender pink ray flowers. Late-blooming — provides fall nectar when few other wildflowers remain.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly herb with tiny white flowers in whorls. Mediterranean weed but historically important cough remedy. Very common on disturbed ground.

fm.Cultural: Traditional cough/throat remedy

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native horehound — bitter aromatic leaves used in cough drops and candy. White woolly stems. Naturalizing in disturbed areas.

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow-flowered mustard blanketing LA hillsides. Even more aggressive than Hirschfeldia. Seeds produce the condiment.

fm.Cultural: Seeds are true mustard seeds
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — tall dense stands increase fire risk

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Black mustard — tall golden-yellow fields covering coastal hills. Mediterranean origin. Seeds yield mustard condiment.

wildflowers.desc: Dramatic spiny thistle with white-veined leaves. Mediterranean invasive. Silymarin extract used medicinally for liver health.

fm.Cultural: Silymarin extract — liver support

wildflowers.hp: Non-native milk thistle — distinctive white-veined leaves. Seeds contain silymarin, used in liver medicine. Important goldfinch food.

wildflowers.desc: Larger-flowered relative of Filaree. Musky scent. Same corkscrew-seed trick. Very common lawn and field weed.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Corkscrew seed dispersal mechanism.

wildflowers.desc: Spiny yellow thistle invading grasslands and scrub. Mediterranean origin. Less aggressive than Yellow Star-thistle but still problematic.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Invasive star-thistle with spiny flower heads. Mediterranean origin. Displaces native annual grassland species.

wildflowers.desc: Small pink flowers. Round "cheese wheel" seed capsules. Among the most common LA weeds. Edible leaves and immature seeds.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Host plant for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies. Edible — leaves and immature seeds.

wildflowers.desc: South American shrub-tree with yellow tubular flowers. Naturalized in washes and roadsides. All parts toxic. Hummingbird-visited.
wildflowers.hp: Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by birds. Hummingbird-pollinated. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Large tropical-looking plant with palmate leaves and spiny red seed pods. Naturalized in LA washes. Seeds contain ricin — extremely toxic.

fm.Seeds: Contain ricin — among most toxic natural substances

wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and ants. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly stems with small pearly flower heads that persist when dried. Smells like maple syrup. Common post-fire species.

fm.Ecology: Post-fire colonizer

wildflowers.hp: California everlasting — woolly-white aromatic plant. Leaves used in traditional medicine. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Large yellow trumpet flowers and striped gourd fruits. Massive underground root. Native gourd — related to squash but extremely bitter.
wildflowers.hp: Coyote melon — huge tuberous root and sprawling vines. Visited by squash bees (Peponapis, Xenoglossa) — specialist pollinators. Gourd fruit inedible but used as soap by indigenous peoples. Extremely drought-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Low silver-gray dune plant. Important coastal sand stabilizer. Windblown sand collects around it forming embryo dunes.
wildflowers.hp: Beach bur — coastal sand dune specialist. Bur fruits disperse on fur and clothing. Important dune stabilizer.

wildflowers.desc: Striking lavender-blue flowers erupting from woolly thistle-like bracts. Desert annual. Among the most photogenic SoCal wildflowers. Tongva used seeds for eye treatment.

fm.Cultural: Tongva used seeds medicinally

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in desert habitats. Tongva used seeds medicinally for eye treatment. Seeds nutritious — indigenous food plant.

wildflowers.desc: Spiny yellow thistle — California's most damaging invasive weed. Covers 15 million acres statewide. Toxic to horses (chewing disease).

fm.Toxicity: Toxic to horses — causes chewing disease
fm.Ecology: 15 million acres statewide

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow clover-like flowers carpeting lawns and gardens in spring. South African bulb nearly impossible to eradicate. Each plant makes 20+ tiny bulbils.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Bermuda buttercup — extremely aggressive bulb invader from South Africa. Dense yellow carpet flowers. Bulbils make eradication nearly impossible.

wildflowers.desc: Native onion with papery red-skinned bulb. White-pink flowers in umbels. Edible — onion-scented. Grasslands and scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Edible — mild onion flavor. Red-skinned bulb diagnostic.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny prostrate weed of sidewalk cracks and gardens. White milky sap. Among the most ubiquitous urban weeds in LA.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native spotted spurge — prostrate mat with milky sap. Tiny flowers visited by small sweat bees and ants. Common in sidewalk cracks and lawns.

wildflowers.desc: Golden daisies on woolly stems. More montane than Golden Yarrow. Mountain meadows and openings.
wildflowers.hp: Woolly sunflower — silvery foliage tolerates extreme drought. Important pollinator resource on dry rocky slopes.

wildflowers.desc: Slender plant with tiny blue-purple snapdragon flowers. Sandy soil. Easy to overlook but common in the right habitat.
wildflowers.hp: Blue toad-flax — slender spikes of tiny blue-violet snapdragon flowers. Easily overlooked but common in sandy grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow flowers opening at dusk, wilting by morning. Hawkmoth-pollinated. Stream banks and moist meadows. Biennial.
wildflowers.hp: Hooker's evening primrose — tall yellow flowers opening at dusk. Night-pollinated by hawkmoths. Biennial with large rosette first year.

wildflowers.desc: White-pink trumpet flowers on aggressive creeping vine. European invasive nearly impossible to eradicate. Deep perennial roots.

fm.Root: Deep perennial — nearly impossible to eradicate

wildflowers.hp: Non-native field bindweed — extremely persistent weed with deep roots (20+ ft). White-pink trumpet flowers. Nearly impossible to eradicate.

wildflowers.desc: Pink mallow flowers with five dark maroon basal spots. Mojave Desert annual after good rain years. Distinctive and photogenic.

fm.Color: Pink with 5 dark maroon spots at base

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Host plant for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with white-tipped rays — similar to L. platyglossa but more inland. Antelope Valley grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Fremont's tidytips — distinguished from L. platyglossa by deeper yellow tips. Spring bloom specialist in grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant scarlet tubular flowers at stream banks. Hummingbird specialist. Much showier than yellow Creek Monkeyflower. Mountain streams.
wildflowers.hp: Scarlet monkeyflower — brilliant red flowers pollinated by hummingbirds. Mountain streams. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate maidenhair-like foliage with dangling stamens — no petals. Wind-pollinated. Shady mountain stream banks.
wildflowers.hp: Meadow rue — delicate wind-pollinated flowers with hanging stamens. No petals — sepals drop early. Moist mountain forests.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red tubular flowers on tall spikes. Hummingbird pollinated. Stream banks — very rare in LA County, at southern range limit.

fm.Habitat: Stream banks — very rare in LA County

wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant red tubular flowers. Very rare in LA County — at extreme southern range limit.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow violet with purple-veined lower petals. Mountain forest understory and meadows. Sweet fragrance.

fm.Color: Yellow with purple veins on lower petals

wildflowers.hp: Host for fritillary butterflies. Seeds dispersed by ants (myrmecochory).

wildflowers.desc: Intensely aromatic purple flower heads. Mountain trails. Crushed leaves release powerful mint-oregano fragrance. Tea plant.

fm.Cultural: Traditional tea plant

wildflowers.hp: Mountain pennyroyal — intensely fragrant purple-pink heads. Alpine meadow specialist. Traditional indigenous remedy for colds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy of high alpine talus and scree above treeline. Among the highest-elevation wildflowers in LA County. San Gabriel Mountains peaks.

fm.Habitat: Alpine talus above treeline — Mt. Baldy, Baden-Powell

wildflowers.hp: Alpine gold — mountain specialist above 8,000 ft. Aromatic glandular foliage. Important high-elevation pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Bright orange cruciform flower clusters on mountain slopes. Vibrant color at mid-elevation after snowmelt. Biennial.
wildflowers.hp: Sierra wallflower — brilliant orange-yellow flower clusters. Important mountain pollinator resource. Host for Sara Orangetip butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Fleshy beach plant with small lavender cruciform flowers. European invasive now dominant on LA beaches. Edible — mustard family.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — yes — peppery mustard flavor.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple trumpets on dune-binding vine with kidney-shaped leaves. Important sand stabilizer. Declining with coastal development.

fm.Ecology: Critical dune stabilizer

wildflowers.hp: Beach morning glory — fleshy leaves on coastal sand dunes. Important dune stabilizer. Purple-pink funnel flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white-pink mallow flowers on prostrate silvery stems. Alkaline soils and disturbed ground. Extremely common but overlooked.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Host for Common Checkered Skipper butterfly larvae. Alkaline soil specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray woolly plant of dry grasslands. Sticky star-shaped leaves. Seeds eaten by doves and quail — hence "turkey" mullein.
wildflowers.hp: Native turkey mullein — gray-woolly leaves. Seeds avidly eaten by doves and quail ('dove weed'). Visited by small native bees. Traditionally used as fish stupefacient.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads with strong minty fragrance. Common on dry slopes. Tea plant — excellent fresh or dried.
wildflowers.hp: Coyote mint — woolly-leaved perennial with purple flower whorls. Coastal sage scrub. Important bee forage in summer months.

wildflowers.desc: White papery flower heads that persist dried for months. Mountain openings. Important host for Painted Lady and American Lady butterflies.

fm.Ecology: Host for Painted Lady and American Lady butterflies

wildflowers.hp: Host for Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) and American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny three-toned flowers — violet ring, yellow throat, dark center eye. Classic California spring annual. Grasslands.

fm.Color: Violet ring, yellow throat, dark eye

wildflowers.hp: Bird's eye gilia — tricolored petals (violet, yellow, purple). One of the showiest small annuals. Grassland specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny pink flowers in spiny involucres on wiry stems. Very common on dry slopes but easily overlooked. Annual.
wildflowers.hp: Fringed spineflower — fringed involucral bracts. Sandy coastal and foothill habitats. Important for native seed-harvesting ants.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple flowers with yellow stamen cone. Shrubby nightshade of woodland edges. Buzz-pollinated by native bees.
wildflowers.hp: Blue witch — native nightshade with blue-purple flowers. Coastal sage scrub and woodland. Important native bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow daisies on extremely sticky stems. Dominant late-summer annual on dry grasslands. Pungent tar-like smell.

fm.Ecology: Dominant late-summer grassland annual

wildflowers.hp: Fascicled tarplant — sticky resinous annual of grasslands. Important late-season native bee forage. Tarry scent.

wildflowers.desc: Tubular red flowers on nearly leafless green stems. Major desert hummingbird plant. "Chuparosa" = hummingbird in Spanish. Desert washes.

fm.Cultural: Chuparosa = hummingbird (Spanish)

wildflowers.hp: Major desert hummingbird plant — critical nectar source in desert washes where few other flowers bloom. 'Chuparosa' = hummingbird in Spanish.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow fall-blooming shrub of coastal slopes. Important late-season nectar when most other plants are done. Aromatic resinous foliage.
wildflowers.hp: Critical late-season nectar source (Aug–Nov). Important for native bees and migrating butterflies when most plants have finished.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple mint family flowers with wrinkled hairy leaves. Shady woodland understory. Doesn't sting like true nettles despite the name.

fm.Note: Does NOT sting — not a true nettle

wildflowers.hp: California hedge nettle — pink tubular flowers in whorls. Square stems (Lamiaceae diagnostic). Moist woodland shade.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flower clusters with carrot-like foliage. Early spring bloomer in grasslands. Important indigenous food plant — roots edible.

fm.Cultural: Roots edible — indigenous food plant

wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects. Edible roots — important indigenous food plant. Early spring nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Wild ancestor of cultivated sunflower. Large yellow heads tracking the sun (heliotropism in young plants). Roadsides and disturbed areas. Native.

fm.Color: Yellow rays, dark disk — classic sunflower
fm.Ecology: Important bird food

wildflowers.hp: Major bee and bird plant. Visited by 50+ native bee species. Seeds eaten by goldfinches, sparrows.

wildflowers.desc: Tall sticky-hairy plant with yellow flowers. Named for growing along old telegraph lines. Among the most common disturbed-ground plants in LA.

fm.Cultural: Named for growing along telegraph lines

wildflowers.hp: Late-season pollinator plant on disturbed ground. Sticky-hairy stems. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisies on woolly white cushions. Sandy desert and grassland soil. Miniature but abundant — forms golden carpets.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny woolly daisy forming yellow carpet displays in desert. Mojave Desert specialist. Superbloom indicator.

wildflowers.desc: Golden-yellow mariposa lily with hairy petals and brown-red markings. Coastal and foothill slopes. Named for botanist C.M. Weed. Fire follower — often abundant 2–3 years post-fire.

fm.Color: Golden-yellow with brown-red markings and hairy inner surface
fm.Ecology: Fire follower — abundant post-fire
fm.Fire: Resprouter — bulb survives fire; fire follower — abundant 2–3 years post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulb provides food for gophers and ground squirrels. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant nodding white globe-shaped flowers in shady woodland. Among the most beautiful native wildflowers. At southern range edge in San Gabriel Mountain canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady woodland, canyon bottoms — at southern range edge
fm.Conservation: Rare in LA County — range edge

wildflowers.hp: White globe lily — pendant globe-shaped flowers. Deep shade specialist. Among the most elegant native bulbs.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow mariposa lily with no basal markings — unusually uniform color for the genus. Pine forest and chaparral openings. Mid-elevation.

fm.Color: Uniform yellow — no basal markings (unusual for genus)
fm.Habitat: Pine forest, chaparral openings at mid-elevation

wildflowers.hp: Golden star lily — uniform golden-yellow without dark markings. Desert-chaparral transition. Less showy than C. clavatus.

wildflowers.desc: Highly variable mariposa lily — white, yellow, pink, or red with intricate central markings resembling a butterfly. At range edge. California's showiest wildflower genus.

fm.Color: Highly variable — white, yellow, pink, or red with butterfly-like markings
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, open woodland — at range edge

wildflowers.hp: Butterfly mariposa lily — variable colors (white/yellow/pink) with butterfly-like markings. Grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to pale yellow poppy relative. Hairy stems with flowers nodding in bud, erect when open. Grasslands. Classic spring wildflower of open slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Cream cups — delicate cream-yellow flowers. Grassland annual. Pollinated by small native bees and beetles.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flower clusters with distinctive burr-like seeds that hitchhike on clothing and fur. Very common woodland understory plant.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects including parasitoid wasps, lacewings, and hoverflies. Burr-like seeds hitchhike on clothing and animal fur.

wildflowers.desc: Dark magenta sand verbena forming prostrate mats on beach sand. Sticky stems trap sand grains. Less common than purple sand verbena. Important dune stabilizer declining with coastal development.

fm.Ecology: Dune stabilizer

wildflowers.hp: Red sand verbena — dark magenta flowers on beach strand. Most deeply colored Abronia. Important dune plant. Evening-fragrant.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray-green coastal plant growing on bluffs and sandy ground. Once widespread on LA coastal strand — now largely lost to development. Important coastal sage scrub component.

fm.Conservation: Largely extirpated from LA coast by development

wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple snapdragon flowers on climbing stems in chaparral. Twining tendrils wrap around adjacent plants. Much more delicate than garden snapdragons.
wildflowers.hp: Wild snapdragon — many-flowered spikes on rocky slopes. Visited by carpenter bees strong enough to open the closed flower.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers in shady moist canyons. Round scalloped basal leaves. One of the few LA wildflowers preferring deep shade and constant moisture.
wildflowers.hp: Brook foam — delicate white flowers on brook banks. Shade and moisture specialist. Mountain canyons.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flower with pink-striped ray undersides. Common spring desert annual. Often grows up through dead shrubs for support.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub — often leans on dead shrubs

wildflowers.hp: Desert chicory — white dandelion-like flowers opening in morning. Important early-season desert pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular white lily of desert sandy flats. Deeply buried bulb to 18 in deep. Only blooms after heavy rain — can wait years underground. The showiest desert wildflower.

fm.Color: Large white trumpets with green midstripes
fm.Bulb: 18 in deep — dormant for years between blooms
fm.Habitat: Desert sandy flats — Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Bulb/corm provides food for gophers.

wildflowers.desc: One of the earliest spring bloomers — white flower clusters appear before the huge round basal leaves. Moist mountain streambanks. At southern range limit.

fm.Height: 6–18 in (flower stalk), leaves to 1 ft across
fm.Ecology: Among earliest spring bloomers — before leaves
fm.Habitat: Mountain streambanks — at southern range limit

wildflowers.hp: Western coltsfoot — large basal leaves appearing after flower stalks. Moist mountain stream banks. Early-blooming.

wildflowers.desc: Intensely fragrant sage relative with tubular crimson flowers. Crushed leaves smell like a combination of sage and camphor. Dry chaparral openings.

fm.vs: Ross' Pitcher Sage (L. rossii) is much rarer, found only in Liebre Mtns, with kinked inflorescence axes and large leafy bracts equaling flowers.

wildflowers.hp: Crimson pitcher sage — fragrant crimson tubular flowers. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Important hummingbird nectar plant.

wildflowers.desc: Wind-pollinated ragweed — one of LA's worst hay fever plants. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes in disturbed areas. Inconspicuous flowers, major allergen impact.
wildflowers.hp: Western ragweed — major pollen allergen. Wind-pollinated. Important fall allergy culprit in LA basin.

wildflowers.desc: Stiff golden-flowered shrub with saw-toothed leaves. Very common on disturbed slopes. Fall bloomer — important late-season nectar.
wildflowers.hp: Sawtooth goldenbush — resinous shrubby perennial of coastal sage scrub. Late-season fall bloomer. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow snapdragon-like flowers on spreading shrub. Dry chaparral and desert edge. More drought tolerant than most penstemons.
wildflowers.hp: Snapdragon penstemon — yellow snapdragon-like flowers. Rocky slopes in chaparral. Important hummingbird nectar plant.

wildflowers.desc: Mojave Desert sunflower relative with silver-gray leaves. More inland and desert-edge than E. californica. Important bee forage on desert margins.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland edges

wildflowers.hp: Acton encelia — Mojave Desert shrub with yellow daisy flowers. Distinguished from E. farinosa by green (not silvery) leaves.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisies with extremely sticky white-resinous buds. Medicinal — used to treat poison oak rash. Roadsides and disturbed ground. Resinous buds diagnostic.

fm.Cultural: Poultice for poison oak rash

wildflowers.hp: Great Valley gumplant — extremely sticky flower heads trap small insects. Resin used in traditional asthma remedies.

wildflowers.desc: White to pale pink pincushion flowers on gray-green aromatic foliage. Very common spring annual on burns and dry slopes. Distinctive flat-topped flower heads.
wildflowers.hp: White pincushion — white flower heads on artemisia-scented foliage. Coastal sage scrub. Fire follower establishing after burns.

wildflowers.desc: White pincushion flowers in sandy desert. Classic spring annual of Antelope Valley and desert washes. Woolly stems.
wildflowers.hp: Fremont pincushion — white pincushion heads in desert washes. Mojave Desert annual. Important spring bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Coiled blue-lavender flower clusters — one of the most common spring annuals in coastal sage scrub and disturbed areas. Strong unpleasant scent. Attracts swarms of native bees.

fm.Habitat: Sage scrub, disturbed slopes — very common

wildflowers.hp: Among the most important native bee forage plants. Visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender bell-shaped flowers with dark veins — among the showiest spring annuals. Sticky hairy stems. Common on burns and disturbed slopes. CA endemic.

fm.Color: Lavender bells with dark veins — showy

wildflowers.hp: Major native bee forage. Sticky glandular hairs trap small insects. CA endemic fire follower providing critical post-fire pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers in shaded understory of chaparral. Extremely common spring annual but overlooked. Ferny aromatic foliage. One of the first annuals after fire.
wildflowers.hp: Common eucrypta — glandular-hairy annual of shaded canyon banks. Chrysanthemum-like leaves. Important early-season shade pollinator.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny blue globe-shaped flower clusters on wiry stems. Common but overlooked spring annual in grasslands and sage scrub openings.
wildflowers.hp: Angel gilia — pale lavender flowers. Named for Los Angeles. Local Asteraceae with limited coastal range.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of blue-purple tubular flowers — among the showiest wildflowers on LA trails. Whorled flower clusters. Chaparral openings and road cuts. Bee and hummingbird pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by bumble bees and hummingbirds. One of the showiest native penstemons.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to white tubular flowers on spreading shrubby stems. Very common in chaparral throughout LA mountains.
wildflowers.hp: Bush penstemon — pale pink gaping flowers. Chaparral and woodland. Fire-adapted resprouter from woody base.

wildflowers.desc: Small white star-shaped flowers with yellow stamens. Black berries (toxic). Extremely common on disturbed ground and trail edges throughout LA. Douglas's nightshade.

fm.Habitat: Disturbed ground, trail edges — very common

wildflowers.hp: White nightshade — native nightshade with white flowers. Moist shaded areas. Berries eaten by birds when ripe.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny prostrate spurge forming mats on dry ground. White-margined leaves. Extremely common but utterly overlooked — grows in every crack and trailhead parking lot.

fm.Habitat: Dry ground, trail edges, parking lots — ubiquitous

wildflowers.hp: Native rattlesnake spurge — mat-forming with white-margined leaves. Tiny flowers visited by minute sweat bees and parasitoid wasps. Desert and sandy habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink-purple lupine of desert washes and sandy flats. Classic Antelope Valley superbloom species. Shorter than Arroyo Lupine.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes, sandy flats — superbloom species

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Nitrogen fixer. Key Antelope Valley superbloom species providing mass nectar resources.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white forget-me-not-like flowers covering grasslands in spring. Extremely common but small. Coiled cymes unfurling as flowers open.
wildflowers.hp: Popcorn flower — small white flowers with yellow eye. Open grasslands. Roots stain fingers yellow-brown.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow four-petaled evening primrose flowers. Miniature annual extremely common in grasslands — easy to overlook. Reddish stems.
wildflowers.hp: Miniature sun cup — tiny yellow flowers opening in afternoon sun. Among the smallest evening primrose relatives. Sandy soils.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-white flowers with orange-red streaks opening in afternoon heat. Sandpaper-rough leaves that stick to clothing. Desert washes.
wildflowers.hp: Sand blazing star — pale cream flowers with reddish veins. Sandy desert washes. Bracts surround flower base.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow sunflower-like heads nodding on slender stems. Chaparral openings and road cuts. More delicate than true Helianthus.
wildflowers.hp: Slender sunflower — nodding yellow heads in chaparral openings. Distinguished from Helianthus by reflexed ray flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with finely dissected carrot-like foliage. Shady woodland understory and chaparral edges. Often the most common carrot-family wildflower on shaded trails.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects including parasitoid wasps and hoverflies. Important shady-trail pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Dense white to pale lavender coiled flower clusters. Extremely sticky hairy stems. Common on mountain road cuts and burns. Strong unpleasant odor.
wildflowers.hp: Mountain bee forage. Sticky glandular hairs may trap small insects. Post-fire specialist providing high-elevation pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers with notched petals in sticky calyx tubes. Common spring annual in grasslands and sage scrub. Stems sticky below nodes — catches small insects.
wildflowers.hp: White catch-fly — sticky stems trap small insects (not carnivorous). White flowers open at night. Woodland specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple trumpet flowers in open umbels on leafless stalks. Spring bulb of grasslands and open woodland. Edible corms were important indigenous food. Formerly Brodiaea.

fm.Cultural: Edible corms — indigenous food plant

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Edible corms were important indigenous food. Geophyte — enhanced flowering after fire due to increased sunlight.

wildflowers.desc: Red-orange tubular flowers — the red counterpart of Sticky Monkeyflower. More southern distribution. Hybridizes freely with D. aurantiacus where ranges overlap.

fm.Ecology: Hybridizes with D. aurantiacus — intermediate colors common

wildflowers.hp: Red bush monkey flower — brick-red tubular flowers. Coastal sage scrub. Important hummingbird nectar plant.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to pale yellow tubular flowers. Very common in coastal sage scrub from LA to Ventura. Important pollinator plant. Formerly Mimulus longiflorus.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — very common SMM

wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees and hummingbirds. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white four-petaled flowers on wiry stems with whorled narrow leaves. Extremely common in chaparral and sage scrub understory — one of the most ubiquitous plants on LA trails.
wildflowers.hp: Narrow-leaved bedstraw — wiry stems with tiny white flowers. Chaparral understory. Provides ground cover preventing erosion.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray-green aromatic shrub on coastal bluffs and dunes. Important host for Painted Lady butterfly. Crushed foliage smells medicinal.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Painted Lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui).

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow flower spike from huge fuzzy gray rosette. European weed now common on every disturbed mountain roadside. Leaves used as toilet paper substitute (hence "cowboy toilet paper").

fm.Cultural: Called "cowboy toilet paper" — soft fuzzy leaves

wildflowers.hp: Non-native woolly mullein — towering biennial with flannel-textured leaves. Used medicinally for centuries. Flowers yield yellow dye.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers in umbels on slender stems. One of the most common but overlooked spring annuals in grasslands. Miniature carrot-family plant.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands, sage scrub openings — very common

wildflowers.hp: Tiny umbels attract minute beneficial insects. Easily overlooked but ecologically important as a widespread nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Tall carrot-family plant with purple-spotted hollow stems. EXTREMELY toxic — killed Socrates. White umbrella flowers. Common along streams and disturbed wet areas. Often confused with wild carrot.

fm.Toxicity: EXTREMELY toxic — all parts. Killed Socrates. Even skin contact can cause reactions.

wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects including parasitoid wasps, lacewings, and hoverflies. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender-blue asters — among the showiest desert wildflowers. Woody base with spiny-tipped leaves. Desert rocky slopes and washes.

fm.Color: Lavender-blue rays, yellow disk — large, showy

wildflowers.hp: Mojave aster — showy lavender-blue ray flowers. Important desert pollinator plant. Distinctive twisted leaves.

wildflowers.desc: Apricot-orange globemallow flowers on gray-green woolly stems. Common desert roadsides. Stellate hairs irritate skin like Flannel Bush. Multiple desert color morphs.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in desert habitats. Host for Common Checkered Skipper butterfly. Stellate hairs irritate skin.

wildflowers.desc: Low mounding desert shrub covered in yellow flowers in spring. Very common in Joshua tree woodland and creosote scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Cooper's goldenbush — low desert shrub with yellow button heads. Important late-season pollinator resource in Mojave.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant orange-red mariposa lily — the most vividly colored of all Calochortus. Desert rocky slopes after wet winters. Very rare and localized in LA County (Antelope Valley edge).

fm.Habitat: Desert rocky slopes — Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Desert mariposa lily — brilliant orange-red flowers. Mojave Desert. Among the showiest desert wildflowers. Fire follower.

wildflowers.desc: Purple lupine with sweetly fragrant flowers — unusual for lupines. Desert sandy flats. Major superbloom species at Antelope Valley.
wildflowers.hp: Sweetly fragrant — unusual for lupines. Bumble bee pollinated. Major Antelope Valley superbloom species.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flowers seemingly floating 2 ft above ground on invisible thread-thin stems. Among the most ethereal desert wildflowers. Sandy-gravelly desert flats.
wildflowers.hp: Gravel ghost — translucent white flowers on thread-thin stems. One of the most ethereal desert wildflowers. Virtually leafless.

wildflowers.desc: White-pink flower clusters with milky sap. Spreading by rhizomes in moist areas. Fiber plant — Tongva used stems for cordage and nets. Related to milkweed.

fm.Cultural: Tongva cordage plant — fiber from stems

wildflowers.hp: Related to milkweeds. Visited by native bees and butterflies. Monarch sometimes uses as nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Woolly milkweed with dense cream-pink flower clusters. Largest-flowered milkweed in SoCal. Monarch host plant. Dry slopes and chaparral openings.
wildflowers.hp: Monarch butterfly host plant. Also used by Queen butterfly. Important nectar for native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tall aromatic herb — the wild form of culinary tarragon. Narrow willow-like leaves. Late summer/fall bloomer. Widespread in many plant communities.

fm.Cultural: Wild form of culinary tarragon

wildflowers.hp: Wild tarragon — aromatic herb. Source of tarragon spice. Wind-pollinated. Important browse for pronghorn and deer.

wildflowers.desc: Bristly golden-flowered perennial of dry slopes. Several subspecies in SMM. Late summer bloomer. Stems and leaves densely hairy.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season bee forage on dry slopes. Blooms Jul–Nov when few other plants flower.

wildflowers.desc: Slender yellow-flowered tarweed with extremely sticky stems. Common spring annual in grasslands and woodland openings. Strong tar-like smell.
wildflowers.hp: Slender tarweed — delicate glandular annual with small yellow flowers. Evening-opening blooms attract nocturnal pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flowers, larger than R. neomexicana. Common spring annual in chaparral burns and disturbed areas. Often confused with Desert Chicory.
wildflowers.hp: California chicory — white dandelion-like flowers with pink-striped buds. Chaparral understory. Blooms in dense shade.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy clusters on gray-green shrubby plant. Washes and dry stream beds. Mildly toxic — pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
wildflowers.hp: Threadleaf ragwort — threadlike leaves. Desert perennial. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow goldenrod plumes in late summer and fall. Important late-season pollinator plant. Incorrectly blamed for hay fever — actually insect-pollinated.

fm.Ecology: Insect-pollinated — wrongly blamed for hay fever (that is ragweed)

wildflowers.hp: Important late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies including Painted Lady. Insect-pollinated — not a hay fever plant despite common misconception.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers producing showy silver-white seed puffs. Very common spring annual in grasslands. The seed heads are more conspicuous than the flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Silver puffs — elegant silvery seed heads. Grassland annual. Important early seed source for finches.

wildflowers.desc: White forget-me-not-like flowers with prickly nutlets that stick to clothing. Very common spring annual in chaparral and coastal scrub openings.
wildflowers.hp: Prickly cryptantha — rough-hairy stems with minute white flowers. Dry slopes. Distinguished by warty (muricate) nutlets.

wildflowers.desc: Fleshy blue-green plant with small white flowers curving in scorpioid cymes. Salt-tolerant — marshes, alkaline areas, beach edges. Spreads by rhizomes.

fm.Habitat: Salt marshes, alkaline areas, beach edges

wildflowers.hp: Seaside heliotrope — fleshy salt-tolerant leaves with tiny white flowers in scorpioid cymes. Alkaline and coastal habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Among the earliest spring wildflowers — white four-petaled flowers appearing in January in shady woodland. Edible — peppery watercress relative. A harbinger of spring on shady trails.

fm.Ecology: Among earliest spring bloomers — January in mild years

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — peppery — watercress relative.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny plant with distinctive round flat lace-edged seed pods — more conspicuous than the small white flowers. Very common spring annual in grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Lace pod — distinctive circular winged seed pods with lace-like veined margins. Woodland annual. Pods valued by dried flower arrangers.

wildflowers.desc: Similar to Lace Pod but with deeply fringed seed pods. Spring annual in grasslands and rocky slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Fringe pod — fringed-margin seed pods. Distinguished from T. curvipes by fringed (not smooth) pod edges.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow cruciform flowers. Rare annual of heavy clay grasslands. Increasingly uncommon due to habitat loss. California endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny kidney-shaped leaves forming dense ground cover. Almost invisible flowers. One of the most common ground-cover natives in shade — looks like a miniature lily pad carpet.

fm.Habitat: Shaded ground — very common ground cover

wildflowers.hp: Western ponyfoot — tiny kidney-shaped leaves forming dense mats. Native ground cover. Shade-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Small gray-green subshrub with tiny white flowers. Salt-tolerant specialist of alkali flats and salt marshes. Indicator of saline soils.
wildflowers.hp: Alkali weed — tiny salt-tolerant shrublet of alkaline flats. Indicator of saline soils. White flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-magenta lupine covered in stinging hairs that cause painful rash on contact. Common on burns and dry slopes. The only stinging lupine. Fire follower.

fm.Toxicity: STINGING hairs — painful rash on contact

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated despite stinging hairs. Fire follower — mass germination post-fire. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple lupine with distinctly truncated (flat-topped) leaves. Common spring annual on burns and grassy openings in chaparral.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Fire follower. Nitrogen fixer. Distinctive truncated leaflets.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink star-shaped flowers with white center. Stunning miniature gentian of grasslands and openings. One of the most beautiful small wildflowers.

fm.Color: Bright pink stars with white center

wildflowers.hp: Native canchalagua — showy pink flowers. Traditional indigenous medicine for fever and stomach ailments. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and bee flies.

wildflowers.desc: Deep purple bell-shaped flowers. Classic spring annual of coastal sage scrub and chaparral. More delicate than P. grandiflora. Common on every SMM trail in spring.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral — very common

wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and small carpenter bees.

wildflowers.desc: White to lavender flowers on sprawling branching stems. Perennial phacelia of chaparral and pine forest. Less showy than annual phacelia species but persistent.
wildflowers.hp: Branching phacelia — many-branched with coiled flower clusters. Important native bee forage on coastal bluffs and rocky slopes.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue flowers with white center on extremely sticky hairy stems. One of the most beautiful annual wildflowers. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral openings.

fm.Color: Deep blue with white center — showy

wildflowers.hp: Excellent native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and mining bees. Sticky stems may trap small invertebrates.

wildflowers.desc: Blue flowers with long curving stamens on aromatic stems that smell like vinegar. Late summer/fall annual of dry grasslands. Annual relative of Woolly Bluecurls.

fm.Ecology: Annual relative of perennial Woolly Bluecurls

wildflowers.hp: Late-season bee forage — important when few other annuals bloom.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers on stems with sandpaper-rough leaves that stick to clothing. Common annual of chaparral openings and rocky slopes. Much smaller than M. laevicaulis.
wildflowers.hp: Small-flowered blazing star — tiny yellow flowers on sticky stems. Common but overlooked in dry areas.

wildflowers.desc: Strange root parasite with no green leaves — entirely parasitic on chamise and other chaparral shrubs. Purple-brown flower spikes emerging from soil. No chlorophyll.

fm.Ecology: Root parasite — no chlorophyll; parasitizes chamise (Adenostoma)
fm.Habitat: Chaparral — on roots of host shrubs

wildflowers.hp: Chaparral broomrape — fully parasitic with no chlorophyll. Purple spikes emerge from underground. Parasitizes chaparral shrub roots.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink flowers erupting from sandy soil in early spring — flowers seem too large for the tiny plant. One of the earliest and showiest spring annuals. Sandy grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Ground pink — bright pink flowers flat to the ground. Sandy soils. Blooms Feb-Apr. Among the earliest spring wildflowers.

wildflowers.desc: Dense mats of tiny pink spiny-bracted flowers on dry slopes. Named for resemblance to Turkish rug patterns. Common but overlooked late-spring annual.
wildflowers.hp: Turkish rugging — dense mats of dried involucres persist, creating 'carpet' effect. Sandy openings in chaparral.

wildflowers.desc: Tall wiry stems with small white-pink flower clusters atop. Important late-season bee forage. Common on dry slopes and roadsides.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season pollinator resource — blooms Aug–Nov when few other plants flower. Visited by native bees, wasps, and butterflies including blues and hairstreaks.

wildflowers.desc: Low mounding buckwheat with cream-pink flower clusters on coastal bluffs. Small leaves. Important butterfly and bee plant at the coast.
wildflowers.hp: Host for Mormon Metalmark butterfly. Important coastal bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Weak-stemmed scrambling plant covered in tiny backward-pointing hooks that stick to everything. Whorled leaves. Extremely common in moist shade — the original velcro plant.

fm.Stems: Covered in tiny hooks — sticks to clothing

wildflowers.hp: Goose grass — clinging stems covered in tiny hooks. Velcro-like — sticks to clothing and fur. Edible young shoots.

wildflowers.desc: Small native carrot relative with lacy white umbels. Fruit covered in hooked spines — hitchhikes on socks. The native wild carrot of chaparral and sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract parasitoid wasps and other beneficial insects. Hooked seeds hitchhike on socks and fur.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with glossy divided leaves. Early spring bloomer on dry ridges. Less common than L. dasycarpum.
wildflowers.hp: Early spring nectar source for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate white umbel flowers with anise-scented roots. Shady oak woodland. Slender fruit with hooked tip. The anise smell when roots are crushed is diagnostic.

fm.Habitat: Shady oak woodland, yellow pine forest

wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract small native bees and beneficial insects in woodland shade. Anise-scented roots — diagnostic field mark.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with sharply toothed basal leaves. Open grassy and rocky slopes below 2,000 ft. Burr-like fruits.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects. Burr-like seeds dispersed by animals.

wildflowers.desc: Tall vivid red-orange paintbrush of mountain meadows and stream banks. Larger and more robust than foothill paintbrushes. Hemiparasitic. SGM above 5,000 ft.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots
fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, stream banks — above 5,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds at mountain elevations. Important high-altitude nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy clusters with distinctive triangular leaves. Mountain stream banks. One of the most common wildflowers along SGM streams above 6,000 ft.
wildflowers.hp: Arrow-leaf senecio — large triangular leaves. Mountain stream specialist. Visited by bumble bees at elevation.

wildflowers.desc: Rare lemon-yellow fragrant lily of mountain streams. Among SoCal's rarest and most beautiful wildflowers. Federally sensitive species. SGM canyons at 4,000–8,000 ft. Severely declined from collection and habitat loss.

fm.Conservation: Federally sensitive. Severely declined from collection.

wildflowers.hp: Lemon lily — CNPS 1B.2. Fragrant citrus-yellow flowers. Mountain stream banks above 4,000 ft. Rare and declining.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads with intense mint fragrance. Mountain trails in SGM. More common at higher elevations than M. villosa. Tea plant.

fm.Habitat: Mountain trails, openings — SGM above 5,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Southern mountain mint — compact purple heads on rocky mountain slopes. Important high-elevation bumble bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender tubular flowers — among the showiest SGM penstemons. Named for naturalist Joseph Grinnell. Rocky chaparral and pine forest openings.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes, pine forest openings — SGM

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and hummingbirds. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.
