wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red tubular flowers in elongated clusters. Hummingbird specialist. Mountain slopes and openings. One of the most vivid mountain wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant scarlet tubular flowers. One of the most vivid mountain wildflowers. Also visited by long-tongued hawk moths.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple nodding flower clusters. Native wild onion of grasslands and chaparral openings. Strong onion scent. Edible — used by Tongva.

fm.Cultural: Edible — used by Tongva

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Edible — Tongva food plant. Geophyte.

wildflowers.desc: Large pink-white flowers emerging from gravelly mountain soil — seemingly without leaves. Famous plant of Lewis and Clark expedition. At extreme southern range limit in SGM above 7,000 ft.

fm.Cultural: Lewis and Clark expedition plant. Montana state flower.
fm.Habitat: Gravelly mountain ridges — at extreme southern range limit
fm.Conservation: Very rare in LA County — southern range edge

wildflowers.hp: Bitterroot — large pink-white flowers from tiny succulent rosette. Montana state flower. Roots were important indigenous food — extremely bitter raw.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple mallow flowers on slender stems in grasslands and moist slopes. Variable — many subspecies. Attractive to native bees.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Host for Painted Lady butterfly. Variable — many subspecies.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate pink-white flower sprays from rocky crevices. Basal rosette of scalloped leaves. Rock specialist at mid to high elevations. Coral bells relative.
wildflowers.hp: Alumroot — coral bells relative with tiny pink flowers on wiry stems. Rock crevice specialist. Mountain cliffs.

wildflowers.desc: Bizarre inflated hollow stems topped with small purple flowers. Desert annual — classic Antelope Valley superbloom species. One of the strangest-looking wildflowers.

fm.Color: Purple flowers on swollen white/green stems

wildflowers.hp: Desert candle — dramatically inflated hollow stems. Mojave Desert annual. Stems edible when young — indigenous food plant.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub with pine-like needle leaves. Scarce — a single plant at Mt. Hollywood overlook may be only one in eastern SMM (Cooper 2013). Dry rocky slopes.

fm.Habitat: Dry rocky slopes — very scarce in SMM

wildflowers.hp: Pine goldenbush — needle-like leaves resembling pine. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Late-season nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Inconspicuous when flowering but transforms into dramatic silvery puffballs — like oversized dandelion globes. Spring grasslands. The puffball stage is the diagnostic field mark.
wildflowers.hp: Blow wives — papery-scaled heads open into fluffy silver spheres. Annual grassland specialist. Important bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers with backward-pointing leaf teeth. More common than A. grandiflora at lower elevations. Open grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Spear-leaved agoseris — dandelion-like heads with recurved basal leaves. Grassland and woodland specialist.

wildflowers.desc: True licorice-flavored roots. White pea flowers in spiky clusters. Moist low areas along streams. The actual licorice plant — sweet root used medicinally.

fm.Color: White-cream pea flowers in spiky clusters
fm.Cultural: Sweet licorice-flavored root — medicinal

wildflowers.hp: Wild licorice — roots contain glycyrrhizin (sweet compound). Traditional medicine. Burred seed pods cling to fur. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow sticky daisies with hairy stems. Grasslands and road banks. Similar to G. camporum but hairier. Important bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Hairy gumplant — sticky resinous heads. Distinguished from G. camporum by hairy stems. Coastal and woodland habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers on wiry subshrub. The only rock rose native to SoCal. Dry slopes and ridges in coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes and ridges, CSS, chaparral

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Drought-adapted groundcover.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue-violet hooded flowers close to ground. Tuberous roots. Dry openings in chaparral and forest. Often hidden among leaf litter.

fm.Habitat: Dry openings in chaparral, under oaks

wildflowers.hp: Skullcap — distinctive cap-shaped calyx. Tuberous roots. Woodland understory specialist. Small violet-blue flowers.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly mint-family flowers on tall stems in wet to seepy places. Taller and whiter than S. bullata. Mountain seeps and moist canyons.
wildflowers.hp: White hedge nettle — woolly stems with pale flowers. Distinguished from S. bullata by white (not pink) corollas and denser wool.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with strongly reflexed rays and prominent dome-shaped disk. Moist meadows and stream margins. Causes sneezing when pollen is inhaled — hence the name.

fm.Color: Yellow, reflexed rays with raised disk
fm.Ecology: Pollen causes sneezing

wildflowers.hp: Sneezeweed — reflexed yellow rays and raised disk. Mountain meadow specialist. Somewhat toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers that open in late afternoon only. Rabbit-ear-shaped leaves. Sticky and aromatic. Dry grasslands and openings. Easily overlooked.

fm.Color: Yellow, tiny — open in afternoon only

wildflowers.hp: Hareleaf — thread-thin annual with tiny yellow flowers. Extremely inconspicuous. Grassland specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny woolly annual of dry burns and disturbed areas. Among the smallest wildflowers — easy to overlook. Cotton-ball-like clusters at stem forks.
wildflowers.hp: California filago — tiny woolly annual. Among the most inconspicuous native wildflowers. Important in grassland soil ecology.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-pink flower heads with strong minty fragrance. Annual mint of rocky chaparral and oak woodland openings. Strong fragrance when stepped on.

fm.Fragrance: Strong mint when crushed or stepped on

wildflowers.hp: Mustang mint — lance-shaped leaves. Open grasslands and rocky slopes. Aromatic oils repel deer browsing.

wildflowers.desc: Flat-topped yellow flower clusters on tall stems in moist areas. Late summer bloomer. Often confused with Solidago but with flat-topped clusters.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season nectar source in moist habitats. Flat-topped clusters attract native bees and butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow wide-throated monkeyflower of desert washes. More compact than D. aurantiacus. Sandy areas at desert-chaparral transition.
wildflowers.hp: Wide-throat monkeyflower — cream-yellow flowers with wide open throat. Desert slopes. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Low subshrub of moist disturbed areas and stream terraces. Inconspicuous flowers. Important native groundcover for restoration in riparian zones.

fm.Ecology: Restoration groundcover for riparian zones

wildflowers.hp: Hayes' iva — low coastal perennial of salt marsh and beach margins. Wind-pollinated. Salt-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Trailing mint with sweet fragrance — the plant San Francisco was named for (Yerba Buena = "good herb"). Shady woodland. Makes excellent tea.

fm.Cultural: San Francisco was originally named "Yerba Buena" after this plant

wildflowers.hp: Yerba buena — the plant for which San Francisco was named. Creeping aromatic mint with white flowers. Makes excellent tea.

wildflowers.desc: Bright red tubular snapdragon flowers on scrambling shrub. Channel Islands endemic — planted on mainland for hummingbird gardens. Spectacular when in full bloom.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — tube length adapted to bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Federally endangered tiny daisy of clay grasslands. One of LA County's rarest plants. Known from fewer than 15 sites in LA and Ventura counties. Grasslands on clay soil.

fm.Habitat: Clay grasslands — fewer than 15 known sites
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered. CNPS 1B.1. Among LA County's rarest plants.

wildflowers.hp: Lyon's pentachaeta — FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. One of rarest Asteraceae in California. Few extant populations. Critical conservation priority.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender bell flowers on erect stems. Endemic to SoCal. Dry rocky slopes in chaparral openings. Less common than other Phacelia species but distinctive.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and mining bees. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Low salt-tolerant subshrub with tiny pink flowers. Salt marsh specialist — excretes salt crystals on leaf surfaces. Ballona Wetlands indicator species.

fm.Ecology: Salt-secreting leaves

wildflowers.hp: Alkali heath — salt-excreting leaves form white crust. Upper salt marsh zones. Important salt marsh structure.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-blue flower sprays on leathery-leaved salt marsh perennial. Important late-season salt marsh color when most other plants have finished. Dried flower arranging.
wildflowers.hp: Sea lavender — lavender flowers on coastal salt marshes. Dried flower clusters popular in arrangements. Salt-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Fleshy yellow-flowered succulent of salt marshes. Forms dense mats. One of the few native composites tolerant of full saltwater immersion.
wildflowers.hp: Marsh jaumea — succulent coastal salt marsh perennial. Important salt marsh habitat structure. Halophyte.

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow plume of desert roadsides. Selenium indicator — grows on selenium-rich soils. Large feathery compound leaves. Toxic to livestock.

fm.Ecology: Selenium indicator plant — grows on seleniferous soils

wildflowers.hp: Prince's plume — tall yellow spikes. Selenium indicator plant — presence signals seleniferous soils toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: Tall weed with small pale yellow dandelion-like flowers. Spiny-edged leaves align north-south ("compass plant"). Wild ancestor of cultivated lettuce. Extremely common on disturbed ground.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native prickly lettuce — ancestor of cultivated lettuce. Milky latex. Leaves orient north-south (compass plant).

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers on milky-juiced stems. European weed found on every patch of disturbed ground in LA. Edible — eaten as salad green in Mediterranean countries.

fm.Edible: Young leaves edible as salad — Mediterranean tradition

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Edible — young leaves edible as salad — mediterranean tradition.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers with extremely bristly prickly stems and leaves. Mediterranean weed common on roadsides and disturbed ground. Distinctive rough texture.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native bristly ox-tongue — rough-textured leaves with white pustules. Mediterranean origin. Common roadside weed.

wildflowers.desc: Dense clusters of tiny white fragrant flowers. Garden escape now on every coastal disturbed site. Extremely common as a weed but also planted intentionally. Honey-sweet scent.

fm.Habitat: Coastal disturbed areas, sidewalks — very common

wildflowers.hp: Non-native sweet alyssum — tiny fragrant white flowers. Mediterranean. One of the most common garden ground covers. Important for beneficial hoverflies.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white star-shaped flowers on weak trailing stems in shade. European weed found in every shady garden and trail edge. Edible — mild lettuce-like flavor.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Edible — mild — eaten as salad green.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow-green cone-shaped flowers with no petals. Smells strongly of pineapple when crushed. Common on compacted paths and trailheads. One of the most distinctive trail-edge weeds.
wildflowers.hp: Pineapple weed — crushed leaves smell strongly of pineapple. Trample-resistant. Native despite sometimes being listed as introduced.

wildflowers.desc: The familiar yellow puffball weed. European introduction now global. Edible — leaves, roots, and flowers all used. Among the most recognizable plants on Earth.

fm.Edible: Leaves, flowers, and roots all edible

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Edible — leaves, flowers, and roots all edible.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers with distinctive heart-shaped seed pods. Among the most common weeds worldwide. The heart-shaped pods are the diagnostic feature.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native shepherd's purse — heart-shaped seed pods. One of the world's most widespread weeds. Traditional herbal styptic.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow cruciform flowers on tall weed. Named for growing in London after the Great Fire of 1666. Extremely common LA winter weed — one of the first to appear after rain.

fm.Cultural: Named for colonizing London after Great Fire of 1666
fm.Habitat: Disturbed ground — among first winter weeds

wildflowers.hp: Non-native London rocket — named for its spread after the Great Fire of London (1666). Common urban weed on nitrogen-rich soils.

wildflowers.desc: Purple pea flowers on tall hairy stems in moist areas. Leathery trifoliate leaves. Stream banks and seeps. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

wildflowers.hp: Leather root — tough rhizomatous perennial of stream banks. Large purple flower spikes. Important riparian nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Glandular-dotted trifoliate leaves used as tea by indigenous peoples and early settlers. Small purple flowers. Shaded woodland.

fm.Cultural: Tea plant — used by indigenous peoples and settlers

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by native bees. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

wildflowers.desc: Broad basal leaf rosette with rat-tail flower spikes. Called "white man's footprint" by Native Americans as it followed European settlement. Medicinal — leaf poultice for insect stings.

fm.Cultural: "White man's footprint" — followed European settlement. Medicinal leaf poultice for stings.

wildflowers.hp: Non-native common plantain — major medicinal herb worldwide. Leaves poulticed on stings and cuts. Wind-pollinated spike.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual with shiny round seed pods. Very common spring annual in grasslands — easy to miss. Important food for mourning doves and sparrows.
wildflowers.hp: Shiny peppergrass — small native annual with peppery-tasting leaves. Grassland specialist. Seeds eaten by finches.

wildflowers.desc: Large white poppy flowers with yellow centers on spiny blue-green stems. All parts toxic — yellow sap. Beautiful but dangerous. Desert washes and disturbed areas.
wildflowers.hp: Prickly poppy — large white flowers with yellow center. Extremely spiny. Latex contains toxic alkaloids. Visited by large carpenter bees.

wildflowers.desc: Small greenish-white star flowers on leafless stalks. The overlooked native bulb — extremely common but ignored. Anagram of Allium (onion) — related but no onion smell.

fm.Ecology: Name is anagram of Allium — related but no onion scent
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, sage scrub openings — very common

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by small native bees. Name is anagram of Allium. Extremely common but overlooked geophyte.

wildflowers.desc: Slender purple flower spikes on tall branching stems. Common in moist disturbed areas and riparian edges. Important butterfly nectar plant.
wildflowers.hp: Important butterfly nectar — visited by Painted Lady, West Coast Lady, and Monarch.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny orange-red (rarely blue) flowers that close in overcast weather — the "poor man's weather glass." European weed common in disturbed areas and lawns. Mildly toxic.

fm.Cultural: "Poor man's weather glass" — flowers close when rain approaches

wildflowers.hp: Non-native scarlet pimpernel — tiny coral-red flowers closing in overcast weather ('poor man's weather glass'). Toxic to livestock. Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple clarkia with distinctive narrow petals. Less common than C. unguiculata. Shady chaparral openings in SGM foothills.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in shady foothill habitats. Less common than C. unguiculata.

wildflowers.desc: Small white clarkia of shady canyon understory. Self-pollinating — flowers barely open. One of the least showy but most common Clarkias.
wildflowers.hp: Self-pollinating — flowers barely open. Shade specialist requiring minimal pollinator visits.

wildflowers.desc: Wine-purple to magenta petals with a darker basal spot. Grasslands and open slopes. One of the most colorful spring annuals.

fm.Color: Wine-purple to magenta with dark basal spot

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Important grassland pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant sapphire-blue flowers on woolly stems. Small but intensely colored. Dry openings in chaparral and sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Sapphire woolly-star — brilliant blue flowers with woolly stems. Open rocky areas. Important native bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-blue tubular flowers on sticky branching stems. Common spring annual in chaparral openings. Sticky and aromatic.
wildflowers.hp: Purple false-gilia — small purple flowers on branching stems. Open grasslands and road cuts.

wildflowers.desc: Red tubular flowers in whorls on tall shrubby stems. Hummingbird specialist. SGM chaparral. Distinctive whorled leaf arrangement.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — tube length adapted to bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow five-petaled flowers on sticky glandular stems. Moist slopes and meadows. Rose family — strawberry-like leaves.
wildflowers.hp: Sticky cinquefoil — glandular-hairy stems with yellow five-petaled flowers. Mountain meadows and openings.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny pink flowers on thread-thin trailing stems. Very common but utterly invisible unless you look closely. Dry slopes in chaparral and sage scrub.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes — very common but easily overlooked

wildflowers.hp: Threadstem — delicate annual with winged bracts. Chaparral understory. Among the most inconspicuous native wildflowers.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual with leathery involucral bracts. Sandy disturbed areas. Among the smallest and most overlooked native wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Leather spineflower — leathery involucral bracts. Coastal sage scrub. Important for specialized native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow daisies on slender stems. Desert annual that appears in good rain years. Antelope Valley and SGM desert slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Bigelow's coreopsis — brilliant yellow desert annual forming carpet displays. Major spring superbloom species.

wildflowers.desc: Low sprawling annual with spiny bur-like fruits that stick to socks and dog fur. Dry stream bottoms and waste areas. Wind-pollinated hay fever plant.

fm.Fruit: Spiny burs — stick to clothing and fur

wildflowers.hp: Bur ragweed — spiny-fruited annual of disturbed sandy areas. Wind-pollinated pollen allergen.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow monkeyflower with many small flowers. Seeps and wet stream banks. More flowers but smaller than E. guttata.
wildflowers.hp: Floriferous monkeyflower — many-flowered yellow stems. Mountain stream banks. Distinguished by profuse flowering.

wildflowers.desc: Prostrate weed with devastating spine-covered seed pods that puncture bicycle tires and pierce shoes. European invasive. The most cursed weed on LA bike paths.

fm.Fruit: Spine-covered nutlets — puncture tires and skin
fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas, bike paths, vacant lots

wildflowers.hp: Desert specialist. Seeds eaten by harvester ants and small rodents.

wildflowers.desc: White flowers on glandular-hairy stems. Rose family relative of cinquefoils. Sandy openings in chaparral. Uncommon — declining with development.
wildflowers.hp: Coast horkelia — wedge-shaped leaflets. Coastal sage scrub and sandstone outcrops. Important native bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Huge herbaceous plant with massive compound leaves — dies to ground each winter. Ball-like white flower clusters. Moist shaded canyons. Looks tropical.

fm.Form: Dies to ground each winter, resprouts
fm.Habitat: Moist shaded canyons — requires permanent water

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and wasps. Provides habitat structure.

wildflowers.desc: Curious hemiparasitic annual with hooked "beak" flowers. Purple to cream. Dry granitic chaparral slopes. Root parasite on neighboring shrubs.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring plant roots

wildflowers.hp: Bird's beak — hemiparasitic. Distinctive beak-shaped flowers. Chaparral specialist. Seeds require host root contact to germinate.

wildflowers.desc: Blue lobelia of moist canyon walls. CNPS Rare Plant rank 1B.2. One of LA County's rarest wildflowers. Hangs from wet cliffs and seeps.

fm.Habitat: Moist canyon walls, wet cliffs, seeps
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.2 — rare. One of LA County's rarest wildflowers.

wildflowers.hp: Rothrock's lobelia — CNPS 1B.2. Rare blue-flowered perennial of mountain streams and seeps. Important hummingbird nectar source at elevation. Conservation priority.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-white star flowers in elegant racemes. TOXIC — all parts contain steroidal alkaloids. Grasslands and open chaparral. Also called "death camas" — fatalities documented.

fm.Toxicity: HIGHLY TOXIC — steroidal alkaloids in all parts. Historical fatalities.

wildflowers.hp: Star lily — creamy white star flowers with green nectary glands. TOXIC — all parts contain steroidal alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved bedstraw endemic to the San Gabriel Mountains. Chaparral and oak woodland. California endemic — limited range.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Provides ground cover in chaparral understory. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: White flowers on stems with large round basal leaves. Moist canyon walls and seeps. One of the prettiest saxifrage relatives.
wildflowers.hp: Round-leaved boykinia — large round basal leaves. Mountain stream margins. Important shade-loving wildflower.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers on hairy stems. Chaparral and pine forest openings. Common at mid-elevation in SGM.
wildflowers.hp: Hawkweed — white milky latex. Southern California endemic. Rocky slopes in chaparral.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny inconspicuous annual of moist shaded walls and rocks. Related to nettles but does not sting. Wind-pollinated — causes hay fever in some people. Also known as Rillita Pellitory.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Red Admiral and West Coast Lady butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny native plantain of grasslands. Slender upright spikes. Important larval food for the endangered Quino Checkerspot butterfly.
wildflowers.hp: Larval food for the endangered Quino Checkerspot butterfly (Euphydryas editha quino).

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers with small round seed pods on branching stems. Common trailside weed. Peppery-tasting — edible as trail nibble. Bird food.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — peppery taste — edible trail nibble.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow pincushion-shaped flower heads on desert annual. Key component of Antelope Valley superbloom. Chaparral openings and sandy areas.

fm.Habitat: Sandy areas, chaparral openings, desert grasslands

wildflowers.hp: Yellow pincushion — one of few Chaenactis with yellow (not white) flowers. Desert and grassland annual.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple daisy with many narrow rays. Common on dry chaparral slopes. Multiple subspecies. More robust than other fleabanes.
wildflowers.hp: Leafy fleabane — lavender rays on multi-headed perennial. Important late-season nectar source in chaparral.

wildflowers.desc: The original tumbleweed — breaks off at base when dry and rolls with wind, dispersing seeds. Russian immigrant that arrived in contaminated flax seed. Symbol of the American West.

fm.Origin: Russian — arrived in contaminated flax seed ca. 1870s

wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Large white flowers aging to pink, opening at dusk. Desert and sandy areas. Sphinx moth pollinated at night. Fragrant.

fm.Color: White aging to pink — open at dusk

wildflowers.hp: California evening primrose — large white flowers. Desert washes and sandy areas. Important hawkmoth forage in arid habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisies with needle-thin leaves. Key superbloom species — carpets fields with yellow alongside poppies. Antelope Valley spring display.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands — key Antelope Valley superbloom species

wildflowers.hp: Needle goldfields — thread-thin leaves with yellow daisy heads. Distinguished from L. californica by needle-like foliage.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow star flowers on grass-like leaves. Wet meadows and seeps. Less common than blue-eyed grass. Not actually a grass — iris family.
wildflowers.hp: Yellow-eyed grass — golden yellow flowers. Moist grasslands and vernal pool margins. Less common than blue S. bellum.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved coral bells endemic to Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. Pink-white flower sprays. Popular native garden plant.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies in moist canyon habitats. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Tall magenta-pink spikes — among the first plants to colonize burns and disturbed areas. Mountain species. Named because it's the first flower after fire.

fm.Ecology: First colonizer of burns — hence "fireweed"

wildflowers.hp: Fireweed — iconic post-fire colonizer forming purple-pink meadows. Seeds with silky plumes travel miles on wind. Edible young shoots.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple lupine of mountain meadows and forest openings. Perennial - blooms later than annual species. Important late-season bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Important late-season mountain bee forage. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved perennial lupine of mountain meadows and stream banks. Blue to lavender flowers. One of the more robust montane lupines. Moist areas.

fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, stream banks - moist areas

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated at mountain elevations. Nitrogen fixer. Moist mountain meadow specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Short annual lupine with brilliant blue flowers and white banner spot. Grasslands. Common superbloom species - forms blue carpets alongside poppies.

fm.Color: Brilliant blue with white banner spot
fm.Habitat: Grasslands - forms blue carpets in superbloom

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Classic superbloom species — forms blue carpets alongside poppies. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Variable annual lupine - flowers white, pink, yellow, or lavender. Small pods. Multiple varieties including the showy var. densiflorus. Grasslands and openings.

fm.Color: Variable - white, pink, yellow, or lavender

wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees. Nitrogen fixer. Variable flower color — multiple varieties. Grassland pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny hairy lupine of sandy desert grasslands. Flowers bicolored pink-purple and white. Among the smallest lupines - easy to miss. Sandy washes.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Among the smallest lupines — desert sand specialist. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Low mat-forming mountain lupine with blue flowers close to ground. Rocky alpine and subalpine areas. The lupine of SGM highest ridges.

fm.Habitat: Rocky alpine, subalpine - SGM highest ridges

wildflowers.hp: Visited by mountain bumble bees at alpine elevations. Mat-forming — stabilizes rocky alpine soils. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Low desert annual lupine with lilac flowers. Sandy flats in western Mojave. Named for botanist William Shockley who collected in the Mojave in the 1880s.

fm.Habitat: Sandy flats - western Mojave, Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Visited by small desert bees. Nitrogen fixer. Western Mojave specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue lupine with distinctive yellow spot on banner. Desert annual. Sandy washes and scrub. Smaller and darker than most desert lupines.

fm.Color: Deep blue with yellow banner spot

wildflowers.hp: Visited by desert native bees. Nitrogen fixer. Sandy wash specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Deep violet-purple bowl-shaped flowers — among the showiest annual Phacelias. Chaparral openings. Named for botanist Charles Parry.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees including bumble bees and mining bees. Fire follower providing post-fire pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender-blue coiled flower clusters with protruding stamens. Widely planted as bee forage cover crop. Native to SoCal but now cultivated globally for honeybees.

fm.Ecology: One of the best bee forage plants — widely cultivated for honeybees worldwide

wildflowers.hp: One of the best bee forage plants worldwide — widely cultivated commercially for honeybee pasture. Native to SoCal but now planted globally. Attracts 50+ bee species.

wildflowers.desc: Small white-lavender phacelia with short-lobed leaves. Shady chaparral canyons. Less showy than other species but common.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees in shady canyon habitats where few other annuals provide early-season nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Low mat-forming buckwheat with white-pink flowers aging to rusty red. Dry rocky slopes at mid-elevation. Important late-season pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Wright's buckwheat — woolly perennial with dense white-to-pink flower heads. Important late-season pollinator resource in arid habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual buckwheat with delicate wiry stems and small white-pink flower clusters. Common but easily overlooked. Dry slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Slender buckwheat — delicate annual with threadlike stems. Open areas in chaparral. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads on subshrub with distinctive white-veined leaf undersides. SGM endemic subspecies. Dry chaparral openings.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow to orange paintbrush of moist areas and stream edges. More delicate than other paintbrushes. Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring roots

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Visited by hummingbirds and native bees along stream edges.

wildflowers.desc: Massive spiny thistle with purple artichoke-like flower heads — the wild ancestor of the globe artichoke. Cal-IPC Moderate. Forms impenetrable stands on coastal bluffs and grasslands. Roots to 6 ft deep.

fm.Cultural: Wild ancestor of the globe artichoke
fm.Ecology: Forms impenetrable stands. Roots to 6 ft deep — very difficult to eradicate.

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Artichoke thistle — massive spiny rosettes and purple flowers displacing native grassland. Mediterranean. Extremely difficult to control.

wildflowers.desc: Spiny purple-flowered thistle with winged spiny stems. European weed common on disturbed ground. Distinguished from native thistles by continuous spiny wings running down the stems.

fm.Stems: Winged with continuous spines (diagnostic — native thistles lack this)

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Bull thistle — large spiny heads. Important goldfinch food. Purple flowers attract bumble bees despite invasive status.

wildflowers.desc: Tall perennial with dense white flower clusters. Cal-IPC High. Extremely aggressive in wetlands and riparian areas — spreads by root fragments. Nearly impossible to eradicate once established.

fm.Ecology: Spreads by root fragments. Nearly impossible to eradicate.

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC High. Perennial pepperweed — aggressive invader of wetlands and riparian areas. Dense white flower clusters. Deep roots make eradication very difficult.

wildflowers.desc: Pale yellow mustard devastating Mojave Desert spring wildflower displays. Cal-IPC High. Germinates earlier than natives, forming dense stands that shade out poppies and desert annuals. Rapidly expanding through Antelope Valley.

fm.Origin: North Africa/Middle East — Cal-IPC High. CDFA C-rated.
fm.Ecology: Germinates earlier than natives — shades out wildflower displays
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Antelope Valley — rapidly expanding

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Purple thistle with clusters of narrow flower heads atop winged spiny stems. Cal-IPC Moderate. One of the most hated invasive thistles in LA — forms impenetrable stands on roadsides, trailheads, and disturbed grasslands. Distinguishable from Bull Thistle by narrower clustered heads.

fm.Color: Purple, narrow heads in clusters of 2-5 (vs solitary in Bull Thistle)
fm.Habitat: Roadsides, trailheads, disturbed grasslands — very common
fm.vs: Bull Thistle (C. vulgare) has solitary larger heads; Italian has clustered narrow heads

wildflowers.hp: Non-native Italian thistle — clustered pink heads. Mediterranean origin. Less aggressive than bull thistle. Roadside colonizer.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly everlasting with felt-covered leaves and stems. Fall bloomer — flowers when most other wildflowers have finished. Dry chaparral slopes. Leaves gray-white below, greener above.

fm.vs: California Everlasting (P. californicum) blooms Jan-Jul and has broader, less woolly leaves with a two-tone appearance

wildflowers.hp: Feltleaf everlasting — gray woolly stems. Provides hummingbird nesting material. Higher elevation than P. californicum.

wildflowers.desc: Bicolored everlasting — leaves green above and white-woolly beneath, giving a striking two-tone effect. Dry slopes in chaparral. One of the more attractive everlastings.

fm.Leaves: Distinctly two-tone — GREEN above, WHITE-WOOLLY beneath (diagnostic)
fm.vs: Feltleaf (P. canescens) is woolly on BOTH surfaces; Two-tone has green upper surface

wildflowers.hp: Two-tone everlasting — bicolored (green above, white-woolly below) leaves. Important for native bee nesting material.

wildflowers.desc: Tall white-woolly everlasting with many tiny flower heads in dense clusters. The whitest and most conspicuous of the LA everlastings. Fall bloomer. Dry open chaparral.

fm.Color: Dense white clusters — many tiny heads
fm.vs: Feltleaf (P. canescens) similar but typically shorter; White Everlasting is taller with more dense flower clusters

wildflowers.hp: White everlasting — tiny flower heads in dense clusters. Chaparral and woodland. Provides hummingbird nesting material.

wildflowers.desc: Rose-pink tinged everlasting — the only pink-flowered Pseudognaphalium in LA County. Uncommon on dry slopes. The pink tinge is subtle and best seen in bud.

fm.Color: Rose-pink tinged flower clusters (unique among LA everlastings)
fm.vs: All other LA everlastings are white; Pink Everlasting has distinctive rose-pink tinge

wildflowers.hp: Pink everlasting — pinkish bracts. Distinguished from congeners by rosy color. Mountain habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellowish-white annual cudweed — European weed common in disturbed areas. Distinguished from native everlastings by annual habit and yellowish (not pure white) flower heads.

fm.Color: Yellowish-white heads (vs pure white in native species)
fm.vs: Native everlastings (Pseudognaphalium) are perennials with pure white heads; Jersey Cudweed is an annual with yellowish heads

wildflowers.hp: Non-native Jersey cudweed — yellowish woolly annual. European. Common garden and roadside weed.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple thistle-like flower heads on spiny subshrub. Dry chaparral slopes. Tongva name "sacapellote" (hair-puller) refers to the spiny involucral bracts that snag clothing and hair. Unusual sweet vanilla fragrance when fresh.

fm.Cultural: Tongva name "sacapellote" = hair-puller (spiny bracts snag clothing)

wildflowers.hp: Sacapellote — small pink-purple heads. Traditional medicinal plant. Chaparral understory. Ethnobotanically important.

wildflowers.desc: Small pink-purple four-petaled flowers with notched petals. Common along streams and moist areas. Produces cottony seeds that drift on wind. Closely related to fireweed. Distinguished from E. canum by small flowers and moist habitat.

fm.vs: California Fuchsia (E. canum) has large red tubular flowers and grows in DRY habitats; Willowherb has small pink flowers in MOIST habitats

wildflowers.hp: Fringed willowherb — small pink-purple flowers. Moist disturbed areas. Important early colonizer of newly created wetlands.

wildflowers.desc: Extremely rare pitcher sage endemic to the western Transverse Ranges — described new to science in 2006. Known from only four populations: two in the Liebre Mountains (LA County) east of Castaic Lake. Fewer than 1,000 plants total.

fm.Color: White to purplish, bell-shaped in racemes
fm.Leaves: Toothed oval blades to 13 cm, hairy and glandular
fm.Inflorescence: Axis bent at 60-90° angles to stem (diagnostic — unique in genus)
fm.Bracts: Large, leaf-like, equaling or exceeding flowers (diagnostic)
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.1 — extremely rare. Only ~4 populations known worldwide.
fm.vs: Fragrant Pitcher Sage (L. fragrans) has straight inflorescence axes and smaller bracts much shorter than flowers. Ross' has geniculate (kinked) axes.

wildflowers.hp: Ross' pitcher sage — CNPS 1B.1. Known from few populations in Santa Monica Mountains. Conservation priority. Hummingbird-pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: Perennial goosefoot with fleshy triangular leaves and a large starchy root used by Tongva and Chumash as soap. Not related to Chlorogalum despite sharing the "soap plant" name. Moist shady slopes.

fm.Leaves: Fleshy, triangular to diamond-shaped, dark green
fm.Root: Large, starchy tuber — crushed for saponin-rich lather
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Chumash soap plant — root crushed for cleaning lather
fm.Habitat: Moist shady slopes, oak woodland understory
fm.vs: Wavyleaf Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) is a lily relative with wavy strap leaves and white flowers — completely unrelated despite shared common name

wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. small melilot — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native sweet clover — common roadside plant. Nitrogen fixer. When improperly cured as hay, produces dicoumarol (blood thinner). Honeybee forage plant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. white clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native white clover — ubiquitous lawn plant. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Important honeybee and native bee forage. Edible flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive non-native. bur clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Non-native bur clover with distinctive spiral seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Common pasture plant but displaces native annuals in grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. White Sweetclover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native white sweet clover. Tall biennial nitrogen fixer. Important honeybee forage. Contains coumarin — precursor to the blood thinner warfarin when fermented.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. black nightshade — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native black nightshade with small white flowers and black berries. Berries toxic when green, edible when ripe in some traditions. Common garden weed worldwide.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. hairy vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native hairy vetch — nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides. Purple flower clusters. Important honeybee forage.

wildflowers.desc: silver bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native silver lotus with silvery-hairy leaves. Important nitrogen fixer in coastal sage scrub. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Spanish clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Spanish clover — widespread annual nitrogen fixer in grasslands and open areas. Host for several blue butterfly larvae. Important bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Black Medick — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native black medick — tiny yellow clover of lawns and disturbed ground. Nitrogen fixer. One of the most common lawn 'weeds' worldwide.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. oceanblue morning glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native oceanblue morning glory — vigorous vine smothering native vegetation in riparian areas and coastal bluffs. Brilliant blue-purple flowers fade to pink. Spreads by rooting stems.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. needle-leaved broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native flax-leaved broom naturalizing on hillsides and road cuts. Nitrogen fixer. Forms dense thickets excluding native shrubs. Related to Cal-IPC High-rated scotch broom.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. western coastal wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native western coastal wattle from Australia. Red arils on seeds dispersed by birds. Nitrogen fixer. Forming dense thickets in some coastal areas.

wildflowers.desc: strigose lotus — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native strigose lotus of dry open areas. Important early-season nitrogen fixer. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Rose Clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native rose clover from Mediterranean — planted for forage but naturalizing in grasslands. Distinctive pink flower heads.

wildflowers.desc: Longleaf Wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Sydney golden wattle — phyllode-leaved acacia naturalizing in coastal areas. Nitrogen fixer. Dense growth excludes native species.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Cootamundra wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Cootamundra wattle — spectacularly showy golden flowers in winter. Widely planted ornamental. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing in some disturbed areas.

wildflowers.desc: Braunton's milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Braunton's milkvetch — FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. Known from fewer than 20 populations. Fire follower dependent on recently burned chaparral. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. common morning-glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native common morning glory — annual vine with showy purple trumpet flowers. Seeds contain LSA (ergine). Common garden escape.

wildflowers.desc: tomcat clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native tomcat clover — important early-season nitrogen fixer and bee forage in California grasslands. Distinctive inflated calyx.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Mexican palo verde — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Mexican palo verde with distinctive green bark (photosynthetic). Thorny. Naturalized along desert washes. Seeds eaten by rodents and birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. vanilla-scented wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native vanilla-scented wattle — low-growing ground cover acacia widely planted in LA landscapes. Nitrogen fixer. Relatively low invasion risk compared to tree acacias.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Common Vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native common vetch — agricultural nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides. Purple-pink flowers. Important honeybee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. tomato — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native cultivated tomato — occasionally persisting in compost piles and disturbed areas. Minimal ecological impact. Important agricultural crop.

wildflowers.desc: island broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native island broom — Channel Islands endemic that has spread to mainland coastal sage scrub. Shrubby nitrogen fixer. Important pollinator plant.

wildflowers.desc: coastal bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native coastal lotus of sandy and rocky coastal habitats. Important nitrogen fixer in coastal strand. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Peanut-Butter Cassia — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native peanut-butter cassia from tropical Africa — distinctive peanut butter scent when leaves crushed. Showy yellow flower spikes. Spreading in disturbed areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Strawberry clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native strawberry clover — distinctive inflated fruiting heads resemble tiny strawberries. Salt-tolerant. Used in saline soil reclamation.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Alfalfa — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native alfalfa — world's most important forage crop. Deep-rooted nitrogen fixer. Important commercial honeybee forage. Escaping to roadsides and river terraces.

wildflowers.desc: Douglas' milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Douglas' milkvetch of mountain chaparral margins. Important nitrogen fixer. Inflated seed pods. Several varieties with different habitat preferences.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. bugweed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native bugweed from South America. Large fuzzy leaves. Purple flowers and yellow berries spread by birds. Colonizing disturbed riparian areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Bladder-senna — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native bladder-senna with inflated papery seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing on disturbed slopes. Mildly toxic seeds.

wildflowers.desc: Indian tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Indian tobacco — culturally significant to Tongva and Chumash peoples for ceremonial use. Small white tubular flowers visited by hawkmoths at night.

wildflowers.desc: Canyon Dodder — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native canyon dodder — completely parasitic with no chlorophyll. Orange-thread stems. Often parasitizes native shrubs. Part of the natural ecosystem despite parasitic lifestyle.

wildflowers.desc: chaparral bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native chaparral lotus with large showy yellow-and-red flowers — the showiest native Acmispon. Important chaparral pollinator plant. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: American black nightshade — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native American black nightshade — distinguished from introduced S. nigrum by glossy (not dull) berries. Common in moist disturbed areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. silver senna — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native silver senna from Australia — gray fern-like foliage with yellow flowers. Common in LA landscaping. Drought-tolerant. Naturalizing from plantings.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. silverleaf nightshade — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native silverleaf nightshade — extremely difficult to eradicate due to deep root system. Toxic berries. Spiny stems. Serious agricultural weed in desert regions.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Silver wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native silver wattle — showy golden flower sprays in winter. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing on hillsides. Important early-season honeybee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. arabian pea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Arabian pea — crushed leaves smell strongly of asphalt (bitumen). Mediterranean origin. Nitrogen fixer. Spreading on disturbed slopes.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. yellow bird-of-paradise shrub — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native yellow bird-of-paradise — showy yellow-and-red flowers with long stamens. South American. Mildly toxic. Common ornamental naturalizing in washes.

wildflowers.desc: Peirson's morning-glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Peirson's morning-glory — SoCal endemic vine with large white-pink funnel flowers. Host for Painted Lady butterfly. Chaparral edges.

wildflowers.desc: San Miguel Island milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native San Miguel Island milkvetch — Channel Islands endemic. Inflated seed pods. Important nitrogen fixer in island ecosystems. Conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Asian ponysfoot — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Asian ponysfoot — small round-leaved ground cover used as lawn substitute. Low ecological impact. Shade-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Big deervetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native big deervetch of mountain meadows and forest openings. Yellow flowers visited by native bees. Important mountain nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. buffalo-bur — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native buffalo-bur with extremely spiny stems and fruit. Ancestral host of Colorado Potato Beetle. Toxic solanine alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. broad-leaved sweet pea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native broad-leaved sweet pea — vigorous perennial vine with showy pink flowers. Escaping from gardens and smothering native vegetation on hillsides.

wildflowers.desc: San Clemente Island milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native San Clemente Island milkvetch — Channel Islands endemic. Important nitrogen fixer. Conservation concern — restricted island distribution.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. lanceleaf nightshade — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native lanceleaf nightshade naturalizing in disturbed areas. Purple flowers. Toxic berries. Spreading in southern LA County.

wildflowers.desc: Peach Thorn — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native peach thorn — important desert shrub with fleshy orange-red berries eaten by birds. Provides nesting structure for desert songbirds. Thorny.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Sweet pea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native sweet pea — iconic garden flower occasionally persisting on road cuts and disturbed slopes. All parts mildly toxic. Many cultivated varieties.

wildflowers.desc: tall silky lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native tall silky lupine of mountain meadows. Showy blue-purple flower spikes. Important bumble bee forage at elevation. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. blackwood — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native blackwood acacia — one of the most invasive acacias in California. Dense shade and leaf litter suppress native seedlings. Hard durable wood.

wildflowers.desc: Guadalupe Island Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Guadalupe Island lupine — rare lupine associated with islands and coastal bluffs. Blue-purple flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Limited distribution.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. potato — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native potato — cultivated tuber crop occasionally volunteering from gardens. Minimal ecological impact.

wildflowers.desc: streambank bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native streambank lotus — moist meadow and riparian specialist. Yellow flowers. Important riparian nitrogen fixer. Stream bank stabilizer.

wildflowers.desc: Baja desert-thorn — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Baja desert-thorn — spiny shrub with fleshy red berries important for desert birds. At northern range limit in southern LA County.

wildflowers.desc: Tree Clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native tree clover — tall annual clover of grasslands and oak woodlands. Important bee forage. Seeds eaten by sparrows and quail.

wildflowers.desc: Coyote Tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native coyote tobacco — fire follower germinating abundantly after burns. Produces nicotine as herbivore defense. Night-blooming — visited by hawkmoths.

wildflowers.desc: southern island clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native southern island clover of Channel Islands and coastal areas. Important nitrogen fixer. Limited mainland distribution.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Persian silk tree — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Persian silk tree — showy pink powder-puff flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing in riparian areas. Compound leaves fold at night.

wildflowers.desc: Freckled Milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native freckled milkvetch — extremely variable species with 40+ varieties across western US. Some varieties accumulate toxic selenium from soil.

wildflowers.desc: Pacific goldenthread — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Pacific dodder — golden-thread parasite on coastal shrubs, especially pickleweed and saltbush. Important component of salt marsh ecology.

wildflowers.desc: pinpoint clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native pinpoint clover — slender annual clover of grasslands and serpentine soils. Tiny but ecologically important nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: clammy clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native clammy clover — sticky-glandular clover of moist grasslands. Important nitrogen fixer. Declining.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. golden wreath wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native golden wreath wattle — aggressive Australian acacia naturalizing in coastal areas. Dense growth displaces native coastal sage scrub.

wildflowers.desc: Peirson's Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Peirson's lupine — desert specialist with yellow flowers. Mojave Desert washes. Nitrogen fixer. Important early-season desert bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: San Diego bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native San Diego lotus — small annual of coastal sage scrub. Important nitrogen fixer. Subtle but ecologically significant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Tangier Pea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Tangier pea — showy annual sweet pea with crimson flowers. Mediterranean. Escaping from gardens to road cuts.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Queensland silver wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Queensland silver wattle — showy golden flower balls on silvery foliage. Widely planted ornamental. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Heermann's Lotus — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Heermann's lotus — woolly-leaved annual of sandy and rocky habitats. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by small native bees.

wildflowers.desc: American vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native American vetch — climbing vine of mountain meadows with purple flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Important bee forage. Seeds eaten by quail.

wildflowers.desc: cowbag clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native cowbag clover — small annual clover of grasslands. Inflated fruiting heads. Important nitrogen fixer in native grassland ecology.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native bird's-foot trefoil — European nitrogen fixer planted for forage. Yellow flowers. Cyanogenic — produces hydrogen cyanide when damaged.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. shoestring acacia — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native shoestring acacia — long pendulous phyllodes. Australian. Common LA street tree. Drought-tolerant. Relatively low invasion risk.

wildflowers.desc: Rancheria clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native rancheria clover — annual clover of grasslands with distinctive bicolored flowers. Important nitrogen fixer and bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Payne's Bush Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Payne's bush lupine — introduced ornamental lupine. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing in some coastal areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Large-flowered Tomatillo — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native tomatillo — cultivated vegetable occasionally persisting near gardens. Purple-veined flowers. Green husked fruit.

wildflowers.desc: Western morning glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native western morning glory — twining vine with white-pink funnel flowers. Distinguished from non-native bindweed by larger flowers and sagittate leaves.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Knife-leaf Wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native knife-leaf wattle — distinctive triangular phyllodes. Australian ornamental. Nitrogen fixer. Limited invasion potential.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Cape gooseberry — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Cape gooseberry — edible orange fruit in papery husk. Occasional garden escape. Minimal ecological impact.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Cock's-eggs — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native cock's-eggs — creeping vine with small white bell flowers and white berry fruit. South American. Naturalizing in shaded urban areas.

wildflowers.desc: small-headed clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native small-headed clover — diminutive annual clover of moist grasslands. Nitrogen fixer. Important for maintaining native grassland soil fertility.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. fava bean — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native fava bean — ancient crop occasionally persisting near gardens. Important nitrogen fixer. Host for bean aphid. Causes favism in G6PD-deficient individuals.

wildflowers.desc: crested milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native crested milkvetch — San Gabriel and San Bernardino Mountains endemic. Inflated pods. Important montane nitrogen fixer. Conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Sierra Lotus — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Sierra lotus — mat-forming perennial of mountain openings. Yellow flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Important revegetation species.

wildflowers.desc: two-seeded milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native two-seeded milkvetch — small annual of grasslands. Inconspicuous but important nitrogen fixer in native grassland communities.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. White leadtree — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native white leadtree — fast-growing nitrogen-fixing tree from tropical America. Highly invasive in tropical regions. Contains toxic mimosine.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Silver Wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native silver wattle — Australian acacia with continuous golden bloom. Naturalizing in some areas.

wildflowers.desc: woollypod milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native woollypod milkvetch — woolly-hairy inflated pods. Mountain slopes. Important nitrogen fixer. Several varieties across western US.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Blue Rock Bindweed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native blue rock bindweed — trailing vine with blue flowers. Mediterranean garden escape on walls and slopes.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. reddish tufted vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native reddish tufted vetch — showy purple-red flower clusters. Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer. Roadside colonizer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Plume Albizia — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native plume albizia — feathery cream flower spikes. Australian nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing in riparian areas.

wildflowers.desc: Field Dodder — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native field dodder — yellow-thread parasite of agricultural crops. Economically important crop pest. Part of natural ecosystems on native hosts.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Mile-a-minute vine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Cairo morning glory — vigorous vine smothering native vegetation. Palmately lobed leaves. Difficult to control once established.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Yellow Sweetclover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native yellow sweetclover — tall biennial nitrogen fixer. Important honeybee forage. Contains coumarin. Roadside plant.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet Milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native scarlet milkvetch — brilliant red flowers. Mountain rock crevice specialist above 5,000 ft. One of the showiest native Astragalus.

wildflowers.desc: Big Bear Valley woollypod — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Big Bear Valley woollypod — San Bernardino Mountains endemic. Woolly inflated pods. Conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Paiute false bindweed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Paiute false bindweed — white funnel flowers. Desert-mountain transition. Distinguished from invasive field bindweed by larger flowers.

wildflowers.desc: carpet clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native carpet clover — tiny mat-forming mountain clover. Important nitrogen fixer in alpine meadows.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. manyflower tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native manyflower tobacco — small tubular flowers. South American. Occasional garden escape.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Woodland Tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native woodland tobacco — tall with fragrant white trumpet flowers attracting hawkmoths at night. Argentine. Garden plant.

wildflowers.desc: Chile trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Chile lotus — small annual nitrogen fixer of grasslands. Important bee forage. Formerly Lotus wrangelianus.

wildflowers.desc: false indigo bush — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native false indigo bush — purple-blue flower spikes. Riparian specialist. Nitrogen fixer. Important for stream restoration.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. black wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native black wattle — one of the world's worst invasive trees in some regions. Nitrogen fixer. Dense growth suppresses native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Sweet acacia — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native sweet acacia — fragrant golden ball flowers. Tropical American. Thorny. Common in SoCal landscaping. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Tara — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native tara — Andean tree with red pods yielding tannins and gums. Rare in LA gardens.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. night jessamine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native night-blooming jessamine — intensely fragrant white flowers at night. Tropical American. Toxic berries. Popular LA garden plant.

wildflowers.desc: small-flowered morning-glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native small-flowered morning glory — tiny white flowers. Grassland and chaparral openings. Native alternative to invasive bindweed.

wildflowers.desc: Sierra False Bindweed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native woolly morning-glory — soft hairy leaves with white funnel flowers. Chaparral edges and dry slopes.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Red Clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native red clover — familiar garden clover with pink-red flower heads. European. Important honeybee forage. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Lesser hop trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native lesser hop clover — tiny yellow flower heads. European. Common lawn weed. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: slender vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Louisiana vetch — delicate climbing vine of moist grasslands and woodlands. Nitrogen fixer. Important spring bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Harlequin Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native harlequin lupine — bicolored yellow-and-pink flowers. Uncommon foothill species. Nitrogen fixer. Bumble bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Christmas Senna — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native climbing senna — yellow flowers on sprawling shrub. South American. Occasional garden escape.

wildflowers.desc: Gambel's milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Gambel's milkvetch — small annual of grasslands and coastal sage scrub. Nitrogen fixer. Inconspicuous but ecologically important.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Shrubby Scorpion-vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native scorpion vetch — yellow flowers. Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer. Garden plant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. seaside petunia — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native miniature petunia — tiny flowers. South American. Common bedding plant. Minimal naturalization.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. African boxthorn — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native African boxthorn — extremely thorny. Bird-dispersed berries. Invasive in some coastal areas. Dense impenetrable thickets.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. scarlet powder-puff — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native powder-puff tree — dramatic red pompom flowers. Tropical American. Popular LA ornamental. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: double-head clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Macrae's clover — annual clover of moist grasslands. Important nitrogen fixer in native grassland communities.

wildflowers.desc: Indian rushpea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Indian rushpea — small yellow-orange flowers. Alkaline soil specialist. Important nitrogen fixer in desert grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. rabbitfoot clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native rabbit-foot clover — fuzzy pink cylindrical flower heads. European. Dry sandy habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Ice-cream-bean — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native ice cream bean — tropical legume tree with edible cotton-like seed pulp. Very frost-sensitive. Rare in LA.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Mexican holdback — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Mexican bird-of-paradise — red-and-yellow flowers. Similar to E. gilliesii but with red stamens.

wildflowers.desc: white-tipped clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native whitetip clover — distinctive white-tipped pink flowers. Moist grasslands. Important nitrogen fixer and bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Spider Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Bentham's lupine — slender annual of mountain meadows. Small blue flowers. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Turkey Berry — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native turkey berry — prickly tropical shrub with white flowers and yellow berries. Southeast Asian vegetable. Rare escape in LA.

wildflowers.desc: lucky morning glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native lucky morning-glory — rare SoCal endemic. Large white funnel flowers. Known from few localities. Conservation priority.

wildflowers.desc: California goldenbanner — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native California false lupine — yellow lupine-like flowers. Important mountain nitrogen fixer. Toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: blue palo verde — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native blue palo verde — stunning blue-green bark tree of desert washes. Photosynthetic bark. Important hummingbird nectar. Indigenous food.

wildflowers.desc: Silky California Broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native silky lotus — prostrate annual of dry rocky habitats. Nitrogen fixer. Small yellow flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Thickpod Milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native thick-podded milkvetch — low shrubby milkvetch of chaparral openings. Thick inflated pods. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. arrowleaf clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native arrowleaf clover — large white flower heads. European forage crop. Nitrogen fixer. Occasional volunteer from agricultural plantings.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. purple crownvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native crown vetch — pink-white flowers. European nitrogen fixer planted for erosion control. Can smother native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Parish's nightshade — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Parish's nightshade — endemic to SoCal mountains. Purple flowers. Important mountain pollinator plant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Dwarf Morning-glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native dwarf morning glory — tricolored blue-white-yellow flowers. Mediterranean. Annual. Garden flower.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Jerusalem cherry — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Jerusalem cherry — ornamental with toxic orange berries resembling cherry tomatoes. TOXIC to children and pets.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. blue potatobush — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native blue potato bush — blue-purple flowers. South American. Very common LA garden shrub. Attracts butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. chili pepper — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native pepper — cultivated vegetable occasionally volunteering. Includes bell peppers, chilis. Minimal ecological impact.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. apple-of-Peru — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native shoo-fly plant — pale blue flowers. Peruvian. Garden plant said to repel whiteflies. Occasional escape.

wildflowers.desc: Sharpleaf groundcherry — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native sharpleaf ground cherry — papery-husked fruits. Desert washes. Edible when fully ripe.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Common Pea — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native garden pea — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering. Nitrogen fixer. Minimal ecological impact.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Spotted medick — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native spotted medick — heart-shaped dark spot on leaflets diagnostic. Mediterranean nitrogen fixer. Common lawn weed.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. woolly clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native woolly clover — fuzzy inflated flower heads. Mediterranean. Sandy coastal areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Golden Wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native golden wattle — Australia's national flower. Showy golden balls. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. narrow-leaf bird's-foot trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native narrow-leaved trefoil — slender yellow-flowered clover relative. European. Moist disturbed areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Reversed clover — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native reversed clover — flowers face downward (unique among clovers). Mediterranean.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Spiny Broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native spiny broom — thorny Mediterranean shrub. Yellow pea flowers. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: giant devil's-fig — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native giant devil's fig — large fuzzy-leaved tropical nightshade. Occasionally persisting from cultivation.

wildflowers.desc: marriage vine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Costa Rican nightshade — spectacular lilac flower clusters on climbing vine. Tropical. Frost-sensitive.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Dwarf Tamarillo — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native dwarf tamarillo — small shrubby nightshade. South American. Rare garden plant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. violet tubeflower — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native mini-brugmansia — purple tubular flowers. South American. Garden plant. Hummingbird-visited.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. porcupine tomato — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native porcupine tomato — dramatic orange spines covering stems and leaves. Madagascar. Rare curiosity plant.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive non-native. Scotch Broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC High. Scotch broom — one of California's worst invasive shrubs. Yellow pea flowers. Explosive seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Very difficult to eradicate.

wildflowers.desc: foothill deervetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native short-pod lotus — small annual of grasslands. Nitrogen fixer. Important for native soil fertility.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. hop trefoil — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native hop clover — golden-yellow flower heads. European. Common dry grassland weed.

wildflowers.desc: Bajada Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Agardh's lupine — slender mountain lupine. Blue-purple flowers. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Peanut — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native peanut — occasionally volunteering near agricultural areas. Nitrogen fixer. Underground fruit development.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Kangaroo thorn — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native kangaroo thorn — thorny Australian acacia. Nitrogen fixer. Limited naturalization in LA.

wildflowers.desc: basalt milkvetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native threadstalk milkvetch — pendant threadlike fruit stalks. Desert-mountain transition. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: San Jacinto Lupine — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native San Jacinto lupine — blue-purple mountain lupine. Nitrogen fixer. Important bumble bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: California Indian breadroot — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native California Indian breadroot — tuberous root important indigenous food plant. Prairie-like habitats. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Sweet broom — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Madeira broom — yellow flowers on arching stems. Canary Islands. Nitrogen fixer. Related to invasive scotch broom.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Argentine Senna — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Argentine senna — bright yellow flowers. South American. Common LA ornamental. Butterfly nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Blackbean — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native Moreton Bay chestnut — Australian rainforest tree. Large seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Rare in LA.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Flinders Range Wattle — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native willow-leaved acacia — weeping form. Australian. Drought-tolerant ornamental. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Woolly Pod Vetch — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native woolly-pod vetch — large purple flowers. Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer. Occasional roadside plant.

wildflowers.desc: honey mesquite — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native western honey mesquite — recently split from Prosopis. Desert tree with edible pods. Indigenous food plant. Nitrogen fixer.

wildflowers.desc: Pacific False Bindweed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Pacific false bindweed — white-pink funnel flowers. Coastal and woodland edges. Important native bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. green cestrum — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native green cestrum — yellow-green tubular flowers. Chilean. Toxic — all parts. Escaping to riparian areas.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. ivy-leaved morning-glory — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native ivy-leaved morning glory — annual vine with blue trumpet flowers. Ivy-shaped leaves diagnostic. Agricultural weed.

wildflowers.desc: orange cestrum — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native orange cestrum — orange tubular flowers. Central American. Hummingbird-visited. Garden escape.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. kidney weed — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native kidney weed — tiny round-leaved ground cover. Australian. Used as lawn substitute. Low ecological impact.
