wildflowers.desc: Non-native. jasmine tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native jasmine tobacco — fragrant white trumpet flowers opening at night. South American. Important hawkmoth pollinator.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. Cultivated tobacco — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native cultivated tobacco — large pink tubular flowers. Tropical American. Occasional volunteer. Contains nicotine.

wildflowers.desc: white bog orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native white bog orchid — fragrant white flower spikes in mountain meadows. One of few native orchids in LA County. Mycorrhizal dependent.

wildflowers.desc: Cooper's Rein Orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Cooper's rein orchid — tall green flower spikes in montane chaparral. Important for native bee pollination. Mycorrhizal.

wildflowers.desc: sparse-flowered bog orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native sparse-flowered bog orchid — green flowers in moist mountain meadows. Mycorrhizal. At southern range limit.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native. centipede grass orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Non-native centipede grass orchid — tiny terrestrial orchid from Asia. Naturalizing in irrigated lawns and parks. One of few naturalized orchids.

wildflowers.desc: Denseflower Rein Orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native denseflower rein orchid — green flower spikes in mountain forest. Mycorrhizal dependent. Uncommon.

wildflowers.desc: Alaska Rein Orchid — a cactus or succulent found in LA County.
wildflowers.hp: Native Alaska rein orchid — at extreme southern range limit in SGM. Green flowers. Important mycorrhizal relationship.

trees.desc: LA's signature tree. Evergreen with holly-like convex leaves. Massive spreading canopy. Foundation species supporting 300+ insect species.

fm.Ecology: Foundation species — 300+ insect species
fm.Fire: Resprouter — resprouts vigorously from root crown after fire

trees.hp: Supports 300+ insect species including California Sister butterfly, many moth species, and acorn woodpeckers.

trees.desc: Largest North American oak. Deciduous with deeply lobed leaves. Majestic spreading form. Once dominant in LA's valley floors — now mostly lost to development.

fm.Conservation: Historically dominant — now rare in LA basin
fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark provides fire resistance

trees.hp: Acorns were the primary food of California's indigenous peoples — ground into flour. Supports hundreds of insect species. Once dominant on LA's valley floors, now largely lost to development.

trees.desc: Dense shrubby oak of chaparral. Evergreen with small spiny leaves. Often forms impenetrable thickets. Fire-resprouting.

fm.Leaves: Spiny-toothed, convex, dull green. Interior species — found miles from coast.
fm.vs: Nuttall Scrub Oak (Q. dumosa) is restricted to within a few miles of the coast, has flatter leaves, and is CNPS 1B.1 rare

trees.hp: Crown-sprouter after fire — resprouts vigorously from underground burl. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker and Western Scrub-Jay. Common chaparral component.

trees.desc: Evergreen oak of inland foothills. Flat leaves (vs convex in Coast Live Oak). Often at slightly higher elevations.

fm.Leaves: Evergreen, flat, spiny or smooth margin

trees.hp: Acorns eaten by woodpeckers, jays, and Band-tailed Pigeon. Supports hundreds of insect species. Fire-adapted resprouter.

trees.desc: Gold-scaled acorn cups diagnostic. Evergreen. Higher elevation than Coast Live Oak. Fire-resistant thick bark.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark + root crown sprouting
fm.Bark: Gray, scaly to furrowed — thick, fire-resistant

trees.hp: Gold-scaled acorn cups diagnostic. Thick fire-resistant bark. Acorns eaten by Band-tailed Pigeon, woodpeckers, and bears.

trees.desc: Deciduous oak of mountain elevations. Deeply lobed leaves turn gold-brown in fall. Important acorn source for wildlife and indigenous peoples.

fm.Fall: Golden-brown — best fall color of LA oaks
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown and epicormic sprouting

trees.hp: Acorns were most important food source for mountain-dwelling indigenous peoples. Golden fall color — best of LA oaks. Fire-adapted resprouter.

trees.desc: Massive white-barked riparian tree. Mottled bark peels in patches. Leaning, multi-trunked form. Defines LA's canyon bottoms.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark, resprouts from base

trees.hp: Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail butterfly. Cavity-nesting habitat for owls, woodpeckers, and Wood Ducks. Massive white bark — defines LA canyon bottoms.

trees.desc: Endemic Southern California tree. Small spreading form with pinnate leaves. Threatened by urbanization and hybridization with English Walnut.
trees.hp: SoCal endemic threatened by urbanization and hybridization with cultivated English Walnut. Walnuts eaten by squirrels, woodpeckers, and jays.

trees.desc: Large deciduous riparian tree. Heart-shaped leaves flutter in any breeze. Cotton-like seeds in spring. Fast-growing but short-lived.
trees.hp: Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak, and Lorquin's Admiral butterfly larvae. Cotton-like seeds in spring. Critical riparian cavity-nesting habitat.

trees.desc: Most common native willow in LA. Multi-stemmed riparian tree. Important caterpillar host plant and streambank stabilizer.

fm.Ecology: Host plant for many moth and butterfly species
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root and stem sprouting

trees.hp: Host for Lorquin's Admiral, Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak butterflies and many moth species.

trees.desc: Tall single-trunked willow with reddish twigs. Larger and more tree-like than Arroyo Willow. Riparian corridors.
trees.hp: Red willow — red-tinged new bark. Important Lorquin's Admiral and Mourning Cloak host. Tallest native willow in LA County.

trees.desc: Aromatic evergreen with pungently fragrant leaves. Crushing leaves releases camphor-like scent. Can cause headaches. Canyon understory tree.
trees.hp: Crushed leaves release camphor-like scent that can cause headaches. Host for California Sister and other moth species. Olive-like drupes eaten by birds.

trees.desc: "Christmas Berry" — bright red berries in winter gave Hollywood its name. Evergreen shrub-tree. Important winter bird food.

fm.Cultural: Hollywood named for Toyon berries

trees.hp: Red berries critical winter food for American Robin, Cedar Waxwing, mockingbirds, and Hermit Thrush. Summer-blooming white flowers visited by native bees. Hollywood named for Toyon berries.

trees.desc: Stunning magenta-pink flowers on bare branches in early spring before leaves emerge. Heart-shaped leaves turn yellow in fall.
trees.hp: Among the first trees to bloom in spring — magenta flowers on bare wood. Visited by early-season bumble bees. Host for Henry's Elfin butterfly (Las Pilitas confirmed).

trees.desc: Produces the heaviest pine cone in the world — up to 11 lbs. Long gray-green needles in bundles of 3. Mountain slopes.

fm.Cones: Massive — to 11 lbs, heaviest pine cone on Earth
fm.Bark: Dark brown-gray, deeply furrowed — thick plates

trees.hp: Produces the heaviest pine cone on Earth — up to 11 lbs. Seeds eaten by Steller's Jay, squirrels, and woodpeckers. Fire-adapted thick bark.

trees.desc: Mountain pine with butterscotch-vanilla scented bark. Stick your nose in the bark crevices to smell it. Three needles per bundle.

fm.Fire: Fire-adapted — thick bark, self-pruning lower limbs

trees.hp: Butterscotch-vanilla scented bark — stick your nose in the crevices. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker and chickarees. Fire-adapted self-pruning.

trees.desc: Tallest pine in the world. Enormous hanging cones to 2 ft long. John Muir called it "the noblest pine." High San Gabriels.

fm.Bark: Red-brown, deeply furrowed into rectangular plates

trees.hp: John Muir called it 'the noblest pine.' Tallest pine species worldwide. Enormous cones to 2 ft. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker.

trees.desc: Aromatic cedar of mountain forests. Pencil-like scent when foliage is crushed. Flat fan-like sprays. Actually used to make pencils.
trees.hp: Incense-cedar — flat sprays of scale-like leaves with distinctive resinous fragrance. Important for pencil manufacture. Fire-resistant thick bark.

trees.desc: Deciduous riparian tree with smooth gray bark. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules enrich stream soils. Catkins in late winter.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — enriches riparian soils

trees.hp: White alder — nitrogen-fixing riparian tree. Important for stream shading and cooling. Host for swallowtail butterfly larvae.

trees.desc: Flat-topped cream flower clusters followed by blue-black berries. Important wildlife food. Valley Elderberry Longhorn Beetle host.

fm.Ecology: Host for Valley Elderberry Longhorn Beetle (ESA)

trees.hp: Berries important for 30+ bird species. Flowers visited by native bees and flower beetles.

trees.desc: Only palm native to California. Natural groves at desert oases. Thatch skirt of dead fronds. Most "palm trees" in LA are imported species.

fm.Habitat: Desert oases — only native CA palm
fm.Bark: Stout, with persistent leaf base thatch skirt

trees.hp: Hooded Orioles nest in palm fronds. Fruit eaten by many bird species. CA/AZ endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

trees.desc: SoCal endemic conifer. Larger cones than Pacific Northwest Douglas-fir. Fire-adapted with thick bark. North-facing mountain slopes.

fm.Fire: Fire-adapted — thick bark provides resistance; survives moderate fire
fm.Bark: Thick, deeply furrowed red-brown — fire resistant

trees.hp: SoCal endemic conifer — fire-adapted with thick bark. Larger cones than Pacific Northwest Douglas-fir. Important raptor nesting habitat.

trees.desc: Deciduous oak with distinctive blue-gray leaves. At southern range limit in northern LA County. Drought-adapted — drops leaves in severe drought.

fm.Habitat: Foothill woodland — at southern range edge

trees.hp: Deciduous oak with blue-gray leaves. Drops leaves in severe drought. At southern range limit in LA County.

trees.desc: Gnarled desert tree-shrub with scale-like leaves and fleshy blue "berries" (actually cones). Desert edge of LA County.
trees.hp: California juniper — blue berry-like cones eaten by Phainopepla (silky-flycatcher). Important desert wildlife tree. Drought-tolerant.

trees.desc: Iconic Mojave Desert tree-yucca at LA County's northeastern edge. Pollinated exclusively by Yucca Moth. Climate-threatened.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert woodland, northeast LA County

trees.hp: Obligate mutualism with Yucca Moth. Pollinated exclusively by Tegeticula synthetica.

trees.desc: White-pink flower spikes in spring. Toxic seeds. Summer-deciduous — drops leaves in drought. Ghostly silver bare trunks in summer.

fm.Behavior: Summer-deciduous — bare silvery trunks in drought
fm.Bark: Smooth, pale gray — silvery when leafless in summer

trees.hp: Summer-deciduous — ghostly bare trunks in drought. Toxic seeds. Host for Echo Blue and Spring Azure butterflies (Las Pilitas). Early-season bumble bee nectar.

trees.desc: Towering blue-gray bark tree planted since 1850s. Monarch roost sites but displaces natives.
trees.hp: Flowers visited by hummingbirds and honeybees. Non-native — may displace native vegetation.

trees.desc: Purple flower canopy iconic to LA. South American. Unofficial symbol of the city.
trees.hp: Flowers attract native bees and hummingbirds. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat.

trees.desc: Graceful weeping tree with feathery compound leaves and pink peppercorn clusters. Planted by Spanish missions throughout California — now naturalized. Gnarled bark with age. Drought-tolerant but allergenic sap related to poison oak.
trees.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Peruvian pepper tree — iconic LA street tree with weeping form. Pink peppercorns used as spice. Displaces native vegetation on hillsides. Provides raptor nesting habitat.

trees.desc: Gnarled Mediterranean tree planted across LA since the mission era. Gray-green evergreen foliage. Highly allergenic pollen in spring — some cities ban new plantings. Fruit requires processing before eating.

fm.Bark: Gray, gnarled, deeply fissured with age

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Mediterranean olive naturalizing on hillsides via bird-dispersed fruit. Dense shade excludes native understory. Pollen is significant allergen.

trees.desc: Not a true willow. Showy orchid-like pink flowers. Desert washes. Hummingbird-pollinated.
trees.hp: Hummingbird-pollinated. Host for Catalpa Sphinx Moth. Important desert wash nectar source. Not a true willow despite the name.

trees.desc: SoCal endemic deciduous oak. Blue-gray leaves. Fewer than 10,000 trees remain — among California's rarest oaks.
trees.hp: SoCal endemic — fewer than 10,000 trees remain. Acorn woodpecker and jay habitat. Blue-gray deciduous leaves.

trees.desc: Tall mountain conifer with blue-green flat needles. Christmas tree shape. Upper montane forests of San Gabriel Mountains.

fm.Bark: Gray, smooth when young — thick corky furrows with age

trees.hp: White fir — blue-green flat needles. Important timber tree. Seeds eaten by chickadees and nuthatches. Fire-sensitive — bark thickens with age.

trees.desc: Slender mountain pine with short needles in pairs. At southern range limit on highest San Gabriel peaks.

fm.Habitat: Mountain peaks above 7,000 ft — southern range edge

trees.hp: Lodgepole pine — straight narrow trunks growing in dense stands. Named for use as teepee poles. Some populations have serotinous cones.

trees.desc: Gnarled high-altitude pine with extremely flexible branches. Krummholz form near treeline. Mt. Baden-Powell and Baldy.

fm.Bark: Gray-white, smooth when young — furrowed when old

trees.hp: Limber pine — flexible twigs that can be tied in knots without breaking. High-elevation specialist. Important for Clark's Nutcracker.

trees.desc: Tall riparian tree with balsam-scented buds in spring. Larger leaves than Fremont Cottonwood. Mountain streams.
trees.hp: Black cottonwood — sticky resinous buds. Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak, Lorquin's Admiral. Tallest native hardwood.

trees.desc: Only native maple in SoCal. Compound leaves unlike typical maples. Riparian corridors. Boxelder bugs congregate on trunks.

fm.Ecology: Host for Boxelder Bugs

trees.hp: Native box elder — the only maple native to LA County lowlands. Seeds eaten by Evening Grosbeak. Fast-growing riparian colonizer. Compound leaves unusual for maples.

trees.desc: Small deciduous tree with two-petaled white flowers. Dry foothill canyons. More drought-tolerant than other ashes.
trees.hp: California ash — only two-petaled ash species. White flower clusters. Foothill woodland. Host for Two-tailed Swallowtail.

trees.desc: The tall slender palm lining LA boulevards. Mexican, not Californian. Taller and thinner than native Washingtonia filifera.

fm.Origin: Mexican — the iconic LA boulevard palm
fm.Bark: Gray, smooth, ringed — much thinner than W. filifera

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Mexican fan palm — naturalizing in riparian corridors from bird-dispersed seeds. Dense stands alter hydrology and displace native willows and cottonwoods.

trees.desc: Massive palm with thick trunk and arching fronds. Planted throughout LA parks and estates. Not from California.

fm.Bark: Thick, diamond-patterned from persistent leaf bases

trees.hp: Non-native Canary Island date palm — iconic LA silhouette. Threatened by South American palm weevil. Dates eaten by birds. Fire hazard from accumulated dead fronds.

trees.desc: Massive buttress-rooted fig. Largest in LA is a 1874 specimen in Santa Monica. Strangler fig ecology in native range.

fm.Cultural: 1874 Santa Monica specimen is famous

trees.hp: Non-native Moreton Bay fig — massive buttressed roots. Australian. Dramatic specimen trees in LA parks (e.g., Santa Monica).

trees.desc: Mediterranean evergreen with leathery pods. Pods are chocolate substitute. Seeds historically used as carat weight standard.

fm.Cultural: Seeds = original carat weight standard

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Seeds and pods eaten by wildlife. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat.

trees.desc: Largest maple leaves of any maple species — up to 2 ft across. Confirmed at Chantry Flat and Santa Anita Canyon. Golden fall color. One of the few native trees offering real autumn display in LA County.

fm.Fall: Golden yellow — best fall color of LA native trees
fm.Habitat: Moist canyons — Chantry Flat, Santa Anita Canyon

trees.hp: Massive leaves support diverse epiphyte communities. Important for moths and beetle larvae.

trees.desc: Tall pine with puzzle-piece bark that smells like butterscotch or vanilla. Long needles in bundles of 3. Mid-elevation San Gabriels.
trees.hp: Ponderosa pine — massive tree with jigsaw-puzzle bark smelling of butterscotch. Fire-adapted thick bark. Most widely distributed NA pine.

trees.desc: Australian tree with ferny leaves and spectacular orange comb-like flowers. Widely planted as street tree across LA. Not a true oak.
trees.hp: Acorns are critical food for Acorn Woodpecker, Western Scrub-Jay, and Band-tailed Pigeon. Supports hundreds of insect species including moth larvae and gall wasps. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat.

trees.desc: Smooth red peeling bark and evergreen leaves. At extreme southern range limit in LA County mountains. Iconic Pacific Northwest tree barely reaching SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Mountain north-facing slopes — at southern range edge

trees.hp: Pacific madrone — smooth red bark peeling to reveal green. Evergreen. Berries important for band-tailed pigeon. Fire-sensitive.

trees.desc: Hybrid plane tree dominating LA street plantings. Mottled bark like native Sycamore but with more uniform canopy. Highly allergenic.

fm.Origin: Hybrid — most planted LA street tree

trees.hp: Non-native London plane — hybrid street tree. Extremely pollution-tolerant. Peeling bark in cream-green patches. Major LA street tree.

trees.desc: Red cylindrical flower brushes. Australian native widely planted. Important urban hummingbird food year-round.
trees.hp: Non-native bottlebrush — red bottle-brush flower spikes. Australian. Major hummingbird nectar source in LA neighborhoods.

trees.desc: Dense evergreen street tree increasingly planted across LA. Australian. White flowers. Replacing aging Ficus as preferred city tree.

fm.Origin: Australian — increasingly popular LA street tree

trees.hp: Non-native Brisbane box — Australian street tree with white flower clusters. Increasingly planted in LA. Attracts honeybees.

trees.desc: Dense evergreen fig with massive buttress roots that famously crack sidewalks. Asian origin. Among the most common LA street trees. Aerial roots.

fm.Roots: Massive surface buttress roots — crack sidewalks
fm.Origin: Asian — one of LA's most common street trees

trees.hp: Non-native Indian laurel fig — common LA street tree with curtain of aerial roots. Fig wasps recently arrived in LA — now producing viable fruit.

trees.desc: Relict Channel Islands endemic with ferny leaves and peeling red bark. Only native to Catalina, San Clemente, Santa Cruz, Santa Rosa Islands. Living fossil — closest relatives are 20 million year old fossils.
trees.hp: Channel Islands endemic — living fossil. Closest relatives are 20-million-year-old fossils. Red peeling bark. Provides nesting habitat for island endemic birds.

trees.desc: Widely planted pine with naturally tiny range (3 mainland CA populations). Dense dark green canopy. Most planted pine worldwide for timber.

fm.Origin: Native CA but planted far outside range in LA

trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, crossbills, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat.

trees.desc: Rarest pine in North America — wild populations only at Torrey Pines and Santa Rosa Island. Long needles in bundles of 5. Planted as specimen across LA.

fm.Needles: 8–13 in, bundles of 5 — longest of any pine

trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, crossbills, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. CA (extreme) endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

trees.desc: Feathery pink-flowered tree invading desert waterways. Drops salt on soil, excluding native plants. Major target of removal programs.

fm.Ecology: Drops salt — poisons soil for native plants. Massive water consumer.

trees.hp: Cal-IPC High. Tamarisk — aggressive riparian invader. Salt-excreting leaves raise soil salinity. Massive water consumption. Active removal programs.

trees.desc: At extreme southern range limit on Mt. San Antonio (Baldy). Few trees persist above 9,000 ft. Blue-green needles. Among the rarest trees in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Mt. Baldy above 9,000 ft — extreme southern range limit
fm.Conservation: Very few trees in LA County

trees.hp: Engelmann spruce — high-elevation specialist at extreme southern range limit in San Gabriel Mountains. Sharp square needles. Cool-climate relict.

trees.desc: Extremely rare native cypress at a few sites in the Santa Ana Mountains edge of LA County. Fire-dependent — serotinous cones open only after fire. Federally threatened.
trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, crossbills, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

trees.desc: Aggressive invasive tree with compound leaves that smell like rancid peanut butter when crushed. Grows through pavement. Root-suckers extensively. Impossible to eradicate once established. Origin of "A Tree Grows in Brooklyn."

fm.Leaves: Pinnate, 1–3 ft long — foul smell when crushed
fm.Origin: Chinese — one of most invasive trees globally
fm.Ecology: Root-suckers aggressively; allelopathic — poisons soil for competitors

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Tree-of-heaven — aggressive non-native producing allelopathic chemicals from roots that inhibit native plant growth. Vigorous root suckering. Difficult to eradicate.

trees.desc: Slender-leaved willow forming dense thickets along streams. Spreads aggressively by root suckers. Critical riparian habitat — first colonizer of sand and gravel bars after floods.
trees.hp: Narrowleaf willow — slender silvery leaves. Forms dense riparian thickets. Important Mourning Cloak larval host. Bank stabilizer.

trees.desc: Low scrubby oak with thick leathery convex leaves. Rare in LA County — Griffith Park has unusual occurrences. More common on serpentine soils in NorCal.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes — rare in LA County; Griffith Park
fm.Conservation: Rare in LA — unusual disjunct occurrence

trees.hp: Leather oak — CNPS 4.2. Thick leathery leaves. Serpentine soil specialist at range edge in LA County. Important acorn producer for wildlife.

trees.desc: Desert tree with distinctive spiral coiled bean pods. More compact than honey mesquite. Desert springs and washes with high water table.
trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Seeds and pods eaten by wildlife.

trees.desc: Ash tree of desert washes and canyon bottoms. Velvety-hairy leaflets. More drought-tolerant than F. dipetala. SGM canyons and Antelope Valley edge.
trees.hp: Native velvet ash of mountain stream banks. Compound leaves with velvety undersides. Host for Swallowtail butterflies. Threatened by emerald ash borer.

trees.desc: The largest native willow — to 60 ft. Dark deeply furrowed bark. Major riparian tree along LA rivers and larger streams. Provides critical shade for stream habitats.

fm.Habitat: Along rivers, larger streams — major riparian tree

trees.hp: Goodding's willow — largest native willow. Important raptor nesting tree. Host for butterfly larvae and moth species.

trees.desc: Tallest native cottonwood — can reach 100 ft. Sticky resinous buds with sweet balsam fragrance in spring. Mountain streams. Fast-growing pioneer species.
trees.hp: Balsam poplar — strongly balsam-scented buds. Mountain riparian zones. Host for swallowtail and admiral butterfly larvae.

trees.desc: Only pine in the world with single needles (all other pines have 2+). Edible pine nuts — Paiute/Shoshone staple food. Desert-mountain transition. Round open crown.

fm.Needles: Single (unique — no other pine has this)
fm.Cultural: Major indigenous food source — Paiute pine nut harvest

trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, crossbills, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

trees.desc: True coastal scrub oak — much rarer than Q. berberidifolia which is often misidentified as this species. CNPS 1B.1. Restricted to coastal sage scrub within a few miles of the ocean.

fm.Leaves: Small, holly-like, FLAT (not convex like Q. berberidifolia)
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — within a few miles of ocean only
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.1 — much rarer than commonly realized. Often confused with Q. berberidifolia.
fm.vs: Inland Scrub Oak (Q. berberidifolia) is found far from coast, has convex spiny leaves, and is very common

trees.hp: Nuttall's scrub oak — CNPS 1B.1. Rare coastal scrub species declining due to development. Important Acorn Woodpecker food.

trees.desc: Red berry clusters on weedy tree. Cal-IPC Limited. Related to CA Pepper Tree but more aggressive. Berries spread by birds. Contact can cause dermatitis in sensitive people — related to poison oak.

fm.Toxicity: Contact dermatitis in sensitive people — Anacardiaceae (poison oak family)

trees.hp: Non-native Brazilian pepper — red peppercorn berries. Bird-dispersed. Displaces native riparian vegetation. Less common than S. molle in LA.

trees.desc: Golden trembling leaves on white-barked tree. At extreme southern range limit on highest SGM peaks. Clonal — entire groves share one root system. The most widely distributed tree in North America.

fm.Fall: Brilliant gold — best fall color in SGM
fm.Habitat: SGM above 7,000 ft — extreme southern range limit

trees.hp: Clonal organism — entire groves connected by single root system. At extreme southern range limit in SGM. Golden fall color. Host for Mourning Cloak and Tiger Swallowtail butterflies.

trees.desc: Multi-stemmed alder of mountain streams above 5,000 ft. Distinguished from White Alder by hairy twigs and leaves. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — enriches mountain stream soils

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Stabilizes mountain stream banks. Host for various moth larvae.

trees.desc: Large willow with glossy dark green leaves and prominent stipules at leaf base. Mountain streams. Distinguished from Red Willow by glossy leaves.
trees.hp: Host for Mourning Cloak, Lorquin's Admiral, and Western Tiger Swallowtail butterfly larvae. Important riparian nesting habitat.

trees.desc: Deciduous mountain mahogany with birch-like leaves. More common at higher elevations than C. betuloides. Feathery spiral seed tails.
trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Hard wood used traditionally for tools. Feathery seed tails wind-dispersed.

trees.desc: White flower racemes followed by dark red-black cherries. Important wildlife food. Mountain canyons and stream banks. Bark and seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides.

fm.Toxicity: Bark and seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides

trees.hp: Fruit eaten by many bird species including Cedar Waxwing and Western Tanager. Important mountain wildlife food. Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail.

trees.desc: Massive evergreen with aromatic camphor-scented leaves. Asian origin — widely planted as street tree across LA. Dense shade. Naturalizing in some riparian areas.

fm.Leaves: Glossy, aromatic — camphor scent when crushed
fm.Origin: East Asian — common LA street tree

trees.hp: Non-native but provides significant urban canopy and shade. Fruits eaten by many bird species. Naturalizing in some riparian areas.

trees.desc: Mottled bark street tree — one of LA's most common planted trees. Small leaves, graceful form. Semi-evergreen in LA's mild climate. More resistant to Dutch Elm Disease than American Elm.

fm.Bark: Mottled — patches of gray, orange, brown (attractive)
fm.Origin: Chinese — extremely common LA street tree

trees.hp: Non-native but heavily used by urban birds for nesting. Mottled bark provides habitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

trees.desc: Fast-growing South American shade tree with yellow flowers and helicopter-like winged seeds. Increasingly popular LA street tree. Nitrogen fixer. Aggressive surface roots.

fm.Origin: South American — popular LA street tree
fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Non-native but provides shade canopy and flower resources for native bees in urban areas.

trees.desc: Spectacular pink flowers on thorny green trunk in fall. South American. Silky floss from seed pods. Increasingly common LA ornamental — unmistakable fat thorny trunk.

fm.Trunk: Green, fat, covered in conical thorns

trees.hp: Fall-blooming non-native providing late-season nectar for hummingbirds and bees when few other trees flower.

trees.desc: World's tallest tree species. Not native to LA County but planted specimens thrive in moist canyons — some reaching 80+ ft. Natural range begins in Big Sur, 200 miles north.

fm.Habitat: Planted in moist canyons — not native to LA

trees.hp: Not native to LA (range begins in Big Sur) but planted specimens provide habitat for native birds and invertebrates. Fire-resistant bark.

trees.desc: Distinctive flat-topped umbrella canopy — iconic Mediterranean pine planted throughout LA parks and boulevards. Edible pine nuts. Heavy cones can be hazardous.
trees.hp: Non-native but provides significant urban canopy. Edible pine nuts eaten by squirrels and jays.

trees.desc: Gnarled mountain juniper at high elevations in SGM. More common in Sierra Nevada — at range edge in LA County. Blue berry-like cones. Extremely slow-growing.
trees.hp: Seeds dispersed by birds including Townsend's Solitaire. Provides nesting habitat for mountain birds. Very slow-growing — some individuals centuries old.

trees.desc: Graceful Himalayan conifer with weeping branch tips — LA's most popular ornamental conifer. Massive specimens in Pasadena and Altadena neighborhoods. Blue-green needles.

fm.Origin: Himalayan — most popular ornamental conifer in LA

trees.hp: Non-native but provides significant urban habitat. Seeds eaten by squirrels and jays. Dense canopy used for raptor nesting.

trees.desc: Mediterranean pine widely planted across LA — tolerates extreme drought. Light green needles in pairs. Asymmetric cones. Very common on hillsides and in parks.

fm.Habitat: Hillsides, parks — very common in LA

trees.hp: Non-native but heavily used by Cooper's Hawk and other raptors for nesting. Drought-tolerant urban canopy tree.

trees.desc: Already in guide as Coast Live Oak. Skipping.

fm.Leaves: Spiny-toothed evergreen, smaller than var. agrifolia

trees.hp: Native Fagaceae family species of LA County habitats.

trees.desc: Desert scrub oak of Mojave margin — at western range limit in northeastern LA County. Small spiny leaves. Extremely drought-tolerant. Named for pioneering CA oak botanist John Tucker.

fm.Habitat: Desert margin — northeastern LA County edge

trees.hp: Desert oak providing acorns for wildlife at Mojave-chaparral transition. Named for UC Davis botanist John Tucker.

trees.desc: Spectacular golden flower clusters in summer — one of LA's showiest ornamental trees. Brazilian origin. Increasingly popular street tree.
trees.hp: Non-native but provides important summer nectar for native bees and carpenter bees when few other trees flower.

trees.desc: Brilliant orange-red flowers on bare branches in late winter — official tree of the City of Los Angeles. South African. Soft corky bark. Thorny branches.

fm.Cultural: Official tree of the City of Los Angeles

trees.hp: Official tree of LA. Critical winter nectar for Anna's Hummingbird during breeding season. Flowers before leaves emerge.

trees.desc: Non-native. common fig — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native common fig naturalizing aggressively in riparian corridors. Fruits consumed and dispersed by birds and mammals. Dense canopy shades out native riparian vegetation.

trees.desc: gray pine — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Native gray pine (formerly digger pine — name changed due to cultural insensitivity). Distinctive gray-green foliage. Large heavy cones. At southern range limit on SGM north slopes.

trees.desc: Non-native. Canary Island pine — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Canary Island pine — tall columnar form widely planted in LA. Fire-adapted — resprouts from trunk after burning. Important raptor nesting tree.

trees.desc: Non-native. Paper mulberry — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native paper mulberry naturalizing in riparian areas. Bark used for traditional tapa cloth in Polynesia. Wind-pollinated — significant allergen.

trees.desc: Non-native. Roxburgh fig — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Roxburgh fig — large-leaved tropical fig occasionally naturalizing near urban plantings. Cauliflorous — fruit grows directly on trunk.

trees.desc: Non-native. white mulberry — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native white mulberry — among the worst pollen allergen trees in LA. Fruits eaten by many bird species. Hybridizes with native M. rubra.

trees.desc: knobcone pine — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Native knobcone pine — serotinous cones remain closed for years, opening only after fire. Fire-dependent reproduction. At extreme southern range limit in LA County.

trees.desc: Non-native. Korean mulberry — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Korean mulberry — dense shade tree with edible fruit. Less common than M. alba in LA. Important bird food.

trees.desc: Non-native. Sacred fig — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native sacred fig (Bodhi tree) — heart-shaped leaves with long drip tip. Religious significance in Buddhism. Planted in temples.

trees.desc: Arizona cypress — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Native Arizona cypress — blue-gray foliage. Planted as ornamental and windbreak. Native to AZ, at edge of natural range.

trees.desc: Smooth Arizona cypress — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Native smooth Arizona cypress — similar to H. arizonica but with smooth (not rough) bark. Native to desert mountains.

trees.desc: Non-native. hairy crabweed — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native hairy crabweed — small Asian mulberry relative spreading rapidly as urban weed. Explosive seed dispersal.

trees.desc: Non-native. Siberian elm — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Siberian elm — tough drought-tolerant street tree. Seeds prolifically. Wood brittle — storm damage risk.

trees.desc: Non-native. Indian banyan — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Indian banyan — massive spreading tree with aerial roots. Planted in parks. Can grow to enormous size.

trees.desc: Monterey Cypress — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Native Monterey cypress — naturally restricted to two groves near Monterey but widely planted. Iconic windswept form.

trees.desc: Non-native. Scots pine — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Scots pine — European. Orange upper bark distinctive. Occasionally planted in LA parks.

trees.desc: Non-native. Common Wild Fig — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native common wild fig — African strangler fig. Rare in LA cultivation.

trees.desc: Non-native. Port Jackson fig — a tree found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Port Jackson fig — Australian fig with rusty-hairy leaves. Planted as street tree in some LA neighborhoods.

trees.desc: Non-native. black locust — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native black locust — nitrogen fixer with fragrant white flower clusters. Aggressively suckering from roots. Hard rot-resistant wood used for fence posts.

trees.desc: Non-native. Coast Coral Tree — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native Coast coral tree — red flowers. South African. Less common than E. caffra in LA. Thorny trunk.

trees.desc: Non-native. honey locust — a wildflower in the  family found in LA County.
trees.hp: Non-native honey locust — long twisted pods. Eastern US native planted as street tree. Thornless cultivars common.

shrubs.desc: The signature scent of coastal sage scrub. Gray-green threadlike aromatic leaves. Drought-deciduous in summer. Foundation species of CSS.

fm.Ecology: Drought-deciduous — drops leaves in summer
fm.Fire: Resprouter — weak; also colonizes from seed after fire

shrubs.hp: Foundation species for Coastal California Gnatcatcher (federally threatened) habitat. Signature scent of coastal sage scrub. Host for many moth species. Drought-deciduous.

shrubs.desc: Pioneer colonizer of disturbed ground. Dioecious — female plants produce cotton-like seed puffs. Wind-dispersed.

fm.Ecology: Pioneer species — first to colonize bare ground
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; also seeds vigorously into burned areas

shrubs.hp: Important fall nectar for native bees and butterflies when few other plants bloom. Pioneer colonizer of disturbed ground. Dioecious — female plants produce cotton-like seed puffs.

shrubs.desc: Most widespread chaparral shrub in California. Needle-like leaves. Highly flammable but resprouts vigorously after fire.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral — most widespread CA chaparral shrub

shrubs.hp: Most widespread chaparral shrub in California — foundation species. Host for Ceanothus Silk Moth. Facultative seeder — resprouts from burl AND seeds germinate from heat/smoke (Keeley 1991).

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with taco-folded aromatic leaves. White flower plumes. Frost-sensitive — its distribution marks mild coastal climate zone.

fm.Ecology: Frost-sensitive — climate indicator

shrubs.hp: Frost-sensitive — distribution marks mild coastal climate zone. Obligate resprouter from root crown after fire. Berries eaten by birds. Taco-shaped leaf folds are diagnostic.

shrubs.desc: Coastal evergreen with sticky reddish berries. Berry coating tastes like lemon when soaked in water — hence the name. Important bird food.
shrubs.hp: Berries important for mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Berry coating lemon-flavored — makes lemonade-like drink. Early-season bee forage (Feb–Apr bloom).

shrubs.desc: Inland relative of Lemonade Berry with ovate folded leaves. Similar lemon-flavored berries. Chaparral and sage scrub.
shrubs.hp: Berries eaten by many bird species including mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

shrubs.desc: Smooth red bark on twisted trunks. Urn-shaped white-pink flowers. Large red berries. Classic chaparral species — the name means "little apple."

fm.Fire: Resprouter — basal burl; also seeds germinated by fire
fm.Cultural: Manzanita = "little apple" (Spanish)

shrubs.hp: Early-season bumble bee forage — flowers Dec–Feb when almost nothing else blooms. Berries eaten by bears, coyotes, and many bird species. Smooth mahogany-red bark diagnostic.

shrubs.desc: Blue-green bark and dense pale blue flower clusters. Nitrogen-fixing. Deer browse resistant. One of the largest Ceanothus species.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Unusual for Ceanothus — resprouts from lignotuber (most Ceanothus are obligate seeders killed by fire). Green bark photosynthesizes.

shrubs.desc: Deep blue flower clusters in spring — among the most intensely blue flowers in the chaparral. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; heat-activated seed bank

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Obligate seeder — killed by fire; heat-activated seed bank. Deep blue flowers among the most intensely blue in chaparral.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen cherry with holly-like spiny leaves. Small white flower clusters. Dark red cherries eaten by birds. Dense shade-casting.
shrubs.hp: Dark red cherries eaten by Band-tailed Pigeon, mockingbird, and other birds. Host for Pale Swallowtail butterfly (Las Pilitas). Fire-adapted resprouter.

shrubs.desc: Rust-brown flower heads that persist year-round. Foundation plant of coastal sage scrub. Critical for native butterflies and bees.

fm.Flowers: White to pink clusters, turning rust
fm.Ecology: Foundation species for pollinators — critical for native bees and butterflies

shrubs.hp: Host for Bramble Hairstreak, Mormon Metalmark, and many other butterflies. Foundation nectar source for native bees.

shrubs.desc: Gray-purple aromatic sage of coastal scrub. Produces allelopathic chemicals that inhibit grass growth. Important bee plant.

fm.Leaves: GRAY-WHITE, woolly-hairy (much lighter than S. mellifera)
fm.Ecology: Allelopathic — inhibits nearby grasses
fm.Habitat: Dry open hills, CSS, chaparral — below 2,500 ft
fm.vs: Black Sage (S. mellifera) has pale BLUE-WHITE flowers and DARK GREEN wrinkled leaves

shrubs.hp: Produces allelopathic chemicals from roots that inhibit grass growth in surrounding soil — creates bare 'halo' zones around shrubs. Important bee forage. Fire-adapted resprouter.

shrubs.desc: Willow-like riparian shrub. Among first plants to colonize gravel bars. Important soil stabilizer. Dense white flower clusters.

fm.Ecology: Critical streambank stabilizer
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root crown and rhizome sprouting

shrubs.hp: Critical streambank stabilizer — among first plants to colonize gravel bars after floods. Year-round bloomer providing continuous nectar for butterflies and bees.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flowers and inflated balloon-like seed pods. Drought-deciduous. Desert edges and coastal bluffs. Malodorous foliage.
shrubs.hp: Bladderpod — inflated seed pods. Important butterfly nectar plant in coastal sage scrub. Visited by many pollinator species.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flowers in fall when most shrubs are done blooming. Important late-season nectar. Coastal and disturbed areas.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; colonizes burns

shrubs.hp: Critical late-season nectar for native bees and migrating butterflies.

shrubs.desc: "Holy herb" — sticky aromatic leaves used medicinally by indigenous peoples. Lavender tubular flowers. Post-fire resprouter.

fm.Cultural: Yerba Santa = "holy herb" (Spanish)
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown and rhizome sprouting

shrubs.hp: Medicinal — 'holy herb' (Yerba Santa). Leaves brewed as tea for respiratory ailments by indigenous peoples. Top-ranking nectar plant for adult butterflies (Las Pilitas top 5).

shrubs.desc: Hard-wooded chaparral shrub with feathery spiral-tailed seeds. Among the hardest woods of any CA plant. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer
fm.Color: Inconspicuous — feathery seed tails are showier

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Among the hardest woods of any CA plant — traditionally used for tools. Feathery spiral-tailed seeds are diagnostic.

shrubs.desc: Glossy evergreen shrub with coffee-bean-shaped berries turning red to black. Bird food. Shade-tolerant understory species.
shrubs.hp: Berries ripen green → red → black sequentially — important multi-season bird food. Shade-tolerant understory species. Fire-adapted resprouter.

shrubs.desc: Dramatic hanging catkin chains in winter — male plants with catkins to 8 in. Evergreen. Dry slopes.
shrubs.hp: Canyon silk-tassel — long pendant catkin-like tassels. Chaparral and canyon woodland. Dioecious — separate male and female plants.

shrubs.desc: Bright yellow four-petaled poppy flowers on an evergreen shrub. Fire follower — seeds require fire or smoke to germinate.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower — seeds need smoke/heat
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds require fire or smoke to germinate

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: The smell of desert rain. Clonal rings to 11,000 years old (King Clone).

fm.Ecology: Clonal rings to 11,000 years old
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; clonal ring expansion

shrubs.hp: Over 60 specialist bee species pollinate Creosote. Foundation species of Mojave Desert scrub. Clonal rings live to 11,000 years (King Clone — oldest living organism).

shrubs.desc: Spectacular large golden-yellow flowers — among the showiest native shrubs. Dense stellate (star-shaped) hairs covering leaves and stems are highly irritating to skin and eyes. Fire follower. California endemic. Chaparral slopes.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds require heat/smoke to germinate; killed by fire but seed bank persists

shrubs.hp: Spectacular golden flowers but CAUTION: dense stellate hairs highly irritating to skin and eyes — wear gloves. Fire follower — seeds require heat/smoke. CA endemic.

shrubs.desc: "Leaves of three, let it be." Urushiol causes severe dermatitis. Brilliant red fall color.

fm.Leaves: Trifoliate — "leaves of three, let it be." Terminal leaflet has a STALK (vs sessile in Skunkbrush).
fm.Toxicity: Urushiol oil — severe contact dermatitis in 85% of people. All parts toxic year-round.
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous regrowth. Urushiol in smoke causes lung damage.
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, chaparral, riparian — very common
fm.Fruit: White berries — important bird food (birds immune)
fm.vs: Skunkbrush (Rhus trilobata) also has 3 leaflets but terminal leaflet is SESSILE (no stalk), leaves are smaller, and crushed leaves smell skunky. Skunkbrush is harmless.

shrubs.hp: Berries are critical fall/winter food for many bird species (birds immune to urushiol). Host for Spring Azure butterfly. All parts contain urushiol year-round. Smoke inhalation causes lung damage.

shrubs.desc: Red-barked chaparral shrub with shredding bark that peels in long strips — diagnostic field mark. More open growth form than its relative Chamise. Higher elevations. Aromatic resinous foliage. Resprouts vigorously after fire.
shrubs.hp: Red shredding bark diagnostic. Higher elevation than chamise. Resprouter from basal burl. Important bee forage — late-summer bloomer (Jul–Sep).

shrubs.desc: Intensely fragrant blue-purple sage. Possibly the most aromatic California native. Popular garden plant.

fm.Fragrance: Intensely aromatic — most fragrant native sage

shrubs.hp: Cleveland sage — intensely fragrant purple flowers. The most aromatic native sage. Important hummingbird and carpenter bee nectar plant.

shrubs.desc: Wine-red fragrant flowers. Crushed bark smells like cinnamon. Rare in LA County — moist mountain canyons only.

fm.Habitat: Moist mountain canyons — very rare in LA

shrubs.hp: Spice bush — fragrant maroon flowers with spicy scent. Riparian specialist. Seeds dispersed by water. Indigenous medicinal plant.

shrubs.desc: Pink drooping flower clusters in fall-winter — one of the earliest-blooming shrubs. Important for Anna's Hummingbird.
shrubs.hp: One of the earliest-blooming shrubs — Oct–Feb. Critical for Anna's Hummingbird which breeds in winter. Important early-season nectar when few other plants flower.

shrubs.desc: Bright red fuchsia-like flowers on thorny stems. Hummingbird specialist. Among the showiest native shrubs.
shrubs.hp: Critical winter nectar for Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds when almost nothing else blooms. Bright red fuchsia-like flowers. CA endemic.

shrubs.desc: Large gray-green shrub of alkaline and coastal areas. Important Quail habitat. Salt-tolerant. Used in restoration of degraded sites.

fm.Ecology: Important Quail habitat — salt-tolerant

shrubs.hp: Quail bush — important food and cover for Gambel's Quail. Large saltbush of alkali flats. Grey-green diamond-shaped leaves.

shrubs.desc: Tree-like daisy to 10 ft with succulent trunk. Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. Spectacular in bloom — looks like a yellow coral tree.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — Channel Islands and mainland

shrubs.hp: Important nectar source for native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus of chaparral. Thick leathery leaves with white undersides. Obligate seeder — killed by fire but regenerates from seed bank.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from seed

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from soil seed bank. Heat-activated seeds. White-woolly leaf undersides diagnostic. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Dense green broom-like shrub of desert edges. Nearly leafless — photosynthesizes through green stems.
shrubs.hp: Broom baccharis — green rush-like stems. Desert wash specialist. Important late-season nectar when few other plants bloom.

shrubs.desc: Green broom-like shrub of dry washes. Scale-like leaves. Important habitat for Coastal California Gnatcatcher.

fm.Ecology: Gnatcatcher habitat

shrubs.hp: Critical habitat for Coastal California Gnatcatcher (ESA threatened).

shrubs.desc: Thorny coastal shrub with small purple flowers and red berries. Related to goji berry. Salt-tolerant.
shrubs.hp: California boxthorn — fleshy red berries important for desert birds. Thorny growth provides nesting protection. Salt-tolerant.

shrubs.desc: Low gray desert shrub. Dominant co-dominant with Creosote Bush in Mojave scrub. Among the most drought-tolerant plants known.

fm.Ecology: Co-dominant with Creosote in Mojave

shrubs.hp: Burro bush — dominant low shrub of Mojave Desert. Important habitat structure for desert lizards and kangaroo rats.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green desert shrub of alkaline flats. Important wildlife browse. Extremely salt and drought tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Important wildlife browse

shrubs.hp: Allscale saltbush — many-seeded. Important browse for desert bighorn sheep. Extremely salt-tolerant.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert shrub with cat-claw-shaped thorns. Yellow puffball flowers. Important bee forage. Desert washes.

fm.Ecology: Important bee forage

shrubs.hp: Catclaw acacia — curved spines catch on clothing ('wait-a-minute' bush). Important desert bee forage. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Wiry green shrub of desert washes. Named for cheese-wheel-shaped fruit. Wind-pollinated — major hay fever plant in desert areas.

fm.Pollen: Major hay fever source in desert

shrubs.hp: Cheesebush — wind-pollinated with pungent odor. Important desert habitat structure. Pioneer on disturbed desert soils.

shrubs.desc: Desert shrub with inflated papery calyx enclosing seeds. Purple-blue flowers. Dry rocky desert slopes.
shrubs.hp: Mexican bladder sage — inflated papery seed pods. Desert-mountain transition. Unusual for the mint family.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green desert shrub with distinctive four-winged fruit. Extremely salt and drought tolerant. Important browse for wildlife and livestock.
shrubs.hp: Hoary saltbush — distinctive four-winged fruits. Alkali-tolerant. Important for desert restoration. Browse for pronghorn.

shrubs.desc: Intensely fragrant gray-leaved desert shrub. Purple flower spikes. Strong lavender scent — attracts swarms of native bees. Desert washes and rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: Desert lavender — fragrant purple-blue flowers in desert washes. Important desert bee forage. Frost-sensitive — indicator of mild microclimates.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with holly-like spiny leaves and small red berries. Important bird food. Dense chaparral component.
shrubs.hp: Hollyleaf redberry — holly-like leaves with red berries eaten by birds. Chaparral understory. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria.

shrubs.desc: Thorny pale-barked ceanothus with blue-white flower clusters. White bark diagnostic — ghost-like in chaparral. Important for nitrogen cycling.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: White bark diagnostic — 'ghost ceanothus.' Nitrogen fixer. Resprouter from root crown (unusual thorny Ceanothus). Important post-fire habitat structure.

shrubs.desc: Inconspicuous maroon flowers but one of the most important native bee plants. Square stems. Woodland shade. Blooms in winter when little else does.

fm.Ecology: Premier winter bee forage — blooms when nothing else does

shrubs.hp: Critical early-season nectar for native bumble bees and carpenter bees when few other plants bloom.

shrubs.desc: Compact rosette succulent of desert slopes. Massive flower stalk 8–15 ft after 20+ years, then dies (monocarpic). Indigenous food plant — hearts roasted in pit ovens.

fm.Lifecycle: Monocarpic — blooms once after 20+ years, then dies
fm.Cultural: Hearts roasted in pit ovens (indigenous food)

shrubs.hp: Desert agave — massive rosette blooming once after decades then dying. Important hummingbird nectar. Indigenous food plant (roasted hearts).

shrubs.desc: Yellow pea flowers on green stems forming dense thickets that exclude all native plants. Mediterranean invasive. Seeds remain viable in soil for decades. Active removal programs across California.

fm.Ecology: Seeds viable 30+ years in soil bank; allelopathic
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — burns hot; reseeds aggressively after fire

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC High. French broom — nitrogen-fixing shrub forming impenetrable thickets. Explosive seed pods. Fire hazard. Major invasive across coastal California.

shrubs.desc: Small evergreen shrub with bright red berries. Important bird food. Spiny stems. Very common in chaparral understory.

fm.Fruit: Small bright red berries — bird food

shrubs.hp: Spiny redberry — thorny with small red berries. Important bird food. Provides protective nesting cover.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with red shredding bark and small urn-shaped flowers. Red berries. Chaparral in Santa Monica Mountains. Less common than manzanita.
shrubs.hp: Summer holly — unique among manzanita relatives for its bumpy red berries and toothed leaves. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

shrubs.desc: Deciduous shrub with three-lobed leaves resembling poison oak — but not toxic. Crushed leaves smell skunky. Red berries used by Tongva to make lemonade-like drink. Important basketry material.

fm.Leaves: Three-lobed — resembles poison oak but terminal leaflet is SESSILE (no stalk) vs stalked in Poison Oak
fm.Fragrance: Skunky when crushed (Poison Oak has no odor)
fm.Cultural: Tongva basketry material and beverage plant
fm.vs: Poison Oak has a STALKED terminal leaflet, no odor when crushed, and causes severe rash. Skunkbrush is harmless.

shrubs.hp: Berries eaten by many bird species including mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus dominating the seaward slopes of the Santa Monica Mountains. Large pods. Among the most common chaparral shrubs on south-facing SMM slopes. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds require fire/heat to germinate

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — killed by fire but seedlings better adapted to post-fire drought than resprouting species (Frazer & Davis 1988). Dominates seaward SMM slopes. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Inconspicuous fall-blooming shrub with cream-colored flower clusters. Important late-season nectar for butterflies and bees. Dry washes and rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: California brickellbush — greenish-white flower heads. Chaparral and woodland. Important fall nectar source for native bees.

shrubs.desc: Small yellow-flowered subshrub of dry slopes. Sticky resinous stems. Very common in coastal sage scrub and chaparral openings. Late summer bloomer.
shrubs.hp: Important nectar source for native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Component of Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat.

shrubs.desc: Low spreading shrub with small pink bell flowers and waxy white berries. Shady chaparral and oak woodland understory. Berries mildly toxic but important for birds.
shrubs.hp: Creeping snowberry — white berries persist through winter. Important food for thrushes and waxwings. Spreads via stolons.

shrubs.desc: Tall herb of dry stream beds with clusters of tiny yellow-green flowers. Unusual — one of only two species in its family worldwide. Source of yellow dye.

fm.Cultural: Source of yellow dye
fm.Ecology: One of only two species in family Datiscaceae worldwide

shrubs.hp: Durango root — unusual riparian herb with yellowish-green flowers. Traditional indigenous medicine. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules.

shrubs.desc: Thorny white-flowered ceanothus dominating post-fire montane chaparral. Dense impenetrable thickets at 5,000–9,000 ft. Nitrogen fixer. Key component of SGM upper montane.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer. Dominant post-fire upper montane shrub.
fm.Habitat: Montane chaparral 5,000–9,000 ft — San Gabriel Mts
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root crown sprouting after fire

shrubs.hp: Dominant post-fire montane shrub — forms impenetrable thickets. Nitrogen fixer. White flowers visited by mountain bumble bees.

shrubs.desc: Golden-backed evergreen leaves with spiny-burred fruits. Mountain chaparral. Closely related to golden chinquapin trees of NorCal. Key SGM montane shrub.

fm.Habitat: Montane chaparral, rocky ridges — San Gabriel Mts

shrubs.hp: Bush chinquapin — edible nuts in spiny burs. Mountain chaparral specialist. Important food for Clark's Nutcrackers and squirrels.

shrubs.desc: Small white-pink tubular flowers with waxy red berries. Mountain shrub. Berries edible but insipid. Important wildlife food.

fm.Fruit: Red waxy berries — edible but bland
fm.Habitat: Mountain slopes, pine forest — SGM above 5,000 ft

shrubs.hp: Wax currant — waxy leaves with tiny pink flowers. Mountain specialist. Edible but bland berries. Important grouse food.

shrubs.desc: Drooping clusters of pink flowers in mountain forests. Blue-black berries. Moist shady areas near streams at mid-elevation. Beautiful ornamental.

fm.Habitat: Moist shady mountain slopes near streams

shrubs.hp: Mountain pink currant — drooping racemes of pink flowers. Mountain streams. Berries edible — tart. Hummingbird nectar source.

shrubs.desc: Red berry clusters (toxic raw) on mountain shrub. Cream dome-shaped flower clusters. Higher elevation than Blue Elderberry. SGM canyons.

fm.Fruit: Red berries — toxic raw; cooked for jelly
fm.Habitat: Mountain canyons, moist sites — SGM 4,000–8,000 ft

shrubs.hp: Red elderberry — red berries toxic raw but edible cooked. Important bird food. Distinguished from S. nigra by red (not black) fruit.

shrubs.desc: Leathery curled evergreen leaves on stiff shrub. Feathery corkscrew seed tails to 3 in. Rocky ridges and exposed slopes at upper montane. Very slow-growing.

fm.Habitat: Rocky ridges, exposed upper montane slopes

shrubs.hp: Curl-leaf mountain mahogany — hard dense wood (heaviest NA wood). Distinctive feathery-tailed fruit. Important deer browse.

shrubs.desc: Higher-elevation snowberry with round leaves and white berries. Pink bell flowers. Mountain pine forest understory. More robust than S. mollis.
shrubs.hp: Mountain snowberry — round-leaved variant of snowberry. Important grouse food. Mountain chaparral understory.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert tree with feathery compound leaves and sweet edible bean pods. Massive taproot to 175 ft deep. Critical desert shade and food. Tongva ground pods into flour.

fm.Fruit: Sweet edible pods — ground into flour by indigenous peoples
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Cahuilla staple food — pods ground into pinole

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Flowers visited by native bees. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

shrubs.desc: Gray leafless tree looking like a plume of smoke in desert washes. Purple pea flowers. Spine-tipped branches. Seeds require flash-flood scarification to germinate.
shrubs.hp: Smoke tree — blue-gray leafless branches resembling smoke from distance. Desert wash specialist. Purple pea flowers. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Bright red winter stems and white berry clusters. Mountain stream banks. One of the most visually striking winter shrubs. Attracts birds to white berries.
shrubs.hp: Red-twig dogwood — vivid red winter stems. White berries eaten by 40+ bird species. Riparian restoration staple.

shrubs.desc: Rosette of long tough grass-like leaves with tall plume-like flower stalk. Chaparral slopes. Important nesting material for woodrats. CA endemic.
shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: White tubular flower clusters on sticky shrub. Smoother leaves than hairy yerba santa. Same medicinal properties. Dry chaparral slopes.

fm.Cultural: Same medicinal uses as hairy yerba santa

shrubs.hp: Hairy yerba santa — woolly-hairy calyx. Traditional cough remedy. Fire follower resprouting vigorously after burns.

shrubs.desc: CNPS Rare Plant List 1B.1. Cream flowers on uncommon shrub of dry washes and slopes. Known from Griffith Park, Verdugo Mountains, and scattered SGM sites. Federally endangered.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, washes — scattered rare populations

shrubs.hp: Nevin's brickellbush — CNPS 1B.2. Santa Monica Mountains endemic. Yellow-green flower heads. Conservation priority.

shrubs.desc: Three-toothed leaves on aromatic shrub. Important deer browse — named "antelope" bitterbrush. Yellow flowers. High desert transition zone.

fm.Ecology: Important deer browse
fm.Habitat: High desert transition — Antelope Valley edge

shrubs.hp: Antelope bitterbrush — critical winter browse for mule deer and pronghorn. Three-toothed leaves. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Blue-lavender flowers on aromatic desert shrub. Mojave Desert endemic. Intensely fragrant dry leaves. More drought-tolerant than coastal sages.
shrubs.hp: Mojave sage — pale blue flowers. Hot desert specialist. Among the most drought-tolerant native sages.

shrubs.desc: CAUTION: causes severe contact dermatitis worse than poison oak. Tall purple-flowered shrub colonizing burns — can dominate post-fire chaparral for years. Beautiful but untouchable.

fm.Toxicity: SEVERE contact dermatitis — worse than poison oak. Do not touch any part.
fm.Habitat: Post-fire chaparral — mass colonizer of burns
fm.Fire: Fire follower — germinates prolifically after fire, dominates 2–5 years

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery.

shrubs.desc: Cascading sprays of tiny cream-white flowers. Mountain chaparral and woodland edges. Named for the foam-like appearance of flower clusters. Important browse for deer.
shrubs.hp: Ocean spray — cascading white flower clusters. Important native bee forage. Hard wood used for arrow shafts by indigenous peoples.

shrubs.desc: Pink-white urn flowers on smooth red-barked shrub. Glandular-hairy leaves distinguish from Bigberry Manzanita. Common SGM chaparral.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — fire-killed; seeds germinate from heat

shrubs.hp: Eastwood manzanita — glandular-hairy twigs. Important post-fire resprouter. Berries fermented into cider by indigenous peoples.

shrubs.desc: Small pendulous red-green flowers on spiny shrub. Shady chaparral understory. Earlier bloom than other Ribes. Small berries attract birds.
shrubs.hp: Hillside gooseberry — spiny stems with fuchsia-like pendant flowers. Chaparral understory. Important early-season hummingbird nectar.

shrubs.desc: Dense yellow flower clusters on resinous shrub. Late summer/fall bloomer — important when few other shrubs flower. Thread-like leaves.
shrubs.hp: Golden fleece — showy golden-yellow flower clusters in fall. Coastal sage scrub. Among the showiest fall-blooming native shrubs.

shrubs.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub of dry chaparral slopes. Named for early SoCal botanist Samuel Parish. Fall bloomer. Interior chaparral.

fm.Cultural: Named for botanist Samuel Parish (1838–1928)

shrubs.hp: Parish's goldenbush — narrow-leaved desert shrub. Yellow button heads in fall. Important late-season desert pollinator resource.

shrubs.desc: Sulfur-yellow flowers on compact white-woolly shrub. CNPS 1B.2 — endemic to Conejo Grade area of Ventura/LA county line. One of the rarest buckwheats in California.

fm.Habitat: Rocky volcanic soils — Conejo Grade area

shrubs.hp: Critical nectar source for native bees and butterflies including hairstreaks and metalmarks. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Desert shrub producing liquid wax "oil" seeds. Rare at eastern LA County edge (Mojave/Sonoran transition). The only plant that produces liquid wax. Major commercial crop.

fm.Fruit: Acorn-like — produces liquid wax, not oil
fm.Cultural: Major commercial crop — cosmetics, lubricant
fm.Habitat: Desert — extreme eastern LA County edge

shrubs.hp: Jojoba — liquid wax from seeds used in cosmetics. The only known plant producing liquid wax esters. Desert specialist.

shrubs.desc: Small white flower clusters and white berries on smooth-stemmed riparian shrub. Mountain stream banks. Less showy than Red-twig Dogwood but more common at lower elevations.
shrubs.hp: Brown dogwood — smooth brown bark. Small blue-white fruit. Riparian understory. Less showy than C. sericea.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flower clusters and blue-purple grape-like berries on holly-leaved shrub. Shady woodland. Berries tart but edible — make good jelly. Inner bark yields bright yellow dye.

fm.Cultural: Inner bark yields bright yellow dye

shrubs.hp: Oregon grape — holly-like leaves with yellow flowers and blue grape-like berries. Berries edible. Root bark yields yellow dye.

shrubs.desc: Brilliant purple-blue flowers on compact silvery shrub. High desert specialist. Intensely aromatic. One of the showiest desert sages. Mojave Desert edge of LA County.

fm.Habitat: High desert - Mojave edge of LA County

shrubs.hp: Purple desert sage — showy purple bracts. High-desert specialist. Important sage grouse forage in Great Basin (not in LA County).

shrubs.desc: Rose-purple bracts and blue flowers on compact shrub. Desert-mountain transition. Thick gray aromatic leaves. Drought specialist.
shrubs.hp: Mountain desert sage — thick succulent-like leaves. Mountain desert transition above 5,000 ft. Highly aromatic.

shrubs.desc: Red-barked manzanita of mid-elevation SGM chaparral. Distinguished from Bigberry by smaller fruits and non-glandular leaves. Named for botanist Charles Parry. Common at 3,500-6,500 ft.

fm.Fruit: Small berries (vs large in A. glauca)
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder - killed by fire; seeds require heat

shrubs.hp: Parry manzanita — prostrate form on exposed ridges. Important soil stabilizer on steep slopes. Desert-chaparral transition.

shrubs.desc: Showy pink flower bracts distinguish this from all other SGM manzanitas. Glandular-hairy twigs. Interior chaparral at 4,000-7,000 ft. One of the most beautiful manzanitas when flowering.

fm.Fire: Burl sprouter - survives fire via lignotuber

shrubs.hp: Pink-bracted manzanita — distinctive pink bracts below flowers. Mountain chaparral above 4,000 ft. Important early-season bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Bright green smooth leaves on spreading shrub - the greenest manzanita in the SGM. Upper montane at 6,000-9,000 ft. Common along Angeles Crest Highway at Dawson Saddle area.

fm.Leaves: Bright green, smooth, nearly round (diagnostic - greenest manzanita)
fm.Habitat: Upper montane 6,000-9,000 ft - common along Angeles Crest Hwy

shrubs.hp: Greenleaf manzanita — bright green leaves (not gray like most species). Mountain forests. Important bear food.

shrubs.desc: Critically imperiled manzanita endemic to one tiny area at Mill Creek Summit divide in the San Gabriel Mountains. Known from a single population. Bright green shiny leaves with smooth edges.

fm.Habitat: Mill Creek Summit divide only - single population
fm.Conservation: CRITICALLY IMPERILED. Endemic to one site in SGM, LA County.

shrubs.hp: Early-season bumble bee forage. Berries eaten by birds, bears, and coyotes. CA (extreme) endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Sharp-pointed leaves on dense spreading shrub. Desert-chaparral transition on north slopes of SGM. More drought-tolerant than other manzanitas. Extends into Mojave edge.
shrubs.hp: Pointleaf manzanita — sharp-pointed leaves. Desert-chaparral interface. Fire-adapted — both resprouting and reseeding populations.

shrubs.desc: Not a true Arctostaphylos but closely related. White-felted leaf undersides and hard woody fruits. Southern LA County coastal chaparral. More common in San Diego County.

fm.Habitat: Coastal chaparral - southern LA County edge

shrubs.hp: Mission manzanita — distinctive bicolored (green above, white below) leaves. Southern California endemic. Coastal sage scrub specialist.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus with small wedge-shaped leaves. Very common throughout chaparral and foothill woodland. More drought-tolerant than most ceanothus species.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds require heat
fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — seeds require fire heat to germinate. Nitrogen fixer. More drought-tolerant than most Ceanothus. Important for native bees.

shrubs.desc: Deciduous ceanothus with white or blue flowers. Important deer browse — hence the name. Mountain canyons and forest openings. The only common deciduous ceanothus in LA County.

fm.Ecology: Important deer browse

shrubs.hp: The only common deciduous Ceanothus in LA County. Important deer browse — hence the name. Nitrogen fixer. Resprouter.

shrubs.desc: Thorn-tipped branches with small blue flowers. Interior chaparral on dry rocky slopes. Among the most drought-tolerant ceanothus species.
shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Important native bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Leafless green-stemmed desert shrub. Ancient gymnosperm lineage — related to conifers not flowering plants. Stems brewed as stimulant tea by indigenous peoples. Contains pseudoephedrine precursors.

fm.Ecology: Gymnosperm — more closely related to pines than to flowering plants
fm.Cultural: Mormon Tea — stems brewed as stimulant drink

shrubs.hp: California ephedra — leafless green stems. Ancient gymnosperm lineage. Related to Chinese ma huang. Wind-pollinated.

shrubs.desc: Bright green broom-like desert shrub with no leaves. Higher-elevation than E. californica. Joshua tree woodland and pinyon-juniper zone. Yellow cone-like structures.
shrubs.hp: Green ephedra — Mormon tea. Stems brewed as tea by settlers. Green photosynthetic stems replace leaves. Desert specialist.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert shrub with small tubular flowers and red berries. Important bird food. Related to goji berry. Tongva ate berries fresh. Desert washes and rocky slopes.

fm.Cultural: Related to goji berry. Tongva ate berries fresh.

shrubs.hp: Anderson's boxthorn — thorny desert shrub with fleshy red berries. Important bird food in desert. Spring nectar source.

shrubs.desc: Spectacular spiny wand-like stems tipped with flame-red flowers. Leafs out after rain, drops leaves in drought — multiple cycles per year. Barely reaches eastern LA County (Mojave edge).

fm.Color: Flame-red tubular clusters at stem tips
fm.Form: Cluster of wand-like spiny stems from base
fm.Ecology: Drought-deciduous — multiple leaf cycles per year
fm.Habitat: Desert rocky slopes — barely reaches eastern LA County

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Hummingbird-pollinated.

shrubs.desc: Blue-purple lupine that smells exactly like grape soda. Desert-mountain transition. Silvery-hairy leaves. One of the most pleasantly fragrant wildflowers.
shrubs.hp: Grape soda lupine — supposedly smells of grape soda. Large showy blue-purple spikes. Mountain chaparral. Nitrogen fixer.

shrubs.desc: Silvery-leaved perennial bush lupine with blue-purple flower spikes. Chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Host plant for the federally endangered Mission Blue Butterfly.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, sandy slopes

shrubs.hp: Host plant for the federally endangered Mission Blue Butterfly (Icaricia icarioides missionensis).

shrubs.desc: Silvery coastal bush lupine of sand dunes and coastal bluffs. Blue-lavender flowers. Important dune stabilizer. Less common than L. albifrons inland.
shrubs.hp: Dune bush lupine — silver-hairy leaves on coastal dunes. Important dune stabilizer. Nitrogen fixer. Purple flowers.

shrubs.desc: White flowers on stiff shrub. Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. The only member of its family endemic to California. Extremely rare on mainland LA County.

fm.Ecology: Sole genus of family Crossosomataceae — CA endemic

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Drooping clusters of white flowers in winter — blooms November through March when almost nothing else flowers. Critical early-season bee and hummingbird nectar.

fm.Bloom: Nov–Mar — flowers when almost nothing else does

shrubs.hp: Critical winter nectar — flowers Nov–Mar. Among the most important plants for overwintering Anna's Hummingbird. White-flowered.

shrubs.desc: Yellow pea flowers on bright green leafless stems. Cal-IPC High. Aggressively colonizes roadcuts and burns throughout SMM and SGM. Seeds viable for decades in soil. Extremely difficult to eradicate.

fm.Ecology: Colonizes roadcuts and burns. Seed bank lasts decades.
fm.Habitat: Roadcuts, burns, disturbed slopes — SMM and SGM

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC High. Spanish broom — bright yellow pea flowers on rush-like stems. Nitrogen fixer. Aggressively colonizes road cuts and disturbed slopes. Fire hazard.

shrubs.desc: Fast-growing New Zealand shrub/tree planted as windbreak. Cal-IPC Moderate. Being devastated by thrips (Klambothrips myopori) since 2005 — dying across LA. Still invasive in riparian areas.

fm.Ecology: Being killed by imported thrips since 2005 — widespread dieback across LA
fm.Habitat: Planted as windbreak — escaping to riparian areas

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation.

shrubs.desc: Fragrant golden tubular flowers in spring. Edible dark berries. Important hummingbird plant. Riparian and canyon edges.
shrubs.hp: Golden currant — fragrant yellow flowers. Riparian specialist. Berries ripen yellow to black — edible, used in jams.

shrubs.desc: Dark blue flowers on sticky-leaved shrub. Warty leaf surfaces give the name. Nitrogen fixer. Chaparral in Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Warty leaf surfaces are diagnostic. Obligate seeder — killed by fire but seeds germinate from heat-activated soil bank.

shrubs.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub of dry inland slopes. Fall bloomer. More common inland than coastal Isocoma. Important late-season pollinator resource.
shrubs.hp: Late-season nectar source for native bees and butterflies. Fire-adapted resprouter.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green shrub with masses of yellow fall flowers. Desert-mountain transition. Contains rubber — WWII rubber extraction attempted. Important fall pollinator plant.

fm.Cultural: Contains rubber — WWII extraction attempted

shrubs.hp: Critical fall nectar for native bees and migrating Monarchs. Pre-hibernation bumble bee queens depend on this late bloom (Xerces Society).

shrubs.desc: Spiny thorny shrub with small white flowers and small almonds. Desert washes. More drought-tolerant than other native Prunus. Indigenous food plant.
shrubs.hp: Indigenous food plant — bitter almonds eaten after leaching. Early spring bee forage in desert. Important bird food.

shrubs.desc: Dense thorny white-barked ceanothus of maritime chaparral. White flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Coast-facing slopes in Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Obligate seeder — killed by fire. Maritime chaparral specialist. White bark diagnostic.

shrubs.desc: Yellow sunflower-like heads on shrubby stems in coastal sage scrub. Distinguished from Encelia by deeply lobed leaves. Southern LA County.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — southern LA County

shrubs.hp: Year-round bloomer providing continuous nectar for native bees. Important Coastal Sage Scrub component.

shrubs.desc: Silver holly-shaped leaves on low desert shrub. One of the most drought and heat-tolerant plants — thrives in Death Valley. At western range edge in NE LA County.

fm.Habitat: Extreme desert — at western range edge in NE LA County
fm.Drought: Among most heat-tolerant plants on Earth

shrubs.hp: Among the most heat and drought-tolerant plants on Earth. Silver leaves reflect solar radiation. Important desert habitat structure.

shrubs.desc: Small citrus-family shrub with white flowers. Strong citrus scent when leaves crushed. Very rare in LA County — at northern range limit on coast-facing slopes.

fm.Fragrance: Strong citrus scent — crushed leaves smell like lemon
fm.Habitat: Coastal slopes — at northern range limit

shrubs.hp: Only member of citrus family native to SoCal chaparral. Aromatic oils attract native bees. Rare at northern range limit.

shrubs.desc: Completely white-woolly paintbrush endemic to Channel Islands. White bracts — unusual for paintbrush. Maritime chaparral.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Hemiparasitic on neighboring shrub roots. Unusual white color in a genus dominated by red and orange.

shrubs.desc: Close relative of Matilija Poppy with bristly-hairy calyx (vs smooth in R. coulteri). Large white flowers. Very rare — a few LA County canyon populations.

fm.Flowers: Large white, yellow center — like R. coulteri
fm.Calyx: Bristly-hairy (vs smooth in R. coulteri — diagnostic)

shrubs.hp: Close relative of Matilija Poppy distinguished by bristly calyx. Fire follower. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Very rare.

shrubs.desc: Island form of Holly-leaf Cherry with larger leaves, fruit, and tree-like form. Planted widely on mainland as ornamental. Native to Channel Islands.

fm.Fruit: Large dark cherries — edible flesh around stone
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands native; widely planted on mainland

shrubs.hp: Island subspecies with larger fruit eaten by many bird species. Widely planted on mainland for wildlife gardens.

shrubs.desc: Pink flowers on spiny thorny shrub at desert-mountain transition. Small fuzzy peach-like fruit. At extreme southern range limit in NE LA County.

fm.Habitat: Desert-mountain transition — at southern range limit

shrubs.hp: Early spring bee forage in desert-mountain transition. Small fruits eaten by birds and ground squirrels.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green leafless desert shrub. Ancient gymnosperm. Distinguished from E. californica by gray-green (not bright green) stems. High desert.

fm.Ecology: Gymnosperm — ancient lineage

shrubs.hp: Ancient gymnosperm lineage. Stems brewed as 'Mormon Tea' by early settlers. Desert habitat structure for small reptiles and mammals.

shrubs.desc: White woolly desert shrub — important winter browse for livestock and wildlife. Desert flats and alkaline areas at NE LA County edge.

fm.Ecology: Critical winter browse — name refers to livestock fattening

shrubs.hp: Critical winter browse for mule deer, pronghorn, and jackrabbits. White woolly stems provide winter food when other forage is scarce.

shrubs.desc: White rose-like flowers followed by dramatic feathery pink seed plumes. Desert-mountain transition. Extremely showy seed heads persist for months.

fm.Seeds: Feathery pink plumes — dramatic (persist for months)

shrubs.hp: Feathery pink seed plumes are among the showiest of any desert shrub. Visited by native bees. Important for erosion control.
