shrubs.desc: Fragrant cream-yellow rose-family flowers on evergreen shrub. Important desert browse. At extreme western range limit at NE LA County edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert-mountain transition — at western range limit
fm.Ecology: Important wildlife browse

shrubs.hp: Important browse for mule deer. Fragrant flowers visited by native bees. At extreme western range limit in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Hairy-leaved manzanita of interior chaparral. Distinguished from A. glandulosa by non-glandular hairs and birch-like leaves. SGM north slopes.
shrubs.hp: Early-season bumble bee forage — blooms Dec–Mar. Obligate seeder killed by fire. Berries eaten by birds and coyotes.

shrubs.desc: Parasitic plant with no chlorophyll — entirely dependent on host plant roots. Purple-brown flower spikes emerge from ground near host (usually Isocoma or Eriodictyon). Coastal sage scrub.

fm.Ecology: Root parasite — no chlorophyll; parasitizes CSS shrubs

shrubs.hp: Holoparasite — entirely dependent on host plant roots for all nutrients. No chlorophyll. Indicator of intact native shrub communities.

shrubs.desc: Large rosette buckwheat with red fruits. Channel Islands endemic — planted on mainland for restoration. White to pink flower clusters. Important butterfly nectar.

fm.Color: White to pink clusters, then red fruit

shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Important nectar source for native bees and butterflies. Red fruits eaten by island birds.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Crofton Weed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Highly invasive Mexican shrub (Cal-IPC Limited) spreading through riparian and shaded canyon habitats. Forms dense monocultures excluding native understory. Toxic to horses (Numinbah disease).

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Garland Daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native garland daisy from Mediterranean — showy golden flowers but forms dense stands displacing native coastal sage scrub species.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. American century plant — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native century plant from Mexico. Despite the name, blooms after 10–30 years (not 100), produces massive flower stalk to 25 ft, then dies. Pups spread vegetatively.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Lawn daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native lawn daisy from Europe. Ubiquitous in irrigated lawns. Edible flowers. Low ecological impact — mostly confined to maintained landscapes.

shrubs.desc: rough cocklebur — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native cocklebur with distinctive hooked bur fruits. Important waterfowl food despite being toxic to livestock. Burs disperse on animal fur and clothing.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Common Cotula — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Australian cotula forming dense mats in lawns and disturbed ground. Tiny button flowers. Low ecological impact — mostly confined to urban areas.

shrubs.desc: Big Sagebrush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native big sagebrush — foundation species of the Intermountain West. Dominant shrub at desert-mountain transition in NE LA County. Critical sage grouse habitat (not in LA). Extremely aromatic.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Common Asparagus Fern — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native common asparagus fern (not a true fern) — delicate feathery foliage. Bird-dispersed red berries spreading into shaded canyons. Smothering native understory.

shrubs.desc: whitedaisy tidytips — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native whitedaisy tidytips with showy white ray flowers tipped yellow. Important bee pollinator plant. Grassland and open woodland specialist.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Belladonna Lily — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native belladonna lily from South Africa — dramatic pink flowers on bare stems in late summer ('naked lady'). Toxic bulb. Extremely drought-tolerant once established.

shrubs.desc: Invasive non-native. horseweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Native horseweed — one of the first plants to evolve glyphosate resistance. Pioneer on disturbed ground. Wind-dispersed seeds travel long distances.

shrubs.desc: Invasive non-native. Flax-leaved Horseweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. South American fleabane naturalizing on disturbed ground worldwide. Among the most common urban weeds in LA. Evolving herbicide resistance.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. common groundsel — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native groundsel — common year-round weed of gardens and disturbed areas. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock. Wind-dispersed seeds.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. prickly sowthistle — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native prickly sowthistle. Common garden weed with milky sap. Seeds wind-dispersed. Visited by native bees. Important early-season food for goldfinches.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Cretanweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Cretanweed from Mediterranean — becoming common in disturbed coastal areas. Small yellow dandelion-like flowers. Increasing concern as grassland invader.

shrubs.desc: silver rock-lettuce — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native silver rock-lettuce forming distinctive silvery rosettes on rock faces and road cuts. Milky latex. Important habitat specialist — indicator of undisturbed rocky slopes.

shrubs.desc: Bigelow's tickseed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Bigelow's tickseed forming brilliant yellow desert carpet displays in good wildflower years. Important desert pollinator resource during spring bloom.

shrubs.desc: Nevin's Woolly Sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Nevin's woolly sunflower — Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic. Large woolly heads. Rare — few mainland populations remain. Conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: desert dandelion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native desert dandelion forming low yellow carpets in Mojave and Colorado Desert. Important spring bee forage. Fire follower in some habitats.

shrubs.desc: slender sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native slender sunflower of chaparral and oak woodland margins. Important late-season nectar source for native bees and butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Island Tarplant — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Island tarplant — Channel Islands endemic. Sticky resinous stems. Important pollinator resource on Channel Islands. Limited mainland populations.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. African Asparagus — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native African asparagus — aggressive spreader via bird-dispersed berries. Dense root mat excludes native plants. Active removal programs in coastal habitats.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Cornflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native cornflower — iconic blue wildflower of European grain fields. Garden escape occasionally naturalizing on disturbed ground. Important bee forage.

shrubs.desc: redray alpinegold — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native redray alpinegold — showy mountain wildflower with red-centered yellow rays. Alpine specialist above 6,000 ft in SGM. Important high-elevation pollinator plant.

shrubs.desc: island senecio — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native island senecio — Channel Islands endemic now planted on mainland. Succulent leaves. Important for island pollinator communities. Drought-tolerant.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Smooth Cat's Ear — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native smooth cat's ear — common lawn and grassland weed from Mediterranean. Dandelion-like but leaves are smoother. Displaces native annuals.

shrubs.desc: Valley Lessingia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Valley lessingia — purple-pink heads in open grasslands and disturbed areas. Important late-season bee forage. Glandular-sticky stems.

shrubs.desc: Emory's rockdaisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Emory's rockdaisy — small annual of desert rock crevices. Among the first desert annuals to bloom after rain. Important early bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Hairy Beggarticks — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native tropical beggartick with barbed seeds that adhere to clothing and animal fur. Colonizes disturbed moist areas. Used medicinally in some cultures.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Onion-Leafed Asphodel — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native onion-leafed asphodel — Mediterranean weed spreading rapidly in desert and coastal sage scrub. Forms dense monocultures. Cal-IPC Watch List.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. feverfew — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native feverfew — traditional migraine remedy (contains parthenolide). White daisy flowers. Garden escape naturalizing in disturbed areas.

shrubs.desc: Blair's munzothamnus — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Blair's munzothamnus — Channel Islands endemic shrub in the lettuce tribe. Known only from San Clemente Island. Rare. Conservation priority.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Yellow Iris — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native yellow iris — invasive in wetlands. Dense rhizome mats exclude native marsh plants. All parts contain irisin glycoside toxin. Cal-IPC rated.

shrubs.desc: crowned muilla — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native crowned muilla — small onion relative of desert and foothill grasslands. Important early-season bee pollinator plant. Easily overlooked.

shrubs.desc: Santolina pincushion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native santolina pincushion of mountain forests. Woolly leaves. Important mountain pollinator plant visited by native bees and butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Cape Smilax — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Cape smilax (bridal creeper) — aggressive vine from South Africa smothering native vegetation. Bird-dispersed berries. Serious ecological threat.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Spring starflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native spring starflower from South America — pale blue star flowers in lawns. Garlic-scented foliage. Garden escape. Low ecological impact.

shrubs.desc: Invasive non-native. Stinkwort — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Rapidly spreading stinkwort — strong unpleasant odor. Contact causes skin dermatitis. Spreading explosively along CA roadsides since 2000s.

shrubs.desc: Santa Susana tarplant — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Santa Susana tarplant — STATE RARE. Known only from sandstone outcrops in western San Fernando Valley. Seriously threatened by development. Conservation priority.

shrubs.desc: firewheel — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native firewheel — showy red-and-yellow pinwheel flowers. Important pollinator plant. Increasingly planted in wildflower mixes and naturalizing.

shrubs.desc: San Clemente Island brodiaea — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native San Clemente Island brodiaea — Channel Islands endemic. Purple-blue flowers. Federally listed as endangered. Very few populations remain.

shrubs.desc: Scale Bud — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native scale bud — distinctive desert annual with dandelion-like heads surrounded by papery bracts. Mojave Desert specialist. Good wildflower year indicator.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. cowpen daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native cowpen daisy — aggressive yellow daisy spreading along roadsides and washes. Forms dense stands. Native to central US, expanding range westward.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Mexican Fleabane — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Mexican fleabane widely planted in LA gardens. Cascading habit. Naturalizing on walls and rocky areas but relatively benign ecologically.

shrubs.desc: Wedgeleaf Goldenbush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native wedgeleaf goldenbush of rocky outcrops. Important late-season nectar source for native bees. Rock crevice specialist.

shrubs.desc: San Diego Wirelettuce — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native San Diego wirelettuce — slender stems with small pink ray flowers. Important nectar source in coastal sage scrub. Milky latex.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Rush Skeletonweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native rush skeletonweed — Cal-IPC Moderate. Deep-rooted perennial spreading via milky-sapped runners. Extremely difficult to eradicate. Agricultural weed.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Wedding Treasureflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native South African gazania — common ornamental ground cover escaping to roadsides and coastal bluffs. Orange-yellow flowers. Displaces native annuals.

shrubs.desc: California cottonrose — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native California cottonrose — inconspicuous woolly annual of open areas. Despite being tiny, plays important ecological role in soil crust formation.

shrubs.desc: Leafy Desertdandelion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native leafy desert-dandelion — Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic. Yellow flower heads on leafy stems. Conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Early Onion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native early onion — among the first spring wildflowers in coastal sage scrub. Pink-white flowers. Important early-season bee forage.

shrubs.desc: pumice alpinegold — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native pumice alpinegold — mountain specialist of volcanic and granitic soils above 6,000 ft. Yellow ray flowers. Alpine adaptation to harsh conditions.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. pot marigold — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native pot marigold — edible orange flowers. Traditional medicinal herb. Occasional garden escape. Minimal ecological impact.

shrubs.desc: Pringle's woolly sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Pringle's woolly sunflower — tiny desert annual forming yellow carpets in good wildflower years. Mojave Desert specialist.

shrubs.desc: Fleshy Pincushion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native fleshy pincushion — desert annual with white to pale pink pincushion heads. Named for botanist Louis Xantus.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Yellow Cosmos — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native yellow cosmos from Mexico — garden escape with orange-yellow flowers. Occasionally naturalizing on disturbed ground. Pollinator-friendly.

shrubs.desc: Common Madia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native common madia — sticky tarweed with showy yellow ray flowers. Important late-season native bee and butterfly forage. Tar-like resin on stems.

shrubs.desc: San Clemente Island triteleia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native San Clemente Island triteleia — Channel Islands endemic with blue-purple flowers. Very rare. Federally listed. Conservation priority.

shrubs.desc: Spineless Yucca — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native spineless yucca from Central America — common LA ornamental. Lacks the sharp-pointed leaves of native yuccas. No obligate moth pollinator relationship.

shrubs.desc: dwarf coastweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native dwarf coastweed — small coastal annual with yellow button flowers. Important component of coastal strand and bluff plant communities.

shrubs.desc: Hairy Horsebrush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native hairy horsebrush — white-woolly desert shrub. Toxic to sheep when combined with sagebrush browsing (causes 'bighead' disease). Mojave Desert.

shrubs.desc: everlasting neststraw — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native everlasting neststraw — tiny woolly annual of open areas. Among the most inconspicuous CA native plants but ecologically significant in soil ecology.

shrubs.desc: Greata's aster — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Greata's aster — LA County area endemic! Purple-rayed fall-blooming aster of shady mountain canyons. Named for a local botanist. Conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: California Goldenbush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native California goldenbush — low shrub of coastal dunes and bluffs. Important late-season nectar. Stabilizes sand. Coastal specialist.

shrubs.desc: Burlew's Onion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Burlew's onion — mountain endemic with pink-purple flowers. Rocky slopes in SGM and SMM. Important early-season bee pollinator plant.

shrubs.desc: Cheesebush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native cheesebush — low desert shrub with pungent odor when crushed. Important desert habitat structure. Visited by specialist native bees.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. chicory — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native chicory — striking blue flowers along roadsides. Tap root used as coffee substitute. European origin. Important bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. red-seeded dandelion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native red-seeded dandelion — similar to common dandelion but with red-brown seeds. European. Less common than T. officinale in LA.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. purple salsify — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native purple salsify — edible root (oyster plant). Spectacular dandelion-like seed heads. European. Roadside colonizer.

shrubs.desc: Pappose Tarweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native pappose tarweed — annual of seasonal wetland margins and grasslands. Sticky-glandular. Declining due to habitat loss. Some subspecies rare.

shrubs.desc: san clemente island hazardia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native San Clemente Island hazardia — Channel Islands endemic shrub. Gray-woolly leaves. Conservation priority. Very limited distribution.

shrubs.desc: Mojave hole-in-the-sand plant — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Mojave hole-in-the-sand plant — grows through sand surface with only flower heads visible. Strong creosote-like scent. Fascinating desert adaptation.

shrubs.desc: False Daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native false daisy — small white-flowered plant of moist disturbed areas. Used in traditional medicine (Eclipta alba) for hair darkening in Asian traditions.

shrubs.desc: western marsh cudweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native western marsh cudweed — woolly annual of seasonal wet areas. Inconspicuous but ecologically important in vernal pool communities.

shrubs.desc: Plummer's baccharis — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Plummer's baccharis — coastal sage scrub shrub endemic to SoCal. Important late-season pollinator resource. Conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Spanish False Fleabane — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Spanish false fleabane — Mediterranean weed spreading in moist disturbed areas. Yellow daisy flowers. Increasing in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Camphor — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native camphor tree — recently split from Cinnamomum. Aromatic camphor-scented leaves. Widely planted LA street tree. Naturalizing in some riparian areas.

shrubs.desc: San Bernardino Mountains onion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native San Bernardino Mountains onion — mountain endemic. Pink-purple flowers on rocky slopes above 5,000 ft. Important early-season bee pollinator.

shrubs.desc: spiny cocklebur — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native spiny cocklebur — triple-spined nodes and bur fruits. More aggressively spiny than native X. strumarium. Livestock hazard. Roadside weed.

shrubs.desc: Dunn's Lobelia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Dunn's lobelia — slender blue-flowered perennial of moist mountain meadows and seeps. Important for mountain pollinators. Declining.

shrubs.desc: Fringed Onion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native fringed onion of rocky chaparral slopes. Distinctive fringed tepals. Important early-season native bee forage. Several varieties.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. stinknet — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native stinknet — rapidly spreading South African weed. Strong unpleasant odor. Causes contact dermatitis. Active eradication programs in AZ and CA deserts.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. tropical horseweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native tropical horseweed — similar to E. canadensis but with broader leaves. Becoming one of the most common urban weeds globally. Herbicide resistance evolving.

shrubs.desc: Fremont's syntrichopappus — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Fremont's syntrichopappus — small desert annual with yellow ray flowers. Mojave Desert specialist. Good spring bloom indicator.

shrubs.desc: southern mule's ears — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native southern mule's ears — large-leaved perennial of mountain meadows with showy yellow sunflower heads. Important mountain pollinator resource.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Prostrate Capeweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native prostrate capeweed — mat-forming South African daisy invading coastal dunes and disturbed areas. Yellow flowers. Displacing native strand plants.

shrubs.desc: snake's-head — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native snake's-head — distinctive nodding yellow buds resemble snake heads. Mojave Desert annual. Spring bloom carpet species.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. narrowleaf cottonrose — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native narrowleaf cottonrose — tiny Mediterranean weed spreading in open grasslands. Woolly-white stems. Displacing native Logfia species.

shrubs.desc: malibu baccharis — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Malibu baccharis — locally endemic shrub known from Santa Monica Mountains area. White flower puffs in fall. Conservation concern due to limited range.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Naples garlic — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Naples garlic — showy white star flowers. Mediterranean. Garden escape colonizing road cuts and grassy areas.

shrubs.desc: seaside daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native seaside daisy — showy lavender-blue flowers on coastal bluffs. Important coastal pollinator plant. Popular in native gardens. Salt-tolerant.

shrubs.desc: cottonbatting plant — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native cottonbatting plant — woolly-white everlasting of grasslands. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds. Retains form when dried.

shrubs.desc: Mojave cottonthorn — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Mojave cottonthorn — woolly desert shrub of high-desert flats. Important habitat structure for desert reptiles.

shrubs.desc: Coulter's horseweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Coulter's horseweed — annual of washes and disturbed areas. Wind-dispersed seeds. Pioneer on recently disturbed ground.

shrubs.desc: Rayless Goldenhead — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native rayless goldenhead — rounded yellow button heads on low desert shrub. Important fall nectar source in Joshua tree woodland.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Cape marigold — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Cape marigold — orange-yellow daisies. South African. Common garden flower escaping to disturbed areas.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. African flag — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native African flag — orange tubular flowers attracting hummingbirds. South African. Garden escape naturalizing on road cuts.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. common soliva — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native lawn burweed — spiny fruits that embed painfully in bare feet. South American. Common in irrigated lawns and parks.

shrubs.desc: prairie sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native prairie sunflower — annual of sandy soils. Important native bee forage. Distinguished from common sunflower by smaller size.

shrubs.desc: cottonthorn — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native cottonthorn — rigid spiny desert shrub. Extremely toxic to sheep. Important structure for desert wildlife. Woolly leaves.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Summer snowflake — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native summer snowflake — white bell flowers. European bulb garden escape. Persists for decades in old garden sites.

shrubs.desc: Coast Range triteleia — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native dark triteleia — distinctive dark blue-purple flowers. Shaded woodland slopes. Among the darkest-flowered native bulbs.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Blue Pigroot — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native blue pigroot — small blue flowers. South American. Naturalizing in disturbed moist areas.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Stinking chamomile — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native stinking chamomile — similar to true chamomile but with foul odor and no medicinal properties. Skin irritant. Roadside weed.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. blessed thistle — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native blessed thistle — spiny Mediterranean herb historically used medicinally. Now a roadside weed in disturbed areas.

shrubs.desc: tehachapi ragwort — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Tehachapi ragwort — yellow daisy of mountain openings. Important pollinator resource at mid-elevations.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. trailing African daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native trailing African daisy — purple daisies. South African ground cover escaping from gardens to coastal bluffs.

shrubs.desc: Spineless Horsebrush — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native spineless horsebrush — gray-woolly non-spiny desert shrub. Important browse for pronghorn and mule deer.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Scarlet Kammetjie — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native scarlet freesia — red tubular flowers. South African. Garden escape persisting in moist shaded areas.

shrubs.desc: grassland silverpuffs — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native grassland silverpuffs — dandelion-like puff balls in grasslands. CA endemic. Important indicator of intact grassland.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Oriental false hawksbeard — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Oriental false hawksbeard — tiny yellow composite of shaded disturbed areas. Asian origin. Rapidly spreading urban weed.

shrubs.desc: thread-leaved brodiaea — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native thread-leaved brodiaea — FEDERALLY THREATENED. Disappearing due to development. Important for vernal pool habitat restoration.

shrubs.desc: slender woolly-marbles — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native slender woolly-marbles — tiny woolly annual of vernal pool margins. Inconspicuous but important vernal pool indicator species.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Trailing bellflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native trailing bellflower — lavender star flowers cascading over walls. European garden escape. Minimal ecological impact.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Common Cat's-ear — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native common cat's-ear — dandelion-like rosette weed of lawns and grasslands. Distinguished from dandelion by branched flower stems and hairy leaves.

shrubs.desc: Chinchweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native chinchweed — aromatic desert annual with lemon-scented foliage. Mojave Desert specialist. Important desert bee forage after summer rains.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Paperwhite — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native paperwhite narcissus — strongly fragrant white flowers. Mediterranean. Common garden bulb occasionally persisting in old garden sites.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. False Hawkbit — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native false hawkbit — Mediterranean weed with pale yellow dandelion-like flowers. Spreading along roadsides.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Onion Weed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native onion weed — white star flowers from grass-like leaves. South American bulb. Extremely persistent once established. Faint garlic scent.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Three-cornered garlic — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native three-cornered garlic — distinctive triangular flower stems with drooping white bells. Strong garlic scent. European. Spreading in moist shade.

shrubs.desc: common hillside daisy — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native common hillside daisy — showy yellow carpets on grassy slopes in good wildflower years. Important bee forage during spring bloom.

shrubs.desc: Fragrant Everlasting — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native fragrant everlasting — woolly-white plant with sweet vanilla fragrance. Important for native bee nesting material.

shrubs.desc: willow baccharis — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native willow baccharis — riparian shrub similar to B. salicifolia. Important streamside habitat structure. Visited by native bees when flowering.

shrubs.desc: Heterotheca species — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native goldenaster — yellow daisy of dry slopes. Late-season bloomer providing fall nectar for native bees.

shrubs.desc: common cocklebur — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native common cocklebur — bur-fruited annual of moist disturbed areas. Burs disperse on animal fur. Seeds toxic in early growth stage.

shrubs.desc: Brownplume Wirelettuce — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native brownplume wirelettuce — slender desert perennial with pink ray flowers. Important late-season nectar source for desert pollinators.

shrubs.desc: slimleaf bursage — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native slimleaf bursage — desert shrub providing habitat structure for lizards and small mammals. Wind-pollinated — contributes to desert allergy season.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. White rain-lily — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native white rain-lily — white crocus-like flowers after rain. South African. Garden escape. Low ecological impact.

shrubs.desc: Devil's Beggarticks — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native devil's beggarticks — barbed flat seeds ('stick-tights') dispersed on clothing and animal fur. Moist disturbed areas. Important waterfowl food.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Harlequin flower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native harlequin flower — tricolored flowers. South African. Garden escape. Low ecological impact.

shrubs.desc: annual saltmarsh aster — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native annual saltmarsh aster — slender fall-blooming aster of moist saline soils. Important late-season nectar in salt marsh habitats.

shrubs.desc: Lemmon's syntrichopappus — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Lemmon's syntrichopappus — tiny yellow-rayed annual of desert sandy soils. Mojave specialist.

shrubs.desc: pitted onion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native pitted onion — several varieties on rocky slopes. Pink-purple flowers. Important early native bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Q-tips — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Q-tips — tiny woolly annual with distinctive woolly round heads resembling cotton swabs. Among the smallest composites. Grassland specialist.

shrubs.desc: Bigelow's sneezeweed — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Bigelow's sneezeweed — showy yellow mountain wildflower of wet meadows. Reflexed ray petals distinctive. Important mountain pollinator plant.

shrubs.desc: white rabbit-tobacco — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Small Wirelettuce — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Common Spikeweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Island Sagebrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Bunch-flowered Daffodil — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: black-eyed Susan — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Safflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Russian knapweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Desert Pincushion — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: poverty weed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. plains coreopsis — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Inyo threadplant — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: deltoid balsamroot — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: yellow tack-stem — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Yellow Salsify — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: California desertdandelion — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Silver Wormwood — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. blue-and-white daisybush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Garden Lobelia — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Parry's Wirelettuce — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Dwarf Brodiaea — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Willowleaf Lettuce — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. bristleleaf pricklyleaf — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native Dahlberg daisy — tiny aromatic yellow daisies. Mexican. Popular ornamental occasionally naturalizing.

shrubs.desc: Heermann's tarweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. wild asparagus — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Dune Kammetjie — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: low woolly marbles — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: brewer's fleabane — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Opposite-leaved Tarweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. petite-licorice — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Thorny Skeletonweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Red Sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Bietou — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: woolly fishhooks — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. field marigold — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: sticky snakeweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: desert threadplant — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Throatwort — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Bicolor Cobra Lily — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: coast tarweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Kellogg's Tarweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Sea Dahlia — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Arizona cottonrose — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Trailing Treasureflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Red Hot Poker — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Stinking iris — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Mauritius hemp — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Karoo Tulp — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Swan-neck Agave — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. common ragweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: newhall sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. grey-leaved euryops — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Nemacladus species — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Palos Verdes Daisy — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. soap aloe — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: New Mexico thistle — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Pennsylvania Cudweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: featherweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: holly dandelion — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native Mojave keyflower — small desert annual with intricately lobed leaves pressed flat to ground. Extremely inconspicuous desert specialist.

shrubs.desc: California Coreopsis — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. upright prairie coneflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: littleleaf horsebrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Paris Daisy — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: White beggarticks — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Turkish Iris — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: California ragwort — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Bud Sagebrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Desert Twinbugs — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Leafy stemmed coreopsis — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Venus's looking-glass — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Mexican sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Fern-leaf Yarrow — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native fernleaf yarrow — flat golden flower clusters. Asian. Garden plant. Important bee forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. nipplewort — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: California sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native California sunflower — tall perennial of moist areas. Large yellow heads. Important native bee and butterfly forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. garden cosmos — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Sweetbush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Brazilian bachelor's button — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Green Rabbitbrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. rain daisy — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: velvet groundsel — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: shrubby alkali aster — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. New Zealand cabbage tree — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Grape hyacinth — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Garlic Chives — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Sprenger's Asparagus — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Thread agave — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Large Fortnight Lily — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Bay laurel — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. shaggy soldier — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: wirestem — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: alkali goldenbush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Cleveland's desert dandelion — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: San Gabriel bluecup — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Canary Island Marguerite — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Non-native fennel-leaved daisy — feathery foliage with white daisies. Canary Islands. Garden escape.

shrubs.desc: desert baccharis — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native desert baccharis — bright green rush-like stems. Desert washes and springs. Important habitat structure.

shrubs.desc: Yellow Rabbitbrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Beautiful Woolly Sunflower — a shrub found in LA County.
shrubs.hp: Native yellowray goldfields — tiny desert annual forming yellow carpets. Spring superbloom species.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Curlycup Gumweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Broom Snakeweed — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Island hazardia — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. max chrysanthemum — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: sowthistle desert dandelion — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. German Chamomile — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Nuttall's threadplant — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: annual turtleback — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Creeping Bellflower — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: fringed sagebrush — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Tricolor Daisy — a shrub found in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Non-native. Succulent Bush Ragwort — a shrub found in LA County.

grasses.desc: California state grass. Once dominant across millions of acres of CA grassland — now reduced to fragments by exotic annual grasses.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands, oak woodland — the iconic California bunchgrass
fm.Conservation: CA state grass — once dominant, now rare
fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with long twisted awns — awns 2-3 in (longer than S. lepida)
fm.vs: Foothill Needlegrass (S. lepida) is smaller (1-2 ft), finer, with shorter awns, and prefers drier slopes

grasses.hp: California state grass. Once dominant across millions of acres — now reduced to fragments by exotic annual grasses. Deep roots stabilize soil and sequester carbon.

grasses.desc: Large bunchgrass forming dense tussocks. Important basket-weaving material for indigenous peoples. Stream margins.

fm.Cultural: Used in Tongva/Chumash basketry

grasses.hp: Important basket-weaving material for Tongva and Chumash peoples. Dense tussocks provide ground-nesting bird habitat. Stream margin stabilizer.

grasses.desc: Massive native grass to 10 ft. Forms tall clumps in coastal and foothill areas. Fire-resprouting. Important erosion control plant.

fm.Ecology: Fire-resprouting, erosion control

grasses.hp: Massive native grass providing cover for ground-nesting birds and small mammals. Fire-adapted — vigorous rhizome resprouting. Important coastal erosion control.

grasses.desc: Native perennial bunchgrass of oak woodland understory. Finer than Purple Needlegrass. More shade-tolerant.

fm.Habitat: Dry foothill slopes, coastal sage scrub
fm.Inflorescence: Slender open panicle with sharp-awned seeds — awns shorter than S. pulchra
fm.vs: Purple Needlegrass (S. pulchra) is taller (2-3 ft), more robust, with longer 2-3 in awns

grasses.hp: Native bunchgrass providing habitat structure for ground-nesting birds and small mammals. Seeds eaten by sparrows, finches, and quail. More shade-tolerant than S. pulchra.

grasses.desc: Blue-green native grass of woodland openings. Important forage grass. Relatively shade-tolerant for a grass.
grasses.hp: Blue wild rye — important native grass for restoration. Shade-tolerant. Seeds eaten by sparrows. Key grassland understory species.

grasses.desc: Dominant lawn and park grass across LA. African/Asian origin. Extremely aggressive — impossible to eradicate once established.
grasses.hp: Non-native Bermuda grass — one of the world's most widespread lawn grasses. Extremely aggressive via stolons and rhizomes. Pollen allergen.

grasses.desc: European annual grass that dominates most CA grasslands. Golden in summer. Largely replaced native perennial bunchgrasses.

fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — annual grass creates flash fuel

grasses.hp: Dominant invasive — largely replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie statewide. The golden grass defining California's summer hillsides is not native.

grasses.desc: Aggressive annual grass with sharp-awned seeds that penetrate animal skin. Fire-promoting — increases fire frequency in native habitats.

fm.Awns: Long, sharp — can injure livestock mouths
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, roadsides — dominant invasive grass in coastal LA
fm.Ecology: Fire-promoting invasive
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — creates continuous fine fuel layer; increases fire frequency
fm.Inflorescence: Large open drooping panicle with stiff awns — spikelets to 3 in long (larger than Red Brome)
fm.vs: Red Brome (B. madritensis) is smaller, more compact, with reddish-purple tinge at maturity

grasses.hp: Non-native fire-cycle driver — creates continuous fine fuel that increases fire frequency in native chaparral and sage scrub, converting shrubland to annual grassland.

grasses.desc: Massive bamboo-like grass to 30 ft choking riparian corridors. Active removal programs.

fm.Ecology: Consumes enormous water
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — burns intensely; resprouts rapidly after fire

grasses.hp: Among the most damaging invasive plants in California riparian corridors. Consumes enormous water, displaces native willows, and burns intensely. Active removal statewide.

grasses.desc: Low-growing native grass of salt marshes and alkaline flats. Tolerates extreme salt concentrations that kill most plants. Key species for coastal wetland restoration. Spreads by rhizomes.
grasses.hp: Key salt marsh restoration species — tolerates extreme salinity. Rhizomatous spread stabilizes marsh substrate. Habitat for salt marsh harvest mouse.

grasses.desc: Native bunchgrass with distinctive sideways-hanging seed heads resembling eyelashes. Extremely drought-tolerant.
grasses.hp: Native blue grama grass — extremely drought-tolerant with deep root systems. Important for soil carbon sequestration. Forms dense turf in desert grasslands.

grasses.desc: Delicate purple-tinged bunchgrass with three-pronged awns. Desert and dry foothill grasslands.
grasses.hp: Native purple three-awn with distinctive three-pronged seed. Desert grassland species. Important soil stabilizer on arid slopes.

grasses.desc: Large bunchgrass of alkaline flats. Open airy seed heads. Important for alkali meadow restoration.
grasses.hp: Native alkali sacaton — extremely salt-tolerant grass of alkaline flats and desert springs. Important for salt marsh and alkaline wetland restoration.

grasses.desc: Native meadow grass of moist areas. Short bristly heads. Important component of native grass restoration.
grasses.hp: Meadow barley — moist meadows and alkali flats. One of few native barley species. Important wetland indicator.

grasses.desc: Tough dark green stems in wet areas. Round stems (not triangular like sedges). Important wetland component.
grasses.hp: Wire rush — dark green round stems. Important for wetland restoration. Indigenous peoples used stems for basket weaving.

grasses.desc: Fine-textured native grass used as lawn alternative. Much less water than Kentucky Bluegrass. Coastal and foothill meadows.
grasses.hp: Bent grass — fine-textured native grass of coastal bluffs and meadows. Important soil binder on sandy soils.

grasses.desc: Native bunchgrass with nodding awns. More compact than Purple Needlegrass. Dry grasslands and openings.
grasses.hp: Nodding needlegrass — graceful drooping inflorescence. Deep-rooted native bunchgrass. Important for California grassland restoration.

grasses.desc: Elegant desert bunchgrass with large nutritious seeds. Major food source for indigenous peoples — ground into flour. Sandy desert flats.

fm.Cultural: Staple food for Paiute and other peoples

grasses.hp: Indian ricegrass — highly nutritious seeds were staple indigenous food. Important desert wildlife forage. Drought-tolerant.

grasses.desc: Rhizomatous native grass forming sod-like patches. Tolerates seasonal flooding and alkaline soil. Restoration workhorse.

fm.Ecology: Important restoration species

grasses.hp: Creeping wild rye — rhizomatous. Important for wetland and alkali site restoration. Salt-tolerant. Dense cover for nesting birds.

grasses.desc: Compact native bunchgrass with one-sided panicle. Very widespread — found coast to alpine. Drought-tolerant.
grasses.hp: One-sided bluegrass — extremely widespread native grass. Tolerates heavy soils. Important in intact grasslands.

grasses.desc: Attractive native bunchgrass with delicate one-sided panicles nodding from slender stems. Common in oak woodland understory and rocky slopes. Excellent drought-tolerant garden native and restoration species.
grasses.hp: California melic — graceful drooping spikelets in chaparral understory. One of few grasses thriving in shrub shade.

grasses.desc: Desert bunchgrass with delicate open panicles emerging from leaf sheaths. Important sand-stabilizing grass. Seeds eaten by birds.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, often partly enclosed in sheath
fm.Ecology: Sand stabilizer, bird food

grasses.hp: Sand dropseed — tiny hidden seeds. Important desert stabilizer on loose sandy soils. Forage for desert rodents.

grasses.desc: Robust native brome grass common on road cuts and woodland openings. Flat, somewhat drooping panicle. Important restoration species.

fm.Ecology: Important restoration species

grasses.hp: California brome — large native bunchgrass of woodland openings. Important for grassland restoration. Seeds eaten by quail.

grasses.desc: Feathery silver seed heads on tall cane-like stems. Native bunchgrass of dry rocky slopes. More common in SoCal than other bluestems.
grasses.hp: Cane bluestem — warm-season grass with distinctive silver-hairy nodes. Desert grassland specialist.

grasses.desc: Dense clumps of smooth cylindrical stems in wet areas. Flowers emerge from side of stem. One of the most common rushes in LA riparian areas. Not a true grass — rush family.

fm.Form: Dense clumps of smooth cylindrical stems
fm.Flowers: Small brown clusters emerging from stem side
fm.Ecology: Important wetland indicator species

grasses.hp: Soft rush — tall green cylindrical stems. Pith used for rush candles. Important marsh habitat structure.

grasses.desc: Iris-like flat leaves on rush stems. Wet meadows and stream margins. Among the most common LA County rushes. Not a grass — rush family.
grasses.hp: Iris-leaved rush — flat sword-shaped leaves. Distinguished from other rushes by iris-like foliage. Freshwater marsh edges.

grasses.desc: Slender dark sedge forming dense stands in moist meadows. Rhizomatous — spreads to form sod. Important restoration species for seasonal wetlands.

fm.Ecology: Important restoration species

grasses.hp: Native sedge of moist meadows and stream banks. Rhizomatous — forms dense sods. Important wetland restoration species. Provides cover for ground-nesting birds.

grasses.desc: Tiny native annual grass of grasslands and openings. One of the most common native grasses but completely overlooked due to small size.
grasses.hp: Small fescue — tiny delicate native annual. Important in intact native grassland communities. Short-lived.

grasses.desc: Bushy foxtail seed heads that break apart and embed in dog fur and socks. European invasive. Extremely common on every disturbed ground. Seeds can injure dogs' ears and feet.

fm.Toxicity: Seeds embed in dog fur/ears — veterinary hazard

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native foxtail barley whose barbed awns embed painfully in dog paws, ears, and noses. Common veterinary hazard. Dominates disturbed urban areas.

grasses.desc: Slender rat-tail-like seed heads. European invasive annual grass dominating disturbed grasslands. One of the most common grasses in LA County.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native rat-tail fescue — one-sided drooping spikes. Fine fuel creating continuous fire load in grasslands.

grasses.desc: The largest native needlegrass — tall dense clumps. Important bunchgrass of chaparral understory and oak woodland. Fire-adapted — resprouts vigorously.

fm.Ecology: Important bunchgrass — fire-adapted

grasses.hp: Native bunchgrass providing habitat structure for ground-nesting birds and small mammals. Seeds eaten by sparrows, finches, and quail. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

grasses.desc: Graceful drooping panicle with small spikelets. The most common woodland grass in LA County. Shady chaparral and oak woodland understory.

fm.Habitat: Shady chaparral/oak woodland understory — very common

grasses.hp: Native perennial grass of chaparral understory and rocky slopes. Important for erosion control on steep slopes. One of few native grasses thriving in chaparral shade.

grasses.desc: Green umbrella-like flower heads on triangular stems. Common in moist disturbed areas and stream margins. Not a grass — sedge family.
grasses.hp: Wetland habitat structure for marsh birds and invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Mediterranean grass invading LA chaparral and sage scrub. Dense clumps with drooping seed heads. Increasingly problematic — converting native shrubland to grassland.
grasses.hp: Non-native smilo grass — Mediterranean perennial displacing native bunchgrasses. Large drooping panicle. Less invasive than annual grasses.

grasses.desc: Tall native grass with distinctive bushy white seed heads in fall. Moist areas and seeps. Among the most visually striking native grasses.
grasses.hp: Bushy bluestem — dense fluffy seed heads. Moist areas and stream banks. Warm-season grass providing fall structure.

grasses.desc: Delicate purple-tinged native annual grass. Open disturbed areas. Often overlooked but common in grasslands and chaparral openings.
grasses.hp: Native annual muhly grass of rocky desert slopes. Important early-season seed source for desert birds.

grasses.desc: Tall smooth rush — the primary basket-weaving rush of indigenous SoCal peoples. Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla all used for basket construction. Disappearing from the LA River.

fm.Cultural: Primary basket rush of Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla — disappearing from LA River

grasses.hp: Primary basket-weaving rush of Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples — disappearing from the LA River due to channelization and water diversion.

grasses.desc: Tufted native bunchgrass of mountain meadows and moist grasslands. Important component of native grassland restoration. Wider leaves than needlegrasses.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with few large spikelets

grasses.hp: California oatgrass — important native grass of coastal and montane meadows. Indicator of intact grassland.

grasses.desc: Compact silvery spike on slender native bunchgrass. Dry grasslands. Named for its June peak bloom. Important component of California native grasslands.
grasses.hp: Native bunchgrass of mountain meadows. Important forage for wildlife. Less showy than other native grasses but ecologically important in intact grassland communities.

grasses.desc: South African grass aggressively invading LA County shaded habitats — oak woodland, riparian, north-facing slopes. Cal-IPC Moderate. Shade-tolerant, which makes it uniquely destructive among invasive grasses. Displacing native understory across the SMM and SGM.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with distinctive 6 glumes per spikelet
fm.Ecology: SHADE-TOLERANT invasive — uniquely destructive because it invades intact woodland understory
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, riparian, north-facing slopes — spreading rapidly
fm.vs: Perennial Veldt Grass (E. calycina) prefers sun and sandy soils; Upright Veldt Grass (E. erecta) invades SHADE

grasses.hp: Uniquely destructive among invasive grasses because it invades intact SHADED woodland understory — most invasive grasses need sun. Displacing native understory across SMM and SGM.

grasses.desc: South African grass invading coastal dunes and sandy habitats. Cal-IPC High. More sun-tolerant than E. erecta. Particularly destructive at El Segundo Dunes and coastal strand habitats.

fm.Ecology: Sand dune and coastal strand invader — FULL SUN species
fm.Habitat: Coastal dunes, sandy habitats — devastating at El Segundo Dunes
fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, spikelets with prominent hairy lemmas
fm.vs: Upright Veldt Grass (E. erecta) is shade-tolerant and invades woodland understory; Perennial Veldt (E. calycina) needs SUN

grasses.hp: Sand dune and coastal strand invader — particularly devastating at El Segundo Dunes, threatening habitat for the federally endangered El Segundo Blue butterfly.

grasses.desc: Giant South American grass with showy white plume seed heads to 10 ft. Cal-IPC High. Each plume produces 100,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Widely planted ornamentally, now invading coastal bluffs and disturbed areas.

fm.Inflorescence: WHITE plumes — requires pollination to set seed
fm.vs: Jubata Grass (C. jubata) has PURPLE-PINK plumes and reproduces asexually (no pollen needed)

grasses.hp: Each plume produces ~100,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Widely planted ornamentally but escaping to coastal bluffs where it displaces native vegetation.

grasses.desc: Reddish-purple annual brome dominating disturbed areas and desert grasslands. Cal-IPC High. Fuels fire cycle in desert — converts native shrubland to annual grassland through repeated burning.

fm.Inflorescence: Compact, reddish-purple at maturity (vs large and drooping in Ripgut)
fm.Ecology: Fire cycle driver in desert — converts shrubland to grassland
fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas, desert grasslands — extremely common
fm.vs: Ripgut Brome (B. diandrus) is taller with larger drooping spikelets; Red Brome is compact and purplish

grasses.hp: Fire-cycle driver in desert ecosystems — converts native shrubland to annual grassland through repeated burning. Dries 4–6 weeks before natives, creating early fire fuel.

grasses.desc: The West's most destructive invasive grass. Dries early, creating fire fuel weeks before natives. Has shortened fire return intervals from 60-110 years to 3-5 years across millions of acres. Present in SGM and Antelope Valley.

fm.Inflorescence: Drooping, purplish, with barbed awns that embed in socks
fm.Origin: Eurasian — Cal-IPC High. Most destructive invasive grass in western North America.
fm.Ecology: Dries 4-6 weeks before natives, creating continuous fire fuel. Shortened fire intervals from decades to years.
fm.Habitat: SGM slopes, Antelope Valley, disturbed areas

grasses.hp: The West's most destructive invasive grass. Shortened fire return intervals from 60–110 years to 3–5 years across millions of acres, converting sagebrush to grassland.

grasses.desc: Dominant annual grass of California grasslands — more common than A. fatua. Golden dried stalks define the California summer hillside. Replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie across the state.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with long twisted awns
fm.Ecology: Replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie statewide
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, hillsides — the golden grass of California summer

grasses.hp: Dominant invasive — replaced native perennial bunchgrass across California. More common than A. fatua. The golden summer hillsides are this species.

grasses.desc: Purple-pink plumed pampas grass — even more invasive than white pampas grass. Cal-IPC High. Reproduces asexually (no pollination needed). Each plant produces millions of wind-dispersed seeds. Coastal bluffs and disturbed slopes.

fm.Inflorescence: PURPLE-PINK plumes (vs white in C. selloana)
fm.Ecology: Asexual reproduction — no pollination needed. Even more invasive than C. selloana.
fm.vs: Pampas Grass (C. selloana) has WHITE plumes and requires pollination to seed

grasses.hp: Even more invasive than white pampas grass — reproduces asexually without pollination. Each plant produces millions of wind-dispersed seeds.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Fountain Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC High. Extreme fire hazard — burns hot and fast, converting native scrub to invasive grass-fire cycle. Rapidly reestablishes after burning. Active eradication programs statewide. Originally planted as ornamental.

grasses.desc: giant wild rye — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Giant native bunchgrass forming dramatic clumps to 10 ft. Important erosion control on slopes. Host for Orange Skipperling butterfly. Deep roots stabilize slopes. Indigenous peoples used stems for arrow shafts.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Umbrella Papyrus — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native umbrella sedge commonly escaped from water gardens into riparian areas. Spreads via rhizome fragments in flood debris. Provides some habitat structure but displaces native sedges.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Red Brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native annual grass converting native desert scrub to grassland through fire-grass cycle. Dries earlier than native plants, creating fine fuel. Among the most ecologically damaging invasive grasses in Mojave Desert.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Smilo Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native perennial grass from Mediterranean. Colonizes disturbed areas and roadsides. Less ecologically damaging than annual bromes but displaces native bunchgrasses.

grasses.desc: Non-native. goldentop grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Mediterranean grass with showy golden inflorescences. Common in disturbed areas and desert washes. Forms dense stands excluding native annuals.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. rabbitfoot grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native grass of moist disturbed areas. Fluffy seed heads diagnostic. Colonizes wetland margins, potentially displacing native rush and sedge communities.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. common soft brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Dominant invasive annual grass in coastal grasslands. Contributes to annual grass fire cycle that has converted millions of acres of native perennial grassland statewide.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Rescue Brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native South American brome occasionally used for erosion control. Large drooping spikelets. Less invasive than other bromes but colonizes disturbed riparian areas.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Dallis grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native South American grass naturalizing in moist disturbed areas and lawns. Sticky seed clusters adhere to clothing and animal fur for dispersal.

grasses.desc: spiny rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native spiny rush of coastal salt marshes and alkaline springs. Sharp-tipped leaves can puncture skin. Important nesting cover for marsh birds. Salt-tolerant.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Annual Meadow-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native annual grass — the most widespread grass weed globally. Ubiquitous in lawns, sidewalk cracks, and disturbed areas. Produces seed year-round.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. harding grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native perennial grass planted for forage but escaping to wetlands. Displaces native riparian vegetation. Contains alkaloids toxic to livestock (phalaris staggers).

grasses.desc: purple needlegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: California state grass. Deep-rooted native bunchgrass once covering millions of acres, now reduced to fragments by invasive annual grasses. Important for grassland restoration. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Italian Ryegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native annual ryegrass planted for erosion control and forage but outcompetes native grasses in wildlands. Rapidly establishes on disturbed ground.

grasses.desc: Desert Needlegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native desert needlegrass with long twisted awns. Important Mojave Desert bunchgrass. Deep roots stabilize sandy soils. Seeds eaten by desert birds and rodents.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Mexican feathergrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native ornamental feathergrass widely planted in LA landscapes. Spreading via wind-dispersed seeds into wildlands. Increasing concern as potential invasive.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Johnson grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native aggressive perennial grass spreading by both seeds and deep rhizomes. One of the world's worst agricultural weeds. Difficult to eradicate once established.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Goose Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native goose grass — one of the world's most common tropical weeds. Flat radiating stems diagnostic. Rapidly evolving herbicide resistance.

grasses.desc: Sand Ricegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native sand ricegrass with distinctive open panicle. Important desert wildlife food. Seeds highly nutritious — indigenous peoples gathered them extensively ('Indian ricegrass').

grasses.desc: Non-native. Sorghums — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native cultivated sorghum — occasional volunteer from birdseed and agricultural plantings. Fifth most important cereal crop globally.

grasses.desc: Non-native. feathertop — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native feathertop — ornamental grass with fluffy white seed heads. Less invasive than fountain grass but naturalizing in disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Common Barley — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native cultivated barley — ancient grain crop occasionally volunteering from birdseed or agriculture. Minimal ecological impact.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Natal Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native natal grass from Africa — pink-purple fluffy seed heads. Spreading on sandy disturbed areas. Fire-adapted. Increasing in SoCal.

grasses.desc: Spreading Rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native spreading rush — important streambank stabilizer. Common in restoration plantings. Dense tussocks provide ground-nesting bird cover.

grasses.desc: Squirreltail — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native squirreltail — awned bunchgrass of dry open habitats. Important rangeland species. Seeds eaten by ground squirrels.

grasses.desc: Creeping wild rye — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native creeping wild rye — rhizomatous. Important for wetland restoration. Salt-tolerant. Provides ground-nesting bird cover.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Perennial Ryegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native perennial ryegrass — ubiquitous lawn grass. Important pollen allergen. Rapidly establishing erosion control species.

grasses.desc: Non-native. orchard grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native orchard grass — European pasture grass. Important pollen allergen. Naturalizing in oak woodlands.

grasses.desc: crested needle grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native crested ricegrass — mountain bunchgrass with distinctive one-sided inflorescence. Rocky slopes above 4,000 ft.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Kikuyu Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native kikuyu grass — extremely aggressive African grass. Dense mats smother native vegetation. Very difficult to eradicate.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Bread Wheat — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native bread wheat — world's most important cereal crop. Occasional volunteer from birdseed or transport spills.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. common Mediterranean grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Non-native Mediterranean grass invading desert habitats. Creates continuous fine fuel in previously fuel-free desert, increasing fire frequency.

grasses.desc: foothill needle grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native foothill needlegrass — common native bunchgrass. More shade-tolerant than purple needlegrass. Important restoration species.

grasses.desc: Non-native. barnyardgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native barnyard grass — common annual of moist disturbed areas. Seeds important waterfowl food despite non-native origin.

grasses.desc: Non-native. longflower veldtgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native longflower veldtgrass — South African grass spreading in coastal areas. Less aggressive than E. erecta but expanding range.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Lesser Canary Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native lesser canary grass — Mediterranean. Important bird food (canary seed). Naturalizing in moist disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. rattail sixweeks grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native annual fescue now dominant in many CA grasslands. Fine stems create continuous fire fuel. Displaces native bunchgrasses.

grasses.desc: rock melic — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native rock melic — delicate native grass of rocky slopes. Important habitat structure in rock outcrops. Seeds eaten by sparrows.

grasses.desc: knotroot bristlegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native knotroot bristlegrass — warm-season grass of moist disturbed areas. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Saint Augustine grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native St. Augustine grass — tropical lawn grass occasionally persisting from old plantings. Dense stoloniferous growth.

grasses.desc: bobtail barley — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native bobtail barley — small native barley of grasslands. Important component of intact native grassland.

grasses.desc: Non-native. water beard grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native water beard grass — moist disturbed areas. Dense green inflorescences. Less invasive than P. monspeliensis.

grasses.desc: nodding needle grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native nodding needlegrass — graceful nodding inflorescence. Native bunchgrass of grasslands and oak woodlands.

grasses.desc: Non-native. swamp pricklegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native swamp dropseed — recently split from Crypsis. Moist alkaline soils. Spreading in wetland margins.

grasses.desc: Toad rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native toad rush — tiny annual rush of seasonal wet areas. Cosmopolitan. Important vernal pool indicator.

grasses.hp: Native shore grass — salt-tolerant grass of upper marsh zones. Important for salt marsh restoration and shore stabilization.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Fern-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native fern-grass — small stiff Mediterranean annual. Spreading on walls and dry rocky areas.

grasses.desc: sprangletop — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native bearded sprangletop — grass of alkaline marshes and saline soils. Salt-tolerant. Marsh habitat indicator.

grasses.desc: Non-native. canary grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native canary grass — birdseed plant occasionally volunteering. Mediterranean origin. Seeds are commercial canary food.

grasses.desc: Foxtail Barley — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native foxtail barley — iridescent awns catch sunlight. Moist alkaline soils. Barbed awns hazardous to livestock and pets.

grasses.desc: california dissanthelium — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native Thomas' bluegrass — mountain bunchgrass. Rocky slopes. Uncommon.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Bulbous Bluegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native bulbous bluegrass — European grass with bulbous stem base. Viviparous — produces bulbils instead of seeds.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Barb Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native coast hardgrass — salt-tolerant coastal grass. Slender cylindrical spikes. Mediterranean.

grasses.desc: Non-native. hooked bristle grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native bristly foxtail — barbed bristles cling to clothing. Tropical. Common urban weed.

grasses.desc: Non-native. tall fescue — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native tall fescue — major turf and pasture grass. Contains endophyte fungi producing alkaloids. Widely planted.

grasses.desc: southwestern bushy bluestem — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native desert purple three-awn — tufted bunchgrass with purple spikelets. Hot desert specialist.

grasses.desc: Non-native. bristly dogtail grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native hedgehog dogtail — distinctive bristly one-sided inflorescence. Mediterranean. Grassland weed.

grasses.desc: Non-native. smooth crabgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

grasses.hp: Non-native smooth crabgrass — common lawn weed. Mat-forming annual. Less hairy than D. sanguinalis.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Jungle Rice — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.desc: sixweeks grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native six-weeks fescue — tiny annual completing life cycle in 6 weeks. Dry open habitats. Important early seed source.

grasses.desc: Shortleaf Spikesedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native shortleaf flatsedge — tiny tropical sedge of moist lawns. Kyllinga-type flat inflorescence.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Brachypodium species — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native false brome — Mediterranean grass increasingly invasive in oak woodlands. Creates continuous fuel. Fire ecology concern.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Maize — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

grasses.hp: Non-native corn/maize — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering. Ancient Mesoamerican domesticate. Minimal ecological impact.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Hairy Crabgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

grasses.hp: Non-native hairy crabgrass — ubiquitous summer lawn weed. One of the most common grasses worldwide. Spreading stems root at nodes.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Yorkshire fog — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native velvet grass — soft fuzzy European grass of moist areas. Moderate ecological impact in grasslands.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Little quaking-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native lesser quaking grass — delicate pendulous heart-shaped spikelets. Mediterranean. Ornamental value.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. Arabian grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Non-native Arabian grass invading desert ecosystems. Fine fuel creator in previously fuel-free habitats. Fire ecology concern.

grasses.desc: Panicled Bulrush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native small-fruited bulrush — tall marsh sedge. Important nesting habitat for marsh birds. Wetland restoration species.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Hare's Tail Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native hare's tail grass — distinctive fluffy oval seed heads. Mediterranean. Common in wildflower mixes and coastal disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: yellow nutsedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native yellow nutsedge — edible tubers (chufa/tiger nut). One of the oldest cultivated crops. Extremely persistent weed.

grasses.desc: california muhly — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native California muhly — clump-forming grass of mountain stream banks. Important for stream bank stabilization.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Mediterranean Hair-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Mediterranean annual grass spreading in open areas. Compact spike-like inflorescence.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Blue Fescue — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native blue fescue — blue-gray ornamental grass. European. Very common in LA landscaping. Minimal naturalization.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Common Oat — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native cultivated oat — crop plant occasionally volunteering from birdseed. Less weedy than wild oats (A. fatua).

grasses.desc: Non-native. Barren Brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native barren brome — European annual with large drooping spikelets. Waste ground colonizer.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Variable Flatsedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native variable flatsedge — rice paddy weed. Moist disturbed areas. Small globose inflorescences.

grasses.desc: knot grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native knotgrass — stoloniferous grass of wet areas. Mat-forming. Important wetland restoration species.

grasses.desc: Lemmon's canarygrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native Lemmon's canary grass — uncommon native of vernal pools and seasonal wet areas. Vernal pool indicator.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Smooth Meadow-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.
