fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Kentucky bluegrass — classic lawn grass. European. Rhizomatous. Major pollen allergen.

grasses.desc: Non-native. narrow-leaved cattail — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native narrow-leaved cattail — slimmer than native T. latifolia. Hybridizes with native cattail. Spreading in freshwater marshes.

grasses.desc: mariposa rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native dubious rush — mountain rush of moist meadows and stream banks. Important mountain wetland species.

grasses.desc: long-leaved rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native long-leaved rush — large-leaved rush of moist mountain meadows. Stream bank stabilizer.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Intermediate Wheatgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native intermediate wheatgrass — Eurasian grass planted for forage and erosion control. Rhizomatous spreader.

grasses.desc: California Cordgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native leafy dropseed — fine-textured grass of open areas. Desert and grassland specialist.

grasses.desc: Non-native. modest prickle grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Nile dropseed — tropical African grass. Spreading in irrigated urban areas.

grasses.desc: sand spikerush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native sand spikerush — slender rush of sandy stream margins. Important wetland indicator.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Greater Quaking Grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native greater quaking grass — large pendulous heart-shaped spikelets. Mediterranean. Ornamental. Naturalizing in grasslands.

grasses.desc: Invasive non-native. purple false-brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native purple false brome — Cal-IPC Moderate. Mediterranean annual creating continuous fire fuel in grasslands.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Awned Canary-grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native hood canary grass — Mediterranean. Spreading in grasslands and disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Asian Rice — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native cultivated rice — rarely persisting from agricultural production or birdseed. Minimal ecological impact.

grasses.desc: rice cutgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native rice cutgrass — rough-edged grass of freshwater marshes. Leaves can cut skin. Important waterfowl habitat.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Chilean chess — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Chilean brome — South American annual. Spreading in disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: tall brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native tall brome — large native brome of mountain slopes and shaded canyons. Less common than introduced bromes.

grasses.desc: columbia brome — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native Columbia brome — shade-tolerant native grass of mountain forests. Important forest understory species.

grasses.desc: western rough sedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native western rough sedge — large sedge of stream banks and marshes. Important riparian habitat structure.

grasses.desc: trigonous sedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native triangular-fruit sedge — distinctive three-angled seed pods. Dry chaparral slopes.

grasses.desc: common spikerush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native creeping spikerush — rhizomatous marsh species. Important wetland restoration plant. Waterfowl habitat.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Mediterranean lovegrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native Mediterranean lovegrass spreading on sandy disturbed ground. Delicate inflorescence.

grasses.desc: Non-native. australian rush — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native common rush — Australian rush naturalizing in moist disturbed areas.

grasses.desc: California orcutt grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native California Orcutt grass — FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. Vernal pool endemic. One of California's rarest plants. Critical conservation priority.

grasses.desc: witch grass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native witchgrass — annual with delicate open panicle. Dry disturbed areas. Tumbles when mature.

grasses.desc: Non-native. river oats — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native inland sea oats — ornamental grass with flat drooping spikelets. Eastern US native. Garden escape.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Papyrus sedge — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native papyrus — dramatic tall sedge of Egyptian antiquity. Planted in water gardens. Frost-sensitive in LA.

grasses.desc: Great Basin Wildrye — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Native basin wild rye — massive native bunchgrass to 6 ft. Important for alkaline site restoration. Seeds eaten by birds.

grasses.desc: Non-native. Palmgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Non-native palm grass — broad pleated leaves resembling palm fronds. Tropical. Garden escape in shaded canyons.

grasses.desc: Non-native. buffelgrass — a grass, sedge, or rush found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and open areas in LA County

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Non-native buffel grass — extremely invasive in desert habitats. Transforms desert fire regime. Active eradication in Sonoran Desert.

ferns.desc: Largest fern in western North America — fronds to 9 ft. Shady canyon stream banks. Dramatic accent in moist forests.

fm.Size: Fronds 3–9 ft — largest western NA fern

ferns.hp: Largest fern in western North America — fronds to 9 ft. Provides microhabitat structure and moisture retention in canyon understory. Important for salamander and invertebrate habitat.

ferns.desc: Small fern with triangular fronds — bright yellow waxy powder on underside. Rocky slopes, including very dry exposed sites. Drought-adapted.

fm.Season: Green in wet season, curls dry in summer

ferns.hp: Bright yellow waxy powder on underside protects from desiccation. Among the most drought-adapted ferns. Curls dry in summer, revives with rain.

ferns.desc: Bluish-green fern of dry rocky slopes. Pinnae on wiry dark stems. Much more drought-tolerant than most ferns.
ferns.hp: Coffee fern — wiry stems with round blue-green pinnae resembling coffee beans. Extremely drought-tolerant for a fern. Rocky slopes.

ferns.desc: Delicate fan-shaped pinnae on black wiry stems. Water beads and rolls off fronds ("maidenhair"). Shady moist grottos and seeps.

fm.Habitat: Moist grottos, shady seeps, waterfall spray zones

ferns.hp: California maidenhair — delicate fan-shaped pinnae on wiry black stems. Canyon seeps and waterfall spray zones. Exquisitely beautiful.

ferns.desc: Large sword-like fronds with eared pinnae. More common in NorCal — at southern range edge in San Gabriel canyons.

fm.Habitat: Mountain canyons — at southern range limit

ferns.hp: Western sword fern — the dominant fern of Pacific Coast forests. Large arching fronds. Important for erosion control on steep slopes.

ferns.desc: Cosmopolitan fern of open woodlands and burns. Triangular fronds from underground rhizomes. Fire-following — dominates after burns.

fm.Ecology: Fire-follower — spreads by deep rhizomes
fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep rhizomes survive; dominates post-fire

ferns.hp: Cosmopolitan fern — found on every continent except Antarctica. Fire follower — deep rhizomes survive fire and dominate post-fire understory. Contains carcinogenic compounds (ptaquiloside).

ferns.desc: Living fossil — unchanged for 300 million years. Jointed hollow stems with silica. Used as sandpaper. Riparian and seep areas.
ferns.hp: Living fossil — horsetails predate flowering plants by 300 million years. Silica-impregnated stems used historically as scouring brushes. Important invertebrate habitat in riparian zones.

ferns.desc: Small fern of dry rock crevices. Woolly or scaly underside. Extreme drought tolerance — curls up completely when dry, revives with rain.

fm.Habitat: Dry rock crevices — extreme drought tolerance

ferns.hp: Coville's lip fern — woolly-brown scales. Dry rock crevice specialist. Curls up when dry, unfurls rapidly after rain.

ferns.desc: Small fern growing on mossy rocks and tree bases in shady canyons. Winter-green — appears with first rains, dies back completely in summer drought. Rhizome tastes and smells like licorice. One of the most seasonal ferns.
ferns.hp: Winter-green — appears with first rains, dies back in summer. Rhizome smells and tastes like licorice. Grows on mossy rocks and tree bases.

ferns.desc: Blue-green pinnae with trident-shaped tips. Very drought-tolerant. Rocky chaparral.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — rhizome survives fire; one of first ferns to reappear post-fire

ferns.hp: Ferns provide habitat structure in moist microenvironments. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

ferns.desc: Small desert fern with star-shaped scales on underside. Extremely drought-tolerant. Desert rock crevices.
ferns.hp: Cochise cloak fern — star-shaped scales on undersurface. Desert rock crevice specialist. Among the most drought-tolerant ferns.

ferns.desc: Tiny triangular fern of dry rocky slopes. Among the most drought-adapted ferns. Curls completely when dry.

fm.Behavior: Curls when dry, revives with rain

ferns.hp: Indian's dream — waxy-coated pinnae resist desiccation. Named for traditional use by indigenous peoples. Dry rocky slopes.

ferns.desc: Delicate maidenhair fern of seeps and waterfalls. Fan-shaped pinnae on black stems. More common in SoCal than A. jordanii.
ferns.hp: Southern maidenhair — delicate fan-shaped pinnae. Named 'Venus hair.' Alkaline seeps and travertine deposits. Cosmopolitan species.

ferns.desc: Tiny triangular fern with lacy dissected fronds. Rock crevices at mid-elevations. Very drought-tolerant.
ferns.hp: Lace fern — extremely dense pinnae creating lace-like appearance. Rock crevice specialist. One of the most drought-tolerant native ferns.

ferns.desc: Tiny spikemoss forming dark mats on rock faces and seeps. Not a true fern or moss — an ancient lineage predating both. Resurrects from dried state.
ferns.hp: Not a true fern or moss — an ancient lineage predating both. Poikilohydric — revives from complete desiccation when wetted. Rock face specialist.

ferns.desc: Large woodland fern with triangular fronds and distinctive "wood fern" growth pattern. The most common large fern in LA mountain canyons. Evergreen in moist shade.
ferns.hp: Most common large fern in LA mountain canyons. Provides important microhabitat structure for invertebrates and amphibians in moist canyon understory.

ferns.desc: Small fern on shady rocky slopes in oak woodland. Narrow fronds from creeping rhizome. Uncommon but locally abundant in Santa Monica Mountains canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady rocky slopes, oak woodland canyons

ferns.hp: Spleenwort — named for traditional use treating spleen ailments. Shaded rock crevices. Dark wiry stipes.

ferns.desc: Smooth green jointed stems without whorled branches — looks like a thin bamboo rod. Ancient lineage — horsetails predate flowering plants by 300 million years. Stream banks.
ferns.hp: Smooth horsetail — unbranched green stems with silica-roughened surface. Ancient lineage predating flowering plants by 300 million years.

ferns.desc: Largest horsetail — thick ivory stems with whorls of green branches like a tiny pine tree. Stream banks in Santa Monica Mountains canyons. Prehistoric appearance.

fm.Form: Thick ivory stems with whorled green branches
fm.Habitat: Stream banks, canyon bottoms — SMM canyons
fm.Season: Deciduous — dies back in dry season

ferns.hp: Giant horsetail — largest horsetail species, with whorled branches creating bottle-brush appearance. Riparian specialist.

ferns.desc: Soft-textured fern of canyon streams. Larger and more lush than most SMM ferns. Fronds to 3 ft. At northern range limit in the Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Habitat: Canyon streams — at northern range limit

ferns.hp: Sonoran maiden fern — large triangular fronds in shaded canyon bottoms. At northern range edge in LA County. Subtropical origin.

ferns.desc: Stiff upright fronds with sword-shaped pinnae. Shady canyon slopes. More drought-tolerant than most ferns. Rocky areas in SGM canyons.
ferns.hp: Imbricate sword fern — overlapping pinnae with spiny tips. Rock ledges and talus. Smaller than P. munitum.

ferns.desc: Delicate lacy fern of moist mountain stream banks. At extreme southern range limit in San Gabriel Mountain canyons. Twice-pinnate fronds with crescent-shaped sori.

fm.Habitat: Moist mountain stream banks — at southern range limit

ferns.hp: Moist canyon specialist providing microhabitat for salamanders and invertebrates. At extreme southern range limit in SGM.

ferns.desc: Small rock fern with blue-green pinnae on dark wiry stipes. Rocky crevices in mountain chaparral. More compact than Coffee Fern.
ferns.hp: Rock crevice specialist stabilizing substrates and retaining moisture in montane habitats.

ferns.desc: Small rock fern with beaded fronds — similar to Newberry's Lip Fern but with narrower, less woolly pinnae. Rocky outcrops in coastal and foothill chaparral.
ferns.hp: Rock crevice specialist. Drought-adapted — curls up when dry, revives with rain. Coastal chaparral.

ferns.desc: Small desert fern with wavy-margined pinnae covered in star-shaped scales. Desert rock crevices at eastern LA County edge. More heat-tolerant than A. cochisensis.

fm.Habitat: Desert rock crevices — eastern LA County edge

ferns.hp: Desert rock fern colonizing extreme arid habitats. Star-shaped scales reduce water loss.

ferns.desc: Grass-like aquatic fern relative growing in seasonal pools. Ancient lineage — resembles early land plants. Rare in LA County vernal pool habitats.
ferns.hp: Ancient lineage related to earliest land plants. Vernal pool specialist — indicator of high-quality seasonal wetland habitat.

ferns.desc: Larger spikemoss than S. bigelovii, forming mats on shady rock faces. Blue-green iridescent sheen in some light. Mountain canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady rock faces in mountain canyons

ferns.hp: Ancient lineage predating true ferns. Retains moisture on rock faces enabling later plant colonization.

ferns.desc: Introduced fern naturalizing on walls, mortar, and limestone. Remarkable ability to hyperaccumulate arsenic from soil — used in phytoremediation research.

fm.Ecology: Hyperaccumulates arsenic — phytoremediation research species

ferns.hp: Arsenic hyperaccumulator — absorbs toxic arsenic from contaminated soil. Used in phytoremediation research. Naturalizing on walls and mortar in LA.

ferns.desc: Large triangular fern of deep mountain shade. At southern range limit in wet SGM canyons above 5,000 ft. Three-times-pinnate fronds.

fm.Habitat: Deep shade in wet SGM canyons above 5,000 ft

ferns.hp: Deep shade specialist at extreme southern range limit. Important microhabitat for mountain invertebrates and salamanders.

ferns.desc: Five-finger maidenhair with fan-shaped pinnae on dark stems. Distinguished from A. jordanii by palmate (hand-shaped) frond arrangement. Rare in LA — wet mountain canyons.

fm.Shape: Palmate — fan of 5 pinnae (vs pinnate in A. jordanii)
fm.Habitat: Wet mountain canyons — rare in LA County

ferns.hp: Distinguished from A. jordanii by palmate (hand-shaped) frond arrangement. Moist mountain habitat specialist.

ferns.desc: Delicate small fern of moist rock crevices in mountain forests. Fronds easily broken — hence 'fragile.' Among the most widespread ferns worldwide.

fm.Habitat: Moist rock crevices in mountain forest

ferns.hp: Among the most widespread ferns worldwide — found on every continent. Moist rock crevice specialist in mountain forests.

ferns.desc: Tiny rock fern that curls into a tight brown ball when dry and unfurls green within hours of rain. Extreme drought tolerance. Rock crevices in chaparral.

fm.Behavior: Curls into tight ball when dry, revives with rain

ferns.hp: Poikilohydric — tolerates complete desiccation and revives within hours of wetting. Rock crevice specialist.

ferns.desc: Non-native. skeleton fork fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Non-native whisk fern — among the most primitive living vascular plants. No true roots or leaves. Living fossil resembling 400-million-year-old ancestors. Escaped from cultivation.

ferns.desc: Newberry's lip fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native Newberry's lip fern of dry rocky crevices. Woolly brown scales protect fronds from desiccation. Curls up when dry, revives rapidly with rain.

ferns.desc: Sticky goldback fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native sticky goldback fern with glandular-viscid undersurface. Distinguished from P. triangularis by sticky (not waxy) coating. Coastal and foothill rock crevices.

ferns.desc: water clover — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native water clover — aquatic fern with clover-shaped fronds. Seasonal pools and pond margins. Sporocarps persist in dried mud for years, germinating when rewetted.

ferns.desc: Non-native. house holly-fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Non-native house holly-fern from East Asia — glossy dark green pinnae with holly-like shape. Escaping from cultivation to walls and shaded crevices.

ferns.desc: California Cloak-fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native California cloak fern of desert rock crevices. Dense waxy coating on underside protects from extreme desiccation. Poikilohydric — revives after complete drying.

ferns.desc: American pillwort — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native American pillwort — tiny grass-like aquatic fern ally of vernal pools. Spherical sporocarps at base. Extremely inconspicuous. Vernal pool indicator.

ferns.desc: field horsetail — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native field horsetail — ancient lineage predating flowering plants. Silica-rich stems used as scouring material. Spreads aggressively via deep rhizomes.

ferns.desc: California adder's-tongue — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native California adder's-tongue — single fleshy leaf with spore-bearing spike. Among the most overlooked native ferns. Mycorrhizal dependent.

ferns.desc: Showy Maiden Fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native showy maiden fern of shaded mountain canyons. Large triangular fronds. Moist habitat specialist at southern range limit.

ferns.desc: Non-native. Fishbone Fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Non-native fishbone fern — tuberous roots store water. Escaping from cultivation via tuber dispersal. Displaces native understory ferns.

ferns.desc: Non-native. water spangles — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Non-native water spangles — tiny floating fern forming dense mats on still water. Can double biomass in days. Aquatic invasive.

ferns.desc: Non-native. spider brake — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Non-native spider brake — multi-fingered fronds. East Asian. Escaping from cultivation to walls and moist shaded areas.

ferns.desc: San Diego Silverback Fern — a fern or fern ally found in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Canyons and shaded slopes in LA County

ferns.hp: Native San Diego silverback fern — sticky glandular underside. Distinguished from P. triangularis by viscid (not waxy) coating. At range edge.

cacti.desc: Flat-padded cactus of coastal scrub. Yellow flowers. Purple-red fruit (tunas) eaten by wildlife and humans. Glochids are irritating.

fm.Pads: Flat, oval, with glochids and spines
fm.Fire: Resprouter — pads reroot after fire dislodges them; vegetative recovery

cacti.hp: Fruit eaten by coyotes, birds, and desert tortoise. Host for Cactus Moth. Pads provide nest sites — Cactus Wren builds nests among spines for protection.

cacti.desc: Branching cylindrical cactus of coastal sage scrub. Pink-magenta flowers. Segments detach and root easily — vegetative spread.

fm.Spread: Segments detach and root on contact
fm.Fire: Resprouter — segments reroot; fire-resilient via vegetative spread

cacti.hp: Segments detach and root on contact — vegetative spread strategy. Pink-magenta flowers visited by native bees and beetles.

cacti.desc: Dense silver-spined cholla of desert margins. Greenish-yellow flowers. More compact and silver than Coastal Cholla.
cacti.hp: Silver cholla — silvery-spined joints. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat. Fruit spiny but edible after careful spine removal.

cacti.desc: Spineless flat pads with hot pink flowers. Pads have irritating glochids but no visible spines. Desert species reaching LA County.
cacti.hp: Beavertail cactus — pad-shaped joints without visible spines (has glochids). Brilliant magenta flowers. Important desert tortoise food.

cacti.desc: Dramatic rosette sending up 6–15 ft flower stalk once in its life, then dying. Chaparral icon. Pollinated by Yucca Moth.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — dramatically increased flowering 1–2 years post-fire; rosettes killed but seed germination enhanced

cacti.hp: Obligate mutualism with Yucca Moth (Tegeticula maculata). Neither can reproduce without the other.

cacti.desc: Rosette succulent at extreme northern range limit. Massive flower stalk. Very rare in LA County — a few natural populations remain.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — extreme northern range limit

cacti.hp: Flowers visited by native bees and hummingbirds. Historically important indigenous food plant. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

cacti.desc: Succulent rosettes on rocky outcrops. Yellow-red flowers on arching stalks. Extremely drought-tolerant. Many species poaching-threatened.

fm.Flowers: Yellow to red on arching stalk
fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, banks, rock crevices — the most common Dudleya
fm.Conservation: Poaching is a major threat to Dudleya species
fm.vs: Canyon Dudleya (D. cymosa) has BROADER rounder leaves and grows specifically from rock crevices on canyon walls

cacti.hp: Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds. Host for rare Dudleya moth. Lance-shaped leaves diagnostic. CA endemic. Poaching is a major conservation threat to Dudleya species.

cacti.desc: Dramatic chalk-white rosette succulent on cliff faces. Tall red flower stalk. Southern California specialty. Poaching-threatened.

fm.Height: Rosette to 1 ft; stalk to 3 ft

cacti.hp: Chalk dudleya — the largest and most dramatically chalky-white dudleya. Impressive rosettes on cliff faces. Hummingbird-pollinated.

cacti.desc: Cylindrical with fierce hooked spines. Leans south ("compass cactus").
cacti.hp: Leans south ('compass cactus'). Flowers visited by native bees and cactus beetles. Fruit eaten by desert bighorn sheep and ground squirrels.

cacti.desc: Densely spined, golden in backlight. "Jumping cholla" — segments detach on contact.

fm.Cultural: Jumping cholla

cacti.hp: Densely spined — segments detach on lightest touch ('jumping cholla'). Cactus Wren builds nests in cholla for spine protection from predators. Golden in backlight.

cacti.desc: Thin-stemmed cholla with pencil-like branches. Pink-brown flowers. Desert species at western range edge.
cacti.hp: Pencil cholla — extremely thin pencil-like joints. Diamond-patterned tubercles. Desert wash specialist.

cacti.desc: Clustering barrel cactus with magenta flowers. Spiny clumps on desert rocky slopes. Fruit edible.
cacti.hp: Hedgehog cactus — cylindrical stems with large magenta-pink flowers. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat. Fruit edible.

cacti.desc: Rosette succulent with pale green leaves (not chalky). White-green flowers. Coastal bluffs and Channel Islands.
cacti.hp: Candleholder dudleya — tall flowering stems resembling candelabra. Channel Islands populations distinct. Conservation interest.

cacti.desc: Low clustering cactus with red-orange flowers. Mojave Desert edge of LA County. Spectacular spring display in good rain years.
cacti.hp: Mojave mound cactus — clustering stems forming mounds. Red-orange flowers. Mojave Desert specialist.

cacti.desc: Small globular cactus with hooked central spines. White-pink flowers. Coastal sage scrub and desert edges. Often hidden under shrubs.

fm.Habitat: Under shrubs — coastal scrub, desert edges
fm.Fire: Fire-sensitive — killed by fire; slow recovery

cacti.hp: Native fish-hook cactus with hooked central spines. Small red fruits eaten by birds. Slow-growing. At northern range limit in LA County.

cacti.desc: Spiky yucca with shreddy fibrous trunk. Mojave Desert and Joshua tree woodland. Tongva used root for soap. Cream flower clusters. Yucca moth pollinated.

fm.Cultural: Tongva root soap plant
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Joshua tree woodland — Antelope Valley

cacti.hp: Mojave yucca — obligate mutualism with Yucca Moth (Tegeticula). The moth is the sole pollinator; larvae feed inside developing fruit. Roots yield saponins used commercially in beverages and animal feed.

cacti.desc: Massive chalk-white rosette — the whitest of all Dudleyas. Rocky bluffs. Severely threatened by poaching for international succulent trade.
cacti.hp: Chalk live-forever — intensely chalky-white rosettes. Baja California origin, planted and naturalizing on cliffs.

cacti.desc: Bright yellow-orange flowers on succulent rosettes growing from rock crevices. Multiple subspecies, several rare. Canyon walls in chaparral and oak woodland. Fire-adapted — survives on cliffs.

fm.Habitat: Rock crevices, canyon walls — specifically cliff faces
fm.Leaves: BROAD, rounded (vs narrow lance-shaped in D. lanceolata)
fm.vs: Lance-leaved Dudleya (D. lanceolata) has narrow lance-shaped leaves and grows on open slopes, not just cliffs

cacti.hp: Canyon dudleya — yellow-orange flower clusters on cliff faces. Important hummingbird nectar source on vertical rock.

cacti.desc: Tiny dudleya with white-pink flowers on clay soils. CNPS 1B.1. Restricted to clay outcrops and openings. One of the most threatened dudleyas — habitat rapidly lost to development.
cacti.hp: Blochman's dudleya — CNPS 1B.1. Tiny rosettes on clay soils. Among the smallest dudleyas. Conservation concern.

cacti.desc: Pale yellow flowers from multiple rosettes on clay soil. CNPS 1B.2. Often grows with Dudleya blochmaniae. Threatened by grassland conversion.
cacti.hp: Many-stemmed dudleya — CNPS 1B.2. Multiple rosettes from single root. Clay soil specialist. Declining due to development.

cacti.desc: Small green rosettes with yellow flowers on rocky granitic slopes. Named for early CA botanist LeRoy Abrams. Interior chaparral. Several subspecies.
cacti.hp: Abrams' dudleya — mountain rock specialist above 4,000 ft. Yellow flowers. Several varieties with different ranges.

cacti.desc: Yellow flowers on fleshy rosettes of coastal bluffs and sea cliffs. Salt-spray tolerant. Forms dense clumps. Variable — integrades with D. lanceolata inland.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs, sea cliffs — salt-spray tolerant

cacti.hp: Coast dudleya — rosettes on coastal bluffs. Yellow flowers. Salt-spray tolerant. Important coastal cliff community member.

cacti.desc: Federally threatened dudleya endemic to Conejo Valley/western LA County edge. Pink-red rosettes with yellow flowers. Known from ~14 populations.

fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. CNPS 1B.1. Endemic to ~14 populations.

cacti.hp: Verity's dudleya — FEDERALLY THREATENED. Known from very few populations. Conejo Volcanics specialist. Critical conservation priority.

cacti.desc: Chalky rosette succulent of coastal bluffs. Yellow flowers. Wind-and salt-tolerant. Poaching threat.
cacti.hp: Bluff lettuce — powdery-white rosettes on coastal bluffs. Yellow flowers. Northern coastal range.

cacti.desc: Branching cholla with prominent spines and yellow-green to red flowers. Desert washes at eastern LA County edge. More open-branching than Teddy Bear Cholla.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes — eastern LA County edge

cacti.hp: Cactus Wren and Curve-billed Thrasher nest in cholla for spine protection from predators. Flowers visited by native bees.

cacti.desc: Dense woolly-topped barrel cactus forming clumps. Mojave Desert specialist. Very slow-growing — some specimens centuries old. Yellow flowers emerge from woolly crown.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert rocky slopes — very slow-growing

cacti.hp: Extremely slow-growing — some specimens centuries old. Flowers visited by native bees and cactus beetles.

cacti.desc: Tree-like prickly pear with woody trunk. Coastal sage scrub. Larger and more tree-like than O. littoralis. Important nesting habitat for Cactus Wren.
cacti.hp: Tree-like form provides important nesting structure for Cactus Wren and Loggerhead Shrike. Fruit eaten by coyotes and birds.

cacti.desc: Low-growing densely spined prickly pear. Mountain and desert-edge populations in LA County. More cold-tolerant than coastal species.
cacti.hp: Cold-tolerant cactus — survives freezing temperatures. Flowers visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by wildlife.

cacti.desc: Succulent shrub of coastal bluffs — the only native succulent Euphorbia in LA County. Small and easily overlooked. At northern range limit.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — at northern range limit

cacti.hp: At northern range limit in LA County. White milky sap is a skin irritant. Coastal bluff specialist.

cacti.desc: Finger-shaped cylindrical leaves on rosette succulent. White flowers. Coastal bluffs and rocky outcrops. Edible — hence the species name. Southern LA County edge.
cacti.hp: Edible succulent — 'edulis' means edible. Pollinated by native bees. Poaching threat.

cacti.desc: Channel Islands endemic dudleya with chalky rosettes and yellow flowers. Known only from Santa Catalina and a few mainland cliff sites. Severely threatened by poaching.

fm.Habitat: Catalina Island and a few mainland cliffs
fm.Conservation: Severely threatened by poaching — island endemic

cacti.hp: Channel Islands endemic threatened by international succulent trade poaching. Pollinated by native bees.

cacti.desc: Narrow-leaved dudleya with pale yellow flowers on coastal and inland rocky slopes. Southern LA County. Distinguished from D. lanceolata by narrower leaves.

fm.Habitat: Coastal and inland rocky slopes — southern LA County

cacti.hp: Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds. Rock outcrop specialist.

cacti.desc: Brilliant red-orange flowers on clustering cactus. High desert and mountain-desert transition. Cold-tolerant — survives SGM winter freezes.
cacti.hp: Hummingbird-pollinated — brilliant red flowers attract Costa's and Black-chinned Hummingbirds. Cold-tolerant cactus.

cacti.desc: Thin-stemmed cholla with bright red fruits persisting into winter — hence 'Christmas cactus.' Desert washes at far eastern LA County edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes — far eastern LA County

cacti.hp: Red winter fruits eaten by Cactus Wren, thrashers, and other desert birds. Provides winter food when little else is available.

cacti.desc: Low mat-forming succulent with spoon-shaped fleshy leaves and yellow star flowers. Rocky outcrops and cliff faces. At southern range limit.

fm.Leaves: Spoon-shaped, fleshy, often with waxy bloom
fm.Habitat: Rocky outcrops, cliff faces — at southern range limit

cacti.hp: Mat-forming succulent stabilizing rock surfaces. Pollinated by small native bees. At southern range limit.

vines.desc: Native grape of riparian corridors. Small tart fruit. Important wildlife food. Can completely cover large trees.

fm.Fruit: Small, dark purple — tart, wildlife food
fm.Color: Green, tiny — fruit is dark purple

vines.hp: Fruit eaten by many bird species including Northern Mockingbird and Cedar Waxwing. Host for Virginia Creeper Sphinx Moth and Grape Leaffolder. Can completely cover large trees.

vines.desc: Fast-growing vine with spiny fruit the size of a fist. Emerges from enormous underground tuber after winter rains. Toxic fruit.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — massive underground tuber survives fire; rapid post-fire regrowth
fm.vs: Man-root (M. fabacea) has SMOOTH round fruits; Wild Cucumber (M. macrocarpa) has SPINY prickly fruits. M. macrocarpa is the common LA County species.

vines.hp: Visited by native bees in winter/early spring when few other flowers available. Massive underground tuber survives fire — rapid post-fire regrowth. Spiny fruit diagnostic (vs smooth in M. fabacea).

vines.desc: White flower clusters followed by silky seed plumes. Vigorous native climber in riparian areas. Can form dense curtains.
vines.hp: Visited by many native bee species. Host for Clematis Clearwing Moth.

vines.desc: Twining native honeysuckle with creamy yellow tubular flowers. Chaparral and woodland edges. Less showy than Japanese Honeysuckle but native.
vines.hp: Nectar source for hummingbirds and native bees. Berries eaten by birds. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

vines.desc: Large white to pink morning glory flowers on vigorous native vine. Coastal and island slopes. Much larger flowers than bindweed.
vines.hp: Island morning glory — large white-pink funnel flowers. Channel Islands endemic expanded to mainland. Vigorous twining vine.

vines.desc: Cream-white flowers and feathery seed heads. Vigorous chaparral climber.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; vigorous post-fire growth

vines.hp: Important early-season nectar for native bees. Seeds with silky plumes dispersed by wind.

vines.desc: Tiny purple snapdragon flowers on twining stems. Delicate desert-edge vine. Climbing by twining leaf stalks.
vines.hp: Snapdragon vine — delicate twining vine with purple snapdragon-like flowers. Rock face specialist. Leaf petioles coil around supports for climbing. Important desert hummingbird nectar.

vines.desc: Orange parasitic vine forming tangled masses over host plants. No leaves, no chlorophyll — entirely parasitic. Native but often mistaken for invasive.

fm.Ecology: Native parasite — not invasive

vines.hp: Native parasite — entirely parasitic with no chlorophyll. Orange threads. Often mistaken for invasive but is a natural part of the ecosystem. Can reduce vigor of invasive hosts.

vines.desc: Native blackberry with delicious small sweet berries — far better flavor than Himalayan blackberry. Thorny arching canes in moist canyons. White flowers. Important wildlife food.

fm.Fruit: Small sweet black berries — superior flavor to non-native species

vines.hp: Berries eaten by birds, bears, raccoons, foxes, and coyotes. Superior flavor to non-native Himalayan blackberry. Flowers visited by native bees. Important riparian wildlife corridor cover.

vines.desc: Fast-growing South African vine smothering riparian trees. Cal-IPC High. Can grow 5+ ft per month in wet conditions. Toxic to livestock. One of LA County's most destructive riparian invasives.

fm.Ecology: Grows 5+ ft/month in wet conditions. Smothers riparian trees.
fm.Habitat: Riparian corridors — one of LA's worst riparian invasives

vines.hp: Cal-IPC High. One of California's most destructive invasive vines — smothers native riparian and coastal vegetation. Bright yellow daisy flowers. Toxic to livestock. Active removal programs statewide.

vines.desc: Aggressive climbing vine that smothers trees and ground. Cal-IPC Moderate. Aerial rootlets damage bark and mortar. Weight of ivy can topple trees in storms. Berry spread by birds.

fm.Ecology: Weight topples trees. Aerial rootlets damage structures.

vines.hp: Cal-IPC High. English ivy — one of California's worst invasive vines. Smothers trees, ground covers, and structures. Berries bird-dispersed.

vines.desc: Blue-violet pinwheel flowers on trailing groundcover. Cal-IPC Moderate. Escapes gardens into riparian areas where it forms dense mats excluding all native plants. Toxic if ingested.

fm.Ecology: Forms impenetrable mats excluding all natives

vines.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Periwinkle — aggressive vine smothering native riparian understory. Purple flowers. Spreads via rooting stems.

vines.desc: Pink tubular flowers on twining vine in mountain woodland. Less common than L. subspicata. Hairy leaves. Important hummingbird nectar plant in SGM canyons.
vines.hp: Pink honeysuckle — native vine with paired pink tubular flowers. Important hummingbird nectar. Berries eaten by thrushes.

vines.desc: Yellow tubular flowers with distinctive fused leaf pairs below flower clusters — the upper leaves form a disc through which the stem passes. Dry chaparral. Less common than other honeysuckles.

fm.Leaves: Upper pairs fused into disc around stem (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Dry chaparral — less common than other Lonicera

vines.hp: Chaparral honeysuckle — native vine climbing through shrubs. Yellow-orange flowers attract hummingbirds. Less common than L. hispidula.

vines.desc: Fragrant white-yellow flowers on aggressive twining vine. Cal-IPC Moderate. Widely planted but escaping into riparian areas. Smothers native shrubs. Extremely sweet fragrance.

fm.Ecology: Smothers native shrubs in riparian areas

vines.hp: Nectar source for hummingbirds and native bees. Berries eaten by birds. Invasive — displaces native vegetation in riparian and woodland habitats.

vines.desc: Massive tuberous vine with white-green flowers and SMOOTH round fruits — the key difference from M. macrocarpa which has SPINY fruits. At the edge of its range in LA County (more common in NorCal/Central Coast). Enormous underground tuber can weigh over 100 lbs.

fm.Fruit: SMOOTH round — no spines (diagnostic vs M. macrocarpa)
fm.Habitat: Canyon bottoms, riparian — edge of range in LA County
fm.vs: Wild Cucumber (M. macrocarpa) has SPINY prickly fruits and is common in LA; Man-root (M. fabacea) has SMOOTH fruits and is rare in LA County (more common NorCal)

vines.hp: Visited by native bees in winter/early spring when few other flowers available.

aquatic.desc: Broad-leaved cattail with fat continuous brown seed heads — no gap between male and female sections. Leaves to 1 in wide (wider than T. domingensis). More common in freshwater; less salt-tolerant than Southern Cattail.

fm.Seedhead: Fat brown cylinder — NO GAP between male (upper) and female (lower) sections (diagnostic vs T. domingensis)
fm.Habitat: Freshwater marshes, pond edges — less salt-tolerant
fm.Cultural: All parts used traditionally — root flour, leaf weaving, seedhead insulation
fm.Leaves: To 1 in wide — wider than Southern Cattail
fm.vs: Southern Cattail (T. domingensis) has a visible GAP between male and female spike sections, and narrower leaves

aquatic.hp: Nesting habitat for Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, Least Bittern, and Virginia Rail. All parts used traditionally — rhizome flour, leaf weaving, seedhead insulation, pollen food.

aquatic.desc: Tiny floating plants forming green mats on still water. Each plant 1–5mm. Doubles in population every 2 days. Important waterfowl food.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Duckweed — world's smallest flowering plant. Floating green discs doubling population in days. Important waterfowl food.

aquatic.desc: Tiny floating fern turning red in sun. Hosts nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria — used as living fertilizer in rice paddies worldwide.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria symbiont

aquatic.hp: Water fern — tiny floating fern with nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium (Anabaena). Turns red in fall. Can cover pond surfaces rapidly.

aquatic.desc: White flower cone with petal-like bracts. Alkaline wetlands. Indigenous medicinal plant.

fm.Cultural: Indigenous remedy

aquatic.hp: Wetland habitat species supporting marsh bird nesting and invertebrate communities. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples.

aquatic.desc: Arrow-shaped leaves. Starchy tubers (wapato) — important indigenous food.

fm.Cultural: Tubers = wapato food

aquatic.hp: Arrowhead — arrow-shaped leaves diagnostic. Edible tubers ('wapato') — important indigenous food. Freshwater marsh specialist.

aquatic.desc: Yellow flowers on floating stems. Can choke waterways when nutrients are high.
aquatic.hp: Water primrose — floating mats with yellow flowers. Can become aggressive in slow-moving water. Native but sometimes problematic.

aquatic.desc: Peppery edible leaves in running streams. European introduction now widespread. Small white flowers.
aquatic.hp: Aquatic habitat supporting fish, amphibian, and invertebrate communities. Edible — peppery leaves — widely foraged.

aquatic.desc: Simple needle-like stems with single terminal spikelet. Freshwater margins. Important component of vernal pool and wetland habitats.

fm.Form: Single needle-like stem with terminal spikelet

aquatic.hp: Native creeping spikerush of freshwater marshes. Rhizomatous — forms dense stands. Important waterfowl food and nesting habitat.

aquatic.desc: Submerged aquatic with fine thread-like leaves. Important waterfowl food. Found in ponds, slow streams, and estuaries throughout LA County.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Sago pondweed — submerged aquatic with narrow leaves. Tubers are critical food for diving ducks and coots.

aquatic.desc: Small white three-petaled flowers in airy branching clusters above broad basal leaves. Shallow marsh and pond edges. Important waterfowl habitat plant.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl habitat plant

aquatic.hp: Wetland habitat species supporting marsh bird nesting and invertebrate communities. Important waterfowl food source.

aquatic.desc: White umbel flowers on weak stems trailing in and along water. Shady stream margins. Caution: resembles toxic water hemlock. Verify ID carefully.

fm.Toxicity: Caution — resembles toxic Cicuta (water hemlock). ID carefully.

aquatic.hp: Water parsley — sprawling aquatic herb. Pacific coast native. Provides shade and cover for fish fry in streams.

aquatic.desc: Round umbrella-shaped leaves on slender stems in shallow water. Tiny white-green flower clusters. Wet margins of ponds and slow streams.
aquatic.hp: Marsh pennywort — round umbrella-like leaves on slender stems. Freshwater marsh edges. Provides cover for aquatic invertebrates.

aquatic.desc: Succulent jointed green stems turning red in fall. Dominant salt marsh plant. Edible — salty crunch. Essential habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow. The iconic LA salt marsh plant.

fm.Form: Succulent jointed stems — turns red in fall
fm.Ecology: Essential nesting habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow

aquatic.hp: Essential habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow (state endangered). Dominant salt marsh plant. Edible — salty crunch. The iconic LA salt marsh species.

aquatic.desc: Narrow-leaved cattail with visible gap between male and female seed head sections — the key field mark vs T. latifolia. Leaves narrower (under 0.5 in). More salt-tolerant. The most common cattail in LA coastal marshes.

fm.Cultural: Tongva food plant — rhizomes, pollen, shoots all edible
fm.Habitat: Marshes, ponds, slow streams — more salt-tolerant than T. latifolia
fm.Seedhead: Brown cylinder with VISIBLE GAP between male (upper) and female (lower) sections (diagnostic vs T. latifolia)
fm.Leaves: Narrower than T. latifolia — under 0.5 in
fm.vs: Broadleaf Cattail (T. latifolia) has NO GAP between spike sections, and wider leaves to 1 in

aquatic.hp: More salt-tolerant than T. latifolia — the dominant cattail in LA coastal marshes. Tongva food plant — rhizomes, pollen, and shoots all edible.

aquatic.desc: Tall marsh sedge with round stems (vs triangular in S. californicus). Dense stands in marshes. Foundation wetland species.
aquatic.hp: Common tule — the 'tule' of 'tule fog.' Dense marsh stands. Important marsh bird nesting habitat. Indigenous boat-building material.

aquatic.desc: Tall triangular-stemmed sedge of marshes and lake margins. Important nesting habitat for marsh birds. Cultural use for tule boats.

fm.Cultural: Tule — used for boats and shelters

aquatic.hp: Cultural significance — tule used by indigenous peoples for boats (balsas) and shelters. Critical nesting habitat for marsh birds.

aquatic.desc: Tall grass with feathery plumes forming dense stands in wetlands. Both native and invasive genotypes exist in LA. The invasive form is displacing native in some areas.

fm.Ecology: Native and invasive genotypes — invasive form spreading

aquatic.hp: Common reed — native genotype declining while invasive European genotype expands. Tall reed stands providing marsh bird habitat.

aquatic.desc: Beautiful lavender flowers on floating aquatic plant — one of the most destructive invasive plants on Earth. Cal-IPC High. Doubles biomass every 6-18 days. Clogs waterways worldwide. Present in LA River system.

fm.Origin: South America — Cal-IPC High. One of world's worst invasive species.
fm.Ecology: Doubles biomass every 6-18 days. Depletes oxygen, blocks light.
fm.Habitat: Slow waterways, ponds — LA River system

aquatic.hp: Aquatic habitat supporting fish, amphibian, and invertebrate communities. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

aquatic.desc: Tall sticky-leaved marsh plant with white flower clusters. Freshwater and brackish marshes. Similar to Mulefat but prefers wetter sites. Important marsh nectar source.
aquatic.hp: Important late-season nectar source in marsh habitats. Visited by native bees and butterflies.

aquatic.desc: Gray-green shrub of alkaline marshes and desert springs. Arrow-straight stems used by indigenous peoples for arrows and building. Pink flower clusters. Indicator of groundwater.

fm.Habitat: Alkaline marshes, desert springs — groundwater indicator
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Cahuilla used stems for arrows and construction

aquatic.hp: Groundwater indicator species. Tongva used straight stems for arrows and construction. Pink flowers visited by native bees.

aquatic.desc: Pink-purple flower clusters on aromatic marsh plant. Strong camphor-like odor. Important late-summer marsh nectar source. Freshwater and brackish wetlands.
aquatic.hp: Important late-summer nectar source in marsh habitats when few other wetland plants flower. Visited by butterflies and native bees.

aquatic.desc: Woolly-fruited sedge of stream banks and marsh margins. Rhizomatous — forms dense stands. Important for wetland restoration.

fm.Ecology: Important wetland restoration species

aquatic.hp: Wetland structural species providing nesting habitat for rails and Song Sparrows. Important for stream bank stabilization.

aquatic.desc: Large robust sedge of shady stream banks. Among the most common LA County riparian sedges. Important for stream bank stabilization and restoration.

fm.Ecology: Stream bank stabilization

aquatic.hp: Important stream bank stabilizer. Dense stands provide cover for Song Sparrow and other riparian birds. Common restoration species.

aquatic.desc: Small spikerush with blunt-tipped spikelet. Shallow marsh margins and vernal pool edges. Distinguished from E. macrostachya by blunt spikelet tip.

fm.Habitat: Shallow marsh margins, vernal pool edges

aquatic.hp: Vernal pool and marsh edge species. Provides habitat structure for aquatic invertebrates.

aquatic.desc: Pink flower spikes floating on or emergent from water. Grows in both aquatic and terrestrial forms. Important waterfowl food — seeds and foliage eaten.

fm.Form: Floating or emergent — two growth forms
fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Seeds and foliage eaten by waterfowl including Mallard, Cinnamon Teal, and American Coot. Amphibious — grows in both water and on land.

aquatic.desc: Feathery aquatic plant forming dense mats on still water. South American invasive choking ponds and slow streams. Fragments spread easily — nearly impossible to eradicate once established.

fm.Ecology: Forms dense mats blocking light and oxygen

aquatic.hp: Invasive — forms dense mats depleting oxygen and blocking light. Displaces native aquatic vegetation. Fragments spread to new sites.

aquatic.desc: Succulent salt marsh shrub — federally endangered. Once common in LA County salt marshes, now reduced to a few populations. Red-purple fall color.

fm.Leaves: Fleshy, linear, turning red-purple in fall
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered — few LA County populations remain

aquatic.hp: Federally endangered salt marsh specialist. Important habitat component for salt marsh invertebrates and Belding's Savannah Sparrow.

aquatic.desc: Hemiparasitic annual of salt marshes — federally endangered. Yellow-green hooded flowers. Depends on host plants (pickleweed, saltgrass) for water and nutrients. Very few remaining populations.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps pickleweed and saltgrass roots
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered — very few populations remain

aquatic.hp: Federally endangered hemiparasite of salt marshes. Depends on pickleweed and saltgrass hosts. Critical indicator of high-quality salt marsh habitat.

aquatic.desc: Robust triangular-stemmed sedge of brackish and alkaline marshes. Important waterfowl food — tubers and seeds eaten by many duck species. Forms dense stands.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food — tubers and seeds

aquatic.hp: Important waterfowl food — tubers eaten by Pintail, Canvasback, and Ruddy Duck. Dense stands provide nesting cover for marsh birds.

aquatic.desc: Iris-like leaves with spherical spiny seed heads. Freshwater marsh margins. Important food for waterfowl — seeds highly nutritious.
aquatic.hp: Seeds highly nutritious waterfowl food. Dense stands provide nesting habitat for marsh wrens and rails.

aquatic.desc: Low salt-tolerant annual of coastal salt marshes and alkali flats. Triangular fruiting bracts diagnostic. Important component of upper salt marsh zone.
aquatic.hp: Upper salt marsh specialist. Seeds eaten by sparrows and finches in coastal habitats.

aquatic.desc: Low mat-forming grass of salt marsh upper zones. One of the most salt-tolerant grasses — grows where almost nothing else can. Important for salt marsh upper zone restoration.

fm.Habitat: Salt marsh upper zones — extreme salt tolerance

aquatic.hp: Extremely salt-tolerant — grows in uppermost salt marsh zone. Important for salt marsh restoration. Provides microhabitat for salt marsh invertebrates.

aquatic.desc: Bright yellow button-like flowers on fleshy stems in shallow water and marsh margins. South African introduction now common in coastal wetlands. Often the most conspicuous flower in salt marshes.

fm.Origin: South African — naturalized in coastal wetlands

aquatic.hp: Non-native but provides nectar in marsh habitats. Yellow button flowers are the most conspicuous bloom in many LA salt marshes.

aquatic.desc: Succulent creeping shrub of salt marshes — one of the most salt-tolerant plants in LA County. Forms dense mats in lower salt marsh zone. Important food for salt marsh caterpillars.

fm.Habitat: Lower salt marsh zone — extreme salt tolerance

aquatic.hp: Lower salt marsh zone specialist — one of the most salt-tolerant plants in LA. Important habitat for salt marsh invertebrates and Western Snowy Plover.

mosses.desc: Tiny silver-green cushions in sidewalk cracks worldwide. One of the most urban-adapted mosses. Thrives in concrete environments.

fm.Season: Wet season only — desiccates in summer
fm.Ecology: Silver moss — one of most common urban mosses worldwide. Sidewalk cracks throughout LA.

mosses.hp: One of the most urban-adapted organisms on Earth — colonizes sidewalk cracks, rooftops, and parking lots worldwide. Pioneer species creating soil in concrete environments.

mosses.desc: Small moss on walls and rocks with hair-tipped leaves that twist when dry. The "corkscrew" motion gives it its name.

fm.Ecology: Wall screw moss — extremely common on concrete, walls, sidewalks.

mosses.hp: Wall screw-moss — tiny but extremely drought-tolerant. Survives on bare concrete and mortar. Twists when dry, unfurls within seconds when wetted.

mosses.desc: Pioneer moss after fire and disturbance. Bright green rosettes with curved pear-shaped capsules on long stalks. The capsule stalk twists with humidity changes.

fm.Size: Plants ½–1 in; stalks to 2 in
fm.Ecology: Bonfire moss — common in disturbed areas, post-fire sites.

mosses.hp: Pioneer after fire — one of the first organisms to colonize burned ground. Capsule stalk twists hygroscopically with humidity changes. Also called 'bonfire moss.'

mosses.desc: Star-shaped rosettes that revive dramatically from complete desiccation. One of the most drought-tolerant plants on Earth.

fm.Color: Green when wet, brown/curled when dry
fm.Drought: Survives years of complete desiccation — poikilohydric
fm.Ecology: Star moss — common desert and semi-arid moss. SoCal verified.

mosses.hp: Among the most drought-tolerant plants on Earth — survives years of complete desiccation and revives within minutes of wetting. Poikilohydric.

mosses.desc: Relatively large moss with stiff juniper-like leaves. Male plants with splash-cup structures. Mountain trails and banks.

fm.Color: Dark green with reddish capsule caps
fm.Ecology: Juniper haircap — mountain areas. Well-documented on iNat for SoCal.

mosses.hp: Juniper moss — distinctive with pointed leaf tips and reddish-brown capsules. Among the tallest mosses. Upright growth form like miniature juniper grove.

mosses.desc: Dark green moss growing on tree bark, especially oaks. Erect capsules among leaves. Most common epiphytic moss in LA.

fm.Ecology: Common on bark in SoCal oak woodland.

mosses.hp: Lyell's tree moss — fuzzy tufts on bark. Named for geologist Charles Lyell. Important bark microhabitat for mites and springtails.

mosses.desc: Creeping feathery moss in shady moist areas. Irregularly branching. One of the most common woodland floor mosses.

fm.Habitat: Shady moist woodland floor, rotting wood

mosses.hp: Feather moss — golden-green feathery mats on soil and logs. One of the most common woodland mosses. Creates humid microhabitat for springtails.

mosses.desc: Flat green thallus with umbrella-like reproductive structures. Not a true moss — a liverwort. Greenhouse and nursery floors.

fm.Habitat: Moist shady ground, greenhouses, nursery pots

mosses.hp: Not a true moss — a liverwort (distinct evolutionary lineage). Umbrella-like reproductive structures are diagnostic. Important soil stabilizer in nurseries and greenhouses.

mosses.desc: Forms pincushion-like mounds on tree bark. Dry-adapted — more common in SoCal than most mosses. Epiphytic.
mosses.hp: Pincushion moss — dense rounded cushions on tree branches. Distinctive julaceous (worm-like) branches. Fog-moisture specialist.

mosses.desc: Small gray-green cushions on rocks with distinctive white hair-tips. Very common on boulders and walls. Drought-adapted.

fm.Ecology: Cushion moss — common on exposed rock in SoCal mountains.

mosses.hp: Grey cushion moss — silvery cushions on rock and concrete. Hair-point leaf tips create distinctive silvery appearance. Desiccation-tolerant.

mosses.desc: Dark green to black cushions on exposed rock. Very drought-tolerant. One of the most common rock mosses in SoCal.
mosses.hp: Smooth rock moss — glossy dark cushions on exposed rock. Distinguished from G. pulvinata by smooth (not hair-pointed) leaf tips.

mosses.desc: Small green cushions with nodding pear-shaped capsules on long red stalks. Very common on walls and paths.
mosses.hp: Thread moss — distinctive pear-shaped capsules on long thin stalks. Among the most common urban mosses. Sidewalks and walls.

mosses.desc: Gray-green moss with white hair points. Forms extensive mats on sandy/rocky ground. Mountain trails.
mosses.hp: Hoary fringe-moss — distinctive white-tipped leaves giving frosted appearance. Sandy and gravelly soils. Important sand stabilizer.

mosses.desc: Distinctive swept-to-one-side leaves. Mountain forest floors. Named for its wind-blown appearance.

fm.Ecology: Broom moss — mountain conifer forest understory.

mosses.hp: Windswept moss — distinctive one-sided leaf orientation. Looks 'combed' by wind. Important nesting material for hummingbirds.

mosses.desc: Named for its spherical apple-like capsules. Mountain rock crevices and soil banks. Green glaucous cushions.
mosses.hp: Apple moss — spherical capsules resembling tiny green apples. Shaded rock ledges and banks. Beautiful under hand lens.

mosses.desc: Very common moss with distinctive reddish stalks (setae). Pioneer on bare soil, rooftops, and cracks. Cosmopolitan — found on every continent including Antarctica.

fm.Habitat: Bare soil, rooftops, paths — extremely common
fm.Ecology: Redshank moss — cosmopolitan. Found on every continent.

mosses.hp: Redshank moss — distinctive red-purple stems visible through translucent leaves. First moss to colonize burned ground after fire.

mosses.desc: Golden-green mats on dry rocks and soil in chaparral. One of the most common SoCal mosses. Distinctive golden sheen in sun. Branches irregular, not feathery.

fm.Substrate: Dry rocks, soil banks in chaparral
fm.Habitat: Very common in chaparral and oak woodland throughout LA County

mosses.hp: Mat moss — golden-brown mats on tree trunks. Somewhat drought-tolerant for a moss. Oak woodland specialist.

mosses.desc: Tiny bright green cushions on rock and mortar. Leaves twist spirally when dry (diagnostic). One of the most common rock mosses in urban LA - grows on walls, concrete, old brick.

fm.Color: Bright green when moist, yellow-brown when dry
fm.Habitat: Urban walls and rocks - very common

mosses.hp: Rock screw-moss — tiny cushions in rock crevices. Extremely desiccation-tolerant. Curls tightly when dry.

mosses.desc: Large springy moss with leaves bent sharply backward from stems - distinctive "squarrose" look. Mountain meadows. Touch it and it springs back.
mosses.hp: Springy turf-moss — distinctive squarrose (spreading) leaves. Bouncy when walked on. Common in lawns under light shade.

mosses.desc: Dense dark green mats draping tree bases and lower trunks in moist canyons. One of the most conspicuous mosses in SMM/SGM canyon bottoms. Glossy appearance.

fm.Habitat: Moist canyon bottoms - SMM and SGM

mosses.hp: Tree-skirt moss — forms skirt-like mats around tree bases. Distinctive crested branch tips. Pacific coast specialist.

mosses.desc: Long trailing stems with irregularly branched shoots in mountain forests. Moist conifer duff. Named for its lanky, sprawling growth habit.
mosses.hp: Lanky moss — large loose wefts in humid forests. Important water retention in forest floor. Sensitive to air pollution.

mosses.desc: Dense rounded cushions of pale blue-green moss. Unmistakable color - no other local moss is blue-green. Unique cell structure holds water like a sponge.

fm.Ecology: Sponge-like water retention - unique cell structure

mosses.hp: Palm tree moss — distinctive white-green cushions resembling miniature palm trees. Color from specialized hyaline cells storing water.

mosses.desc: The only truly aquatic moss in LA County - grows fully submerged in flowing streams. Long dark green trailing stems anchored to rocks. Name means "against fire" - historically used to fireproof roofs.

fm.Form: Aquatic - long trailing stems fully submerged
fm.Cultural: Name means "against fire" - historically packed around chimneys

mosses.hp: The only truly aquatic moss in LA County — grows fully submerged. Provides habitat for stream invertebrates. Name means 'against fire' — historically packed around chimneys.

mosses.desc: The iconic bog moss - pale green with unique water-holding cells. Extremely rare in SoCal - known from a few mountain seeps in SGM. Creates its own acidic habitat.

fm.Ecology: Creates its own acidic habitat. Holds 20x its weight in water.
fm.Habitat: Mountain seeps - extremely rare in SoCal
fm.Conservation: Very rare in LA County - a few SGM seeps only

mosses.hp: Holds 20x its dry weight in water. Creates its own acidic habitat (pH 4–5). Extremely rare in SoCal — a few SGM seeps only. Peat formation basis.

mosses.desc: One of the most common mosses worldwide. Curved yellow-green shoots in neat mats on bark, rock, and soil. The "default" moss people imagine. Curved leaves diagnostic.
mosses.hp: Thatch moss — curved leaves resembling miniature cypress fronds. Among the most common mosses worldwide. Forms dense mats on logs.

mosses.desc: Tiny bright green tufts in sidewalk cracks and between pavers. Related to the common Silver Moss but greener. Among the first colonizers of disturbed urban ground.
mosses.hp: Sidewalk bryum — tiny silver-green cushions in sidewalk cracks. One of the most drought-tolerant mosses. Urban specialist.

lichens.desc: Bright orange-yellow foliose lichen on trees and rocks. Nitrogen-loving — common near bird perches and dog-walking areas.

fm.Habitat: Tree bark, rocks, concrete — nitrogen-enriched sites
fm.Season: Year-round — grows fastest in wet months
fm.Ecology: Very common on urban trees and fences across LA.

lichens.hp: Nitrogen-loving — common near bird perches, dog walks, and vehicle exhaust. Bright orange color from anthraquinone pigments provides UV protection.

lichens.desc: Gray foliose lichen with net-like ridges (sulcate). Common on tree bark. Moderate air pollution tolerance.

fm.Ecology: Widespread in N. Hemisphere but uncommon in SoCal lowlands. Verify.

lichens.hp: Shield lichen — distinctively ridged and furrowed thallus with soredia in cracks. One of most studied lichens globally. Pollution biomonitor.

lichens.desc: California state lichen. Dramatic hanging net-like strands from tree branches. Fog-dependent. Air quality indicator — absent from polluted areas.

fm.Form: Fruticose — hanging net-like strands to 3 ft
fm.Conservation: CA state lichen — air quality indicator
fm.Ecology: CA State Lichen. Hasse collected extensively in LA. Common on coast live oaks.

lichens.hp: CA state lichen. Fog-dependent — absorbs moisture directly from marine layer. Absence from formerly occupied areas indicates declining air quality. Important nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow foliose lichen on conifer bark in mountain forests. Contains vulpinic acid — toxic yellow pigment historically used as wolf poison in Europe.

fm.Toxicity: Vulpinic acid — historically used as wolf poison
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer bark — San Gabriel/San Bernardino Mts

lichens.hp: Powdered sunshine lichen — bright yellow-green foliose. Mountain conifer bark specialist. Contains vulpinic acid.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green crustose lichen forming map-like patterns on rock. Extremely slow-growing — used for dating rock surfaces (lichenometry). Some colonies centuries old.

fm.Growth: Extremely slow — used for rock dating (lichenometry)
fm.Ecology: Map lichen on high-elevation granite. San Gabriel Mountains.

lichens.hp: Map lichen — the iconic 'map-like' pattern used for lichenometric dating. Fluorescent yellow-green areoles on black hypothallus. Among the world's longest-lived organisms — some thalli estimated 8,000+ years old.

lichens.desc: Small podetia with bright red apothecia on tips. On decaying wood and humus in mountain forests. The SoCal equivalent of eastern British Soldiers.

fm.Habitat: Decaying wood, humus in mountain conifer forest

lichens.hp: Lipstick cladonia — distinctive red apothecia ('lipstick') on white podetia. Forest floor and rotting wood. Acid rain indicator.

lichens.desc: Large gray-brown foliose lichen growing on soil and mossy banks. Undersides with white veins and rhizines. Contains cyanobacteria partner.

fm.Ecology: Dog lichen on soil and moss in mountain forests.

lichens.hp: Dog lichen — large foliose with dog-tooth apothecia. Cyanolichen fixing atmospheric nitrogen. Important soil crusts in grasslands.

lichens.desc: Large single-lobed lichen attached to rock by a central cord. Dark brown-black. Eaten as emergency survival food in some cultures.

fm.Form: Foliose — single large lobe, attached centrally
fm.Habitat: Rock faces, boulders in mountain areas
fm.Use: Emergency survival food in some traditions
fm.Ecology: Rock tripe on granite. San Gabriel Mountains.

lichens.hp: Rock tripe — single central holdfast. Brown-black foliose on exposed granitic rocks. Edible in emergencies (soaked and boiled).

lichens.desc: Small orange foliose lichen with powdery soredia on margins. Very common on city trees and fences. Pollution-tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Very common orange lichen on bark. Well-documented.

lichens.hp: Powder sunburst — orange foliose with granular soredia. Common on urban trees and concrete. Nitrogen-loving.

lichens.desc: Pendant greenish beard-like lichen hanging from tree branches. Central elastic cord inside strands (pull to test — unique to Usnea). Air quality indicator.

fm.ID: Pull strand gently — central white elastic cord is diagnostic for Usnea

lichens.hp: Old man's beard — pendant lichen with elastic central cord (pull gently to test). Multiple species in LA County mountains. Extremely slow-growing — large specimens may be decades old.

lichens.desc: Pale greenish-gray crustose lichen forming rosettes on concrete and rock. One of the most common urban lichens. Very pollution-tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Extremely common on concrete/sidewalks in LA. Among most-observed lichens on iNat.

lichens.hp: Rim lichen — concentric rosettes of olive-green lobes on rock and concrete. Pioneer colonizer of urban walls and sidewalks.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with crater-like fruiting bodies (apothecia). Rock surfaces. The craters are distinctive with a hand lens.

fm.Ecology: Common on limestone and concrete in SoCal.

lichens.hp: Crater lichen — immersed crustose with sunken crater-like apothecia. Parasitizes other lichens when young, then becomes independent.

lichens.desc: Tiny goblet-shaped structures on soil. Green granular surface. Often among mosses. The "cups" collect rainwater and dispersal propagules.

fm.Ecology: Pixie cup lichen on soil. Widely distributed.

lichens.hp: Pixie cup — goblet-shaped podetia collecting rainwater. Forest floor specialist. Reproduces via granular soredia inside cups.

lichens.desc: Powdery blue-green crust on tree bark and shady rock. No distinct form — just powder. Very common but overlooked.

fm.ID: Powdery texture distinguishes from crustose lichens
fm.Ecology: Common dust lichen but name may be misapplied for SoCal populations.

lichens.hp: Dust lichen — entirely leprose (powdery), never forming distinct lobes. Sheltered rock and bark surfaces. Shade specialist.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow-green crustose lichen on exposed rock. Very common on granite boulders at all elevations.

fm.Ecology: Common on exposed rock. San Gabriel Mts.

lichens.hp: Golden moonglow — dark crustose with yellow-green pruinose disk. Exposed alpine and subalpine rock faces.

lichens.desc: Gray-white branching crust on volcanic rock and concrete. Looks like tiny coral. Interesting dual symbiosis — green algae plus cyanobacteria.

fm.Ecology: Primarily volcanic substrates. Verify for LA County.

lichens.hp: Rock foam — stalked pseudopodetia with cephalodia containing nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria. Volcanic rock specialist.

lichens.desc: Branching fruticose lichen used historically in perfumery (oakmoss). Hanging from oak branches. Air quality indicator.

fm.Cultural: Oakmoss — used in perfumery
fm.Ecology: Reported from oak woodland in SoCal mountains.

lichens.hp: Oak moss — important perfumery ingredient. Pendant lichen on oaks. Used as fixative in fragrances since ancient times.

lichens.desc: Smooth white crustose lichen giving trees a whitewashed appearance. Very common on smooth-barked trees. Often overlooked as lichen.

fm.ID: Looks like white paint on bark
fm.Ecology: Primarily NorCal/Pacific NW. May not reach LA County.

lichens.hp: Whitewash lichen — smooth white crustose on bark resembling whitewash paint. Distinguished from Lepraria by smooth surface.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright yellow-green foliose lichen on tree bark. Very small but very common in urban areas. Often grows alongside Sunburst Lichen.

fm.Ecology: Very common on bark in urban areas. Well-documented.

lichens.hp: Candleflame lichen — bright egg-yolk yellow foliose. Common on urban trees and wooden fences. Nitrogen-loving.

lichens.desc: Dark olive-black gelatinous lichen forming biological soil crust in desert. Swells dramatically when wet. Critical for soil stability and nitrogen fixation in desert ecosystems.

fm.Form: Foliose — swells when wet, shrivels when dry
fm.Ecology: Biological soil crust — nitrogen fixing, soil stabilizing

lichens.hp: Critical component of biological soil crust — stabilizes soil, fixes nitrogen, and prevents erosion in desert. EXTREMELY FRAGILE — decades to recover from a single footstep.

lichens.desc: Gray-green foliose lichen with distinctive network of ridges on surface (isidia). Very common on rocks and tree bark. One of the most frequently encountered lichens in SoCal mountains.

fm.Habitat: Common on rocks and bark - SMM and SGM

lichens.hp: Hammered shield lichen — network of ridges creating hammered-metal appearance. Important biomonitor for heavy metals.

lichens.desc: Small gray-white rosettes with distinctive hooded lobe tips (soralia). Extremely common on urban trees and fences. One of the most pollution-tolerant lichens - thrives in LA.

fm.Ecology: Very pollution-tolerant - thrives in urban LA

lichens.hp: Hooded rosette — distinctive hood-shaped lobe tips (soredia inside hoods). Common on urban trees. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Neat gray-white star-shaped rosettes on tree bark. Common on oaks throughout LA County. Among the first lichens beginners learn to recognize.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland - very common throughout LA

lichens.hp: Star rosette — distinctive star-shaped gray lobes radiating from center. Bark specialist. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Brown foliose lichen with tiny dark isidia (pepper-like). Moist shady habitats. Contains cyanobacteria - fixes nitrogen. Sensitive to air pollution.

fm.Ecology: Contains cyanobacteria - nitrogen fixer. Air pollution indicator.

lichens.hp: Peppered moon lichen — large foliose with cyphellae (pores) on lower surface. Cyanolichen — fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

lichens.desc: Large gray-green foliose lichen with broad ruffled lobes and soredia along margins. Common on oak bark. One of the largest and most conspicuous SoCal lichens.

fm.Size: 4-12 in across - among the largest SoCal lichens
fm.Color: Gray-green upper, black below with brown bare zone at margins

lichens.hp: Ruffle lichen — large ruffled gray-green lobes with dark cilia. Important woodland epiphyte. Pollution-sensitive.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen tightly pressed to rock surfaces. Very common on exposed boulders and outcrops throughout SoCal. Tolerates full sun and heat.

fm.Ecology: Tolerates full sun, heat, and drought
fm.Habitat: Exposed boulders and outcrops - very common

lichens.hp: Cumberland rock shield — loosely attached foliose on rock. Brown below. Can detach and blow as 'vagrant lichen.'

lichens.desc: Large dark brown single-lobed lichen attached to rock by central point (umbilicus). Blistered upper surface. Mountain rocks. Edible in emergencies but bitter.

fm.Form: Foliose, single lobe attached by central point
fm.Cultural: Edible in emergencies but very bitter

lichens.hp: Blistered rock tripe — single central holdfast on alpine rocks. Edible in emergencies. Arctic-alpine distribution.

lichens.desc: Brilliant chartreuse-yellow shrubby lichen on conifer bark. Toxic - historically used to poison wolves and foxes in Europe (hence the name). One of the most eye-catching mountain lichens.

fm.Toxicity: Contains vulpinic acid - historically used as wolf/fox poison

lichens.hp: Brilliant chartreuse-yellow — unmistakable. Contains vulpinic acid — historically used to poison wolves and foxes in Europe (hence the name). Toxic but not through skin contact.

lichens.desc: Long pale green-yellow pendulous strands hanging from conifer branches. Mountain forests. Distinguished from Usnea by lack of central cord. Important bird nesting material.

fm.Ecology: Important bird nesting material
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forest - SGM upper elevations

lichens.hp: Witch hair lichen — long pendant strands in mountain forests. Indicator of old-growth forest. Extremely slow-growing.

lichens.desc: Erect gray-green finger-like branches in coastal fog zone. Fog-dependent - absorbs moisture from marine layer. Sensitive to air pollution. Indicator of clean coastal air.

fm.Ecology: Fog-dependent - absorbs moisture from marine layer. Air quality indicator.

lichens.hp: Photosynthetic symbiosis between fungus and algae/cyanobacteria. Bioindicator of air quality. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

lichens.desc: Small bright yellow-green foliose lichen on mountain conifer bark. Closely related to V. canadensis. SGM pine forest.
lichens.hp: Powdered sunshine — bright yellow-green foliose on conifers. Contains pinastric acid. Mountain forest specialist.

lichens.desc: White crust on smooth bark with black line-like fruiting bodies resembling Egyptian hieroglyphics. Common on smooth-barked trees. Among the most distinctive and easily recognized crustose lichens.

fm.Color: White crust with black script-like lines

lichens.hp: Script lichen — black linear lirellae (fruiting bodies) resembling handwritten text on bark. Smooth-barked tree specialist.

lichens.desc: Brilliant orange-red crustose lichen forming small rosettes on rocks. Common on calcareous rock and concrete. One of the most colorful and conspicuous crustose lichens.

fm.Habitat: Very common on rocks, walls, concrete throughout LA

lichens.hp: Firedot lichen — orange-yellow crustose on calcareous rock. Distinctive orange apothecia. Important pioneer on cement.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow-orange powdery crust on concrete, mortar, and bird-perching rocks. The most common urban lichen - look at any concrete wall in LA. Nitrogen-loving.

fm.Ecology: Nitrophilous - loves nitrogen from bird droppings and urban runoff
fm.Habitat: Every concrete wall in LA - the most common urban lichen

lichens.hp: Sidewalk firedot — bright yellow-orange on concrete and mortar. Among the most common urban lichens in LA.

lichens.desc: Gray-white crustose lichen with small wart-like soralia. Tastes extremely bitter (the name "amara" means bitter). Common on oak bark. Chemical test: C+ red.

fm.Taste: Extremely bitter (diagnostic - but do not taste regularly)

lichens.hp: Wart lichen — extremely bitter taste (diagnostic). White crustose with conspicuous soralia. Bark specialist.

lichens.desc: Gray-white crust with striking blood-red fruiting discs (apothecia). Shady rocks and bark. The red discs make this one of the most visually dramatic crustose lichens.
lichens.hp: Blood-spot lichen — distinctive red-brown apothecia on pale thallus. Rock and bark. Blood-red color from haematommone pigment.

lichens.desc: Pink-brown squamulose lichen forming soil crusts in desert. Part of the cryptobiotic soil community critical for desert ecosystem function. Extremely fragile - destroyed by foot traffic.

fm.Ecology: Part of cryptobiotic soil crust - critical for desert nitrogen fixation and erosion control
fm.Toxicity: EXTREMELY FRAGILE - decades to recover from footstep

lichens.hp: Part of cryptobiotic soil crust community critical for desert nitrogen fixation and erosion control. Pink-brown scales on soil. Destroyed by foot and vehicle traffic.

lichens.desc: Unattached lichen that tumbles across desert flats with the wind - a lichen tumbleweed. Yellow-green, crumpled. One of the most unusual lichen life strategies anywhere.

fm.Form: Foliose but UNATTACHED - rolls like tumbleweed
fm.Ecology: Vagrant life strategy - tumbles across desert with wind

lichens.hp: Vagrant lichen — unattached, tumbles across desert flats with wind like a tumbleweed. One of the most unusual life strategies in any organism.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen with tiny granular isidia covering surface like black pepper. Very common on exposed rocks and boulders in full sun. Loosely attached.

fm.Color: Yellow-green with dark isidia like pepper
fm.Substrate: Exposed rocks, boulders in full sun
fm.Habitat: Very common on sunny rocks throughout LA County

lichens.hp: Peppered rock shield — olive-brown foliose with isidia. One of the most common rock lichens in temperate regions.

lichens.desc: Single large leathery lobe attached to rock by central point. Smooth dark brown upper surface, black below. Mountain rocks. Closely related to U. phaea.

fm.Form: Foliose, single lobe from central umbilicus
fm.Color: Dark brown upper, black below, smooth

lichens.hp: Smooth rock tripe — multiple leaf-like lobes from single point. Exposed mountain rocks. Edible in emergencies.

lichens.desc: Tiny gray-white rosettes with powdery lobe tips (soredia). Common on urban trees, fences, and concrete. Very similar to P. adscendens but with flatter lobe tips.

fm.Ecology: Pollution-tolerant urban lichen
fm.Habitat: Urban trees and structures - very common

lichens.hp: Powder-tipped rosette — narrow gray lobes with powdery tips. Common on deciduous trees. Distinguished from P. adscendens by open (not hooded) soralia.

lichens.desc: Large yellow-green foliose lichen with tiny raised dots (soredia) on surface. Common on oaks and other hardwoods. One of the more conspicuous bark lichens in LA.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland - common throughout LA mountains

lichens.hp: Speckled greenshield — yellow-green with soredia. One of the most common foliose lichens on urban trees. Moderately pollution-tolerant.
