lichens.desc: Brilliant orange bushy lichen on tree twigs and branches. Becoming rare due to air pollution sensitivity. One of the most spectacular SoCal lichens when found.

fm.Ecology: Air pollution sensitive - declining
fm.Habitat: Coastal trees - becoming rare in LA

lichens.hp: Orange bush lichen — bright orange bushy lichen on twigs. Golden-eye apothecia distinctive. Declining due to air pollution — formerly common, now increasingly rare.

lichens.desc: Pale yellow-green hanging strands with a central cord (pull apart a strand to see white elastic cord inside). Mountain conifers. The central cord distinguishes all Usnea from Alectoria.

fm.Test: Pull strand apart - white elastic central cord visible (diagnostic for Usnea)

lichens.hp: Yellow beard lichen — pendant with yellow medulla. Elastic central cord diagnostic for Usnea. Mountain conifer specialist.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright yellow-green rosettes on conifer bark at mountain elevations. Forms dense colonies. Distinctive color against dark bark makes it easy to spot.

fm.Form: Tiny foliose rosettes in dense colonies

lichens.hp: Green starburst — small yellow-green foliose on conifer bark. Subnival (under-snow) specialist. Mountain forests.

lichens.desc: Blue-gray crust with conspicuous black fruiting discs. Very common on shaded rocks. The black discs on pale background are distinctive.
lichens.hp: Smoky-eye boulder lichen — white crustose with distinctive blue-gray discs. Granitic rock specialist. Acid-loving.

lichens.desc: White crust dotted with small brown fruiting bodies resembling chocolate chips. Coastal rocks and mortar. Charming appearance.
lichens.hp: Chocolate chip lichen — dark apothecia on pale crustose thallus. Mediterranean limestone specialist. Range-edge in LA County.

lichens.desc: Brilliant orange rosettes on rock, especially where birds perch (nitrogen-enriched). More vivid and neatly circular than X. parietina. Mountain rocks.

fm.Ecology: Nitrophilous - indicates bird droppings
fm.Habitat: Mountain rocks - especially near raptor perches

lichens.hp: Elegant sunburst — brilliant orange rosettes on nitrogen-enriched rocks. Color from parietin pigment. Used for lichenometric dating in Arctic.

lichens.desc: Dark olive-black gelatinous crust on desert soil. Part of cryptobiotic crust community. Swells dramatically when wet, shrinks when dry. Nitrogen fixer via cyanobacteria.

fm.Form: Gelatinous when wet, crustose when dry
fm.Ecology: Cryptobiotic soil crust - nitrogen fixer via cyanobacteria
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Antelope Valley - do not step on

lichens.hp: Soil crust jelly lichen — swells dramatically when wet. Cyanolichen fixing nitrogen. Critical for desert soil crust integrity.

lichens.desc: Another vagrant (unattached) lichen of desert flats. Darker green than X. chlorochroa. Accumulates in wind-deposited piles against fences and rocks in Antelope Valley.

fm.Ecology: Vagrant - tumbles across desert; accumulates against obstacles

lichens.hp: Tumbleweed shield — loosely attached foliose that detaches and blows across desert. One of few vagrant (unattached) lichens.

lichens.desc: Large wrinkled yellow-green foliose lichen on tree bark — the most common large lichen on oaks in LA County. Wrinkled surface with granular soredia in center. Closely related to F. flaventior but wrinkled (not dotted).

fm.Surface: Wrinkled with granular soredia in center (vs raised dots in F. flaventior)
fm.Size: 3-10 in across — often the largest lichen on a given oak
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — the most common large lichen on LA oaks
fm.vs: Speckled Greenshield (F. flaventior) has raised dot soredia; Common Greenshield has wrinkled surface soredia

lichens.hp: Common greenshield — one of most frequently encountered foliose lichens. Gray-green, wrinkled. Bark specialist on hardwoods.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen with powdery soredia along lobe margins (vs laminal soredia in F. flaventior). On bark and rock. Distinguished from Speckled Greenshield by marginal rather than surface soredia placement.

fm.Surface: Soredia along LOBE MARGINS (vs on surface in F. flaventior)
fm.vs: Speckled Greenshield (F. flaventior) has soredia arising from surface pseudocyphellae; Powder-edged has soredia concentrated at lobe margins

lichens.hp: Powder-edged greenshield — sorediate variant of the Flavopunctelia complex. Common on oaks and hardwoods.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen on ROCK (not bark). Distinguished from Common Greenshield (F. caperata) by pustular isidia-like outgrowths instead of granular soredia. Prefers exposed rock in chaparral.

fm.Habitat: Exposed rock in chaparral and woodland
fm.vs: Common Greenshield (F. caperata) grows on BARK with granular soredia; Rock Greenshield grows on ROCK with pustular outgrowths

lichens.hp: Rock greenshield — yellow-green foliose on acidic rock. Distinguished from bark-dwelling congeners by rock substrate.

lichens.desc: Tiny greenish-gray horn-shaped stalks (podetia) growing from a crust of squamules on rotting wood and bark bases. Powdery soredia cover the horns. One of the most common Cladonia species in moist SoCal mountain habitats.

fm.Form: Fruticose — tiny horn-shaped podetia 0.5-2 cm tall rising from basal squamules
fm.Substrate: Rotting wood, bark bases, mossy stumps
fm.Habitat: Moist mountain forest — SMM and SGM canyons
fm.vs: Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata) has CUP-SHAPED tops; Powderhorn has POINTED tops. Lipstick Cladonia (C. macilenta) has RED tips.

lichens.hp: Common powderhorn — narrow podetia with farinose soredia. Forest floor and rotting logs. Important ground-cover lichen.

lichens.desc: Slender gray-green stalks topped with neat trumpet-shaped cups — like tiny goblets. Powdery soredia cover the cups. On soil, rotting wood, and mossy banks. More delicate than Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata).

fm.Form: Fruticose — slender stalks with neat trumpet/goblet cups at top
fm.Color: Gray-green with powdery soredia on cups
fm.vs: Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata) has wider, rougher cups with granular cortex; Trumpet Lichen has slender, neat cups with powdery soredia

lichens.hp: Trumpet lichen — elegant goblet-shaped podetia flaring to trumpet form. Forest floor specialist. Indicator of intact forest.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow cobblestone-like crustose lichen on desert rock — one of the most common lichens in the Mojave. Pioneer species. Yellow color from rhizocarpic acid.

fm.Form: Crustose, areolate to squamulose — cobblestone pattern
fm.Habitat: Desert rock surfaces — extremely common in Mojave portion of LA County
fm.vs: Map Lichen (Rhizocarpon geographicum) is also yellow-green on rock but has black prothallus lines between areoles; Cobblestone lacks black lines

lichens.hp: Yellow cobblestone lichen — bright yellow areoles on desert pavement. Important component of biological soil crust preventing erosion.

lichens.desc: Dark brown cobblestone crustose lichen — the brown counterpart to A. socialis. Among the most common rock lichens worldwide. Often overlooked due to drab color.

fm.Substrate: Rock — extremely common on any siliceous rock
fm.vs: Yellow Cobblestone (A. socialis) is bright yellow; Brown Cobblestone is dark brown. Both have similar areolate texture.

lichens.hp: Brown cobblestone — small brown areoles creating cobblestone pattern. Among the most common crustose rock lichens worldwide.

lichens.desc: Vivid orange-red crustose lichen on rock and concrete — the most conspicuous firedot lichen. Often the brightest spot of color on urban walls and gravestones.

fm.Color: Vivid ORANGE-RED (brighter and redder than C. flavovirescens)
fm.Substrate: Rock, concrete, mortar — loves calcareous substrates
fm.Habitat: Urban walls, gravestones, concrete bridges — very common
fm.vs: Firedot Lichen (C. flavovirescens) is more yellow-orange; Red Firedot is distinctly REDDER. Sidewalk Firedot (C. citrina) is powdery yellow.

lichens.hp: Red firedot — deep orange-red crustose on calcareous rock. Distinguished from C. citrina by darker red color.

lichens.desc: Tiny orange foliose lichen with hooded lobe tips on bark — named after early LA lichenologist Hermann Hasse. Very common on urban trees throughout LA.

fm.Form: Foliose, small, with hooded lobe tips
fm.Habitat: City trees, oak bark — one of the most common bark lichens in LA
fm.vs: Powder Sunburst (X. fallax) has powdery soredia; Hooded Sunburst has hooded lobe tips without soredia

lichens.hp: Hooded sunburst — orange foliose with hood-shaped lobe tips. Common on urban trees in LA. Named for pioneering LA lichenologist Hasse.

lichens.desc: Black crustose lichen on limestone, concrete, and mortar — probably the most overlooked lichen in LA because it looks like a dark stain. Tiny pimple-like perithecia.

fm.Form: Crustose — thin black crust, often cracked
fm.Surface: Tiny raised dots (perithecia) — look like pimples
fm.Substrate: Limestone, concrete, mortar — very common on urban surfaces
fm.Habitat: Sidewalks, walls, bridges — everywhere in urban LA
fm.vs: Looks like a dark stain, not a lichen. Black shield lichens (Buellia) have disc-shaped apothecia instead of pimple-like perithecia.

lichens.hp: Common rockdweller — black crustose lichen on limestone and concrete. Endolithic — penetrates rock surface. Extremely common on urban structures.

lichens.desc: Aquatic lichen forming black patches on rocks in streams and seeps — one of the few truly aquatic lichens. Often mistaken for wet rock discoloration.

fm.Form: Crustose — thin black film on submerged or splash-zone rock
fm.Habitat: Permanently wet rock — streams, waterfalls, seeps
fm.vs: Looks like wet rock discoloration. Distinguished by tiny raised perithecia visible with hand lens.

lichens.hp: Freshwater verrucaria — black crustose on periodically submerged stream rocks. Semi-aquatic lifestyle unusual for lichens.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow powdery crust on bark in shaded locations — looks like someone sprinkled gold dust on the tree. No organized thallus structure.

fm.Form: Leprose — pure powder, no organized thallus
fm.vs: Not a true lichen by some definitions (no organized thallus). Distinguished from Candelariella by purely powdery form with no crust beneath.

lichens.hp: Gold dust lichen — powdery bright yellow crust on bark. Looks like yellow spray paint. Entirely leprose — never forms lobes or apothecia.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright egg-yolk yellow crustose lichen on rock, concrete, and bark. Extremely common but easily overlooked due to small size. Often the first lichen to colonize bare rock.

fm.Substrate: Rock, concrete, bark, wood — very versatile
fm.Habitat: Everywhere — one of the most common urban lichens
fm.vs: Smaller and more granular than Sunburst Lichen (Xanthoria). Gold Dust (Chrysothrix) is purely powdery; Egg-yolk has a granular crust.

lichens.hp: Egg-yolk lichen — brilliant yellow crustose on rock. Named for egg-yolk color. Nitrogen-loving — common on bird-perch rocks.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with prominent black button-like apothecia on bark — one of the most common gray crustose lichens on trees in LA.

fm.Form: Crustose — gray thallus with black disc apothecia
fm.vs: Distinguished from Lecidella by bark substrate and larger apothecia. Hooded Rosette (Physcia) is foliose, not crustose.

lichens.hp: Button lichen — tiny black apothecia on gray-green thallus. Common but overlooked on tree bark. Crustose.

lichens.desc: Thin gray crust with tiny black discs — perhaps the single most common crustose lichen on bark in temperate regions. Usually identified only to genus in the field.

fm.Color: Gray with tiny black disc apothecia
fm.Habitat: Any bark surface — one of the most common bark crustose lichens worldwide
fm.vs: Very similar to B. griseovirens; distinguished mainly by thallus chemistry (field ID to genus is usually sufficient)

lichens.hp: Disc lichen — tiny black-dotted crustose on bark and wood. Important early colonizer of exposed wood surfaces.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with olive-brown to black discs — extremely common on bark. Usually the most abundant crustose lichen on deciduous trees.

fm.Color: Gray crust with olive-brown to black disc apothecia
fm.vs: Rim Lichen (Lecanora muralis) has rimmed apothecia; Lecidella has flat discs without a rim

lichens.hp: Olive disc lichen — olive-brown apothecia on bark. Common epiphyte. Important component of bark lichen communities.

lichens.desc: Small dark olive-green foliose lichen forming neat rosettes on bark and rock — very common in urban environments. Tolerates high nitrogen from vehicle emissions.

fm.Color: Dark olive-green to brown when dry, greener when wet
fm.vs: Hooded Rosette (Physcia adscendens) is gray-white; Shadow Lichen is distinctly darker olive-green

lichens.hp: Shadow lichen — dark gray-brown foliose forming neat orbicular rosettes. Extremely common on urban trees. Very pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Tiny dark brown foliose lichen tightly pressed to bark — one of the most common but most overlooked urban bark lichens. So small and flat it looks crustose.

fm.Form: Foliose but very tightly appressed — appears almost crustose
fm.Habitat: City trees — very common, very overlooked
fm.vs: Appears crustose but has tiny lobe tips visible with hand lens. Shadow Lichen (Phaeophyscia) is similar color but larger and more obviously foliose.

lichens.hp: Powdered ruffle lichen — tightly appressed dark foliose. Among the most pollution-tolerant foliose lichens. Thrives on urban trees.

lichens.desc: Medium brown foliose lichen with tiny isidia on the surface — common on oaks. Distinguished from similar species by brown color and granular isidia.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — common in SGM and SMM canyons
fm.vs: Shield Lichen (Parmelia sulcata) is gray with ridges; Brown-eyed Sunshine is distinctly brown with isidia

lichens.hp: Brown-eyed sunshine — brown foliose with isidia. Distinguished from M. californica by chemistry. Bark specialist.

lichens.desc: White-fringed strap-like foliose lichen on trees in fog-belt habitats — dramatic ciliate lobe margins. A coastal fog indicator species.

fm.Form: Foliose, strap-like lobes with dramatic white cilia on margins
fm.Habitat: Coastal fog-belt — indicator of persistent fog
fm.vs: Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) is pendant; Fringed Strap is appressed with fringed margins

lichens.hp: Fringed strap lichen — distinctive white-ciliate margins. Tropical and subtropical. At range edge in LA County.

lichens.desc: White to chalky gray crustose lichen on limestone and concrete — extremely common on LA sidewalks and retaining walls. White rimmed apothecia.

fm.Form: Crustose — thick chalky white to gray
fm.Habitat: Urban sidewalks, retaining walls — very common on calcareous surfaces
fm.vs: Rim Lichen (Lecanora muralis) has lobed margins; Chalk-rim is more uniformly crustose without lobes

lichens.hp: Chalk-rim lichen — white crustose on calcareous rock. Important pioneer on limestone. Extremely long-lived.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with sunken dimple-like apothecia — one of the most common gray crustose lichens on siliceous rock worldwide.

fm.vs: Chalk-rim (A. calcarea) prefers calcareous rock and is whiter; Rock Dimple prefers siliceous rock and is grayer

lichens.hp: Rock dimple lichen — gray crustose with sunken apothecia creating dimpled surface. Common on siliceous rocks.

lichens.desc: Gray-white foliose lichen with black peppery soredia — extremely common on urban trees in warm areas. A nitrogen-lover indicative of vehicle exhaust.

fm.Habitat: City trees near roads — nitrogen pollution indicator
fm.vs: Similar to Physcia but has characteristic dark/peppery soredia vs the white-tipped soredia of Physcia species

lichens.hp: Pepper lichen — gray-white crustose with conspicuous dark soralia. Tropical to warm-temperate bark specialist.

lichens.desc: Thin gray crustose lichen with dark-rimmed apothecia on bark — very common but requires a hand lens to appreciate. Often overlooked.

fm.Color: Gray with brown-black apothecia with pale rim
fm.vs: Similar to Buellia but apothecia have a pale rim (lecanorine). Buellia has unrimmed (lecideine) apothecia.

lichens.hp: Bark rim lichen — tiny crustose with brown-ringed apothecia. Common but requires hand lens. Oak bark specialist.

lichens.desc: Stonewall Rim Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 120 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Common rock lichen with distinctive concentric rings. Important in urban ecology — among the most pollution-tolerant lichens. Pioneer species on concrete and mortar.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Golden Hair-lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 68 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native golden hair lichen — bright orange-yellow filamentous tufts on coastal shrubs. Indicator of clean coastal air. Becoming rarer due to air quality decline.

lichens.desc: Brown-eyed Wolf Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 55 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Brown-eyed wolf lichen — similar to L. vulpina but with brown apothecia instead of yellow. Contains vulpinic acid. Mountain conifer specialist.

lichens.desc: Green Rock-posy — a lichen found in LA County with 37 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native rock-posy lichen forming distinctive green-yellow rosettes with dark centers on exposed alpine rocks. Important pioneer on bare rock surfaces.

lichens.desc: Farinose Cartilage Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 32 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native cartilage lichen with sorediate (powdery-surfaced) straps on tree bark. Nitrogen cycling contributor. Sensitive to air pollution — indicator of air quality.

lichens.desc: Lesser Salted Rocktripe Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 25 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Rock tripe lichen anchored by single central holdfast. Edible in emergencies. Grows on exposed mountain rocks. Extremely slow-growing — large specimens may be centuries old.

lichens.desc: Elegant Sunburst Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 23 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Brilliant orange lichen on nitrogen-enriched rocks — especially bird perching stones. Color from anthraquinone pigments provides UV protection. Cosmopolitan distribution.

lichens.desc: gold cobblestone lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 19 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Bright chartreuse-yellow cobblestone lichen on exposed acidic rocks. Forms distinctive small lobes. Alpine and subalpine specialist. UV-protective pigments.

lichens.desc: Pin-cushion Sunburst Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 14 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Pin-cushion sunburst lichen forming small orange-yellow cushions on bark and rock. Common on urban trees. Tolerates moderate air pollution.

lichens.desc: Non-native. White Pleated Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 10 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native white pleated lichen forming distinctive white crustose patches on bark and concrete. Nitrogen-loving — common near agricultural areas.

lichens.desc: Soil Paint Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 9 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native soil paint lichen forming bright yellow-green crusts on desert rocks. Among the most conspicuous desert rock lichens.

lichens.desc: Protoparmeliopsis species — a lichen found in LA County with 8 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native Sierra rim lichen — crustose lichen on mountain rocks. High-elevation specialist. Related to the common P. muralis.

lichens.desc: Mealy Pixie Cup — a lichen found in LA County with 7 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native mealy pixie cup — goblet-shaped podetia on forest floor and rotting wood. Indicator of intact forest understory ecology.

lichens.desc: Beaded Rosette Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 7 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native beaded rosette lichen — gray-green foliose lichen on bark. Beaded margins diagnostic. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Waxy Firedot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 7 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native waxy firedot lichen — bright orange crustose lichen on coastal rocks. UV-protective anthraquinone pigments.

lichens.desc: Red Firedot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 7 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native red firedot lichen — bright orange-red crustose lichen on rock. UV-protective anthraquinone pigments. Desert rock specialist.

lichens.desc: Orange Atoms — a lichen found in LA County with 7 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native orange atoms — tiny orange squamules on rock. Among the smallest visible lichens. Desert rock specialist.

lichens.desc: Frosted Rim-lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 6 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native frosted rim-lichen — gray-blue crustose lichen on bark. Moderately pollution-tolerant. Common on urban trees.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Niebla species — a lichen found in LA County with 6 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native fruticose fog lichen of coastal habitats. Depends on marine fog moisture. Indicator of coastal fog frequency.

lichens.desc: hoary cobblestone lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 6 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native hoary cobblestone lichen — brown-gray crustose lichen on exposed rocks. Pioneer species on bare rock surfaces.

lichens.desc: Mortar Rim Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 6 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native mortar rim lichen — crustose lichen colonizing mortar, concrete, and limestone. Common on urban structures.

lichens.desc: Rock Nipple Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 5 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native rock nipple lichen — tiny dark fruiting bodies on rock. Specialized desert rock colonizer.

lichens.desc: Powdery Sea-Fog Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 5 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native powdery sea-fog lichen — coastal fog-dependent. Sorediate tips. Important for coastal nutrient cycling.

lichens.desc: Acarospora species — a lichen found in LA County with 5 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native — yellow-green rosette-forming cobblestone lichen on rock. Desert and mountain rock specialist.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Dimelaena species — a lichen found in LA County with 4 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — radiating crustose lichen on rock. Black-and-white pattern diagnostic. Rock colonizer.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Flame Firedot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 4 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native flame firedot lichen — bright orange crustose on rock. UV-protective pigments. Spreading on urban substrates.

lichens.desc: Parasitic Firedot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 4 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native parasitic firedot lichen — grows parasitically ON other lichens. Fascinating biology — one of few parasitic lichens.

lichens.desc: Black Sheet Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native black sheet lichen — large foliose lichen with distinctive netted upper surface. Bark dweller in humid forests.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Inelegant Strap Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native inelegant strap lichen — pendant lichen on coastal trees. Canary Islands origin. Fog-dependent.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Lecanora species — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — honey-colored rim lichen on bark. Spreading on urban trees.

lichens.desc: Yellow-edged Frost Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native yellow-edged frost lichen — gray foliose with yellow medulla visible at margins. Bark specialist.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Acarospora species — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — cobblestone lichen possibly spreading on urban substrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Gyalolechia species — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — stalked yellow crustose lichen. Rock and soil specialist.

lichens.desc: Coral Firedot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native coral firedot lichen — orange coral-like growth form. Rock specialist. Unusual branching morphology for Teloschistaceae.

lichens.desc: Frosted Grain-spored Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 3 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native frosted grain-spored lichen — black crustose lichen with frosted surface. Rock colonizer.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Bouquet Sea-Fog Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native bouquet sea-fog lichen — branching fruticose fog lichen. Coastal specialist. Fog-dependent.

lichens.desc: Sulphured Crimson Dot Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native sulphured crimson dot lichen — tiny red-orange fruiting bodies on bark. Oak specialist.

lichens.desc: Cowpie Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native cowpie lichen — crustose lichen growing over mosses and soil. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Bare-bottomed Sunburst Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native bare-bottomed sunburst lichen — orange foliose lichen lacking rhizines on lower surface (hence 'bare-bottomed'). Bark specialist.

lichens.desc: Rock Disk Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native rock disk lichen — dark crustose on rock and concrete. Common but overlooked. Urban colonizer.

lichens.desc: Granite-speck Rim Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native granite-speck rim lichen — yellow-green crustose on granitic rock. Important pioneer on bare rock.

lichens.desc: Brown Tile Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native brown tile lichen — dark brown crustose on exposed rocks. Alpine and subalpine specialist.

lichens.desc: Western Pixie-cup — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native western pixie-cup — goblet-shaped podetia. Related to British Soldier lichen. Forest floor specialist.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Firm Cup Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native firm cup lichen — robust pixie-cup. Soil and rotting wood colonizer.

lichens.desc: Lecidea species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native — dark crustose lichen on rock. Rock surface specialist.

lichens.desc: Frosted Rosette Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native frosted rosette lichen — gray foliose with frosted tips. Bark specialist. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Thelomma species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — minute dark lichen on rock and wood. Named for Swedish lichenologist Santesson.

lichens.desc: Elegant Camouflage Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native elegant camouflage lichen — brown foliose with isidia. Bark specialist in mountain forests.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Aspicilia species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — dark crustose lichen on rock. Rock surface colonizer.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Leprocaulon species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — described from Santa Monica Mountains. Leprose crustose on soil and rock. Taxonomically interesting.

lichens.desc: Non-native. California Camouflage Lichen — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native California camouflage lichen — brown foliose on bark. Despite the name, establishment status uncertain.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Polycauliona species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — orange firedot lichen. Rock specialist. Spreading on urban substrates.

lichens.desc: Letharia species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Native — related to wolf lichen but with different chemistry. Mountain conifer specialist. Contains vulpinic acid.

lichens.desc: Non-native. Niebla species — a lichen found in LA County with 2 research-grade iNaturalist observations.
lichens.hp: Non-native — isidiate fog lichen. Coastal specialist dependent on marine fog.
